titles.abstract: Locke: A Very Short Introduction unravels the meaning of John Locke's political thought and his contribution to English ideology. One of the greatest English philosophers of the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries, he argued in his masterpiece, An Essay Concerning Human Understanding, that our knowledge is founded in experience and reaches us principally through our senses; but its message has been curiously misunderstood. This VSI shows how Locke arrived at his theory of knowledge, and how his exposition of the liberal values of toleration and responsible government formed the backbone of enlightened European thought of the eighteenth century.
titles.summaryAI: John Locke's philosophy spanning epistemology, political theory, and religious toleration in late 17th and early 18th-century England. Central work is An Essay Concerning Human Understanding, arguing that knowledge is founded in sensory experience rather than innate ideas. Covers Locke's empiricist theory of knowledge, his defense of liberal values including toleration and responsible government, and his influence on Enlightenment thought across Europe. Distinguished from continental rationalism of Descartes and Leibniz by its empiricist foundations. Connects to political liberalism, social contract theory, the philosophy of mind, religious freedom, and the intellectual origins of constitutional democracy.
highlights: 'lucid and lively ... offers a rich insight into the triumphs and tragedy of the source of English ideology' New Society
Written by the leading authority on Locke
Insightful and lively, unravels the meaning of Locke's political thought

titles.title: Modern Ireland
titles.author: Senia Paseta
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Modern Ireland: A Very Short Introduction attempts to answer the Irish Question. The term has become something of a catch-all, a convenient way to encompass numerous issues and developments which pertain to the political, social, and economic history of modern Ireland. No other issue brought down as many nineteenth-century governments and no comparable twentieth-century dilemma has matched its ability to frustrate the attempts of British cabinets to find a solution; this inability to find a lasting answer to the Irish Question is especially striking when seen in the context of the massive shifts in British foreign policy brought about by two world wars, decolonization, and the cold war.
titles.summaryAI: Modern Ireland's political, social, and economic history from the Act of Union through the 19th and 20th centuries. Covers the Irish Republican Brotherhood, Charles Stewart Parnell, Home Rule, the Orange Order, land tenure reform, Eamon De Valera, and Northern Ireland's partition. Examines the Church of Ireland's role and House of Commons debates on Irish governance. Addresses how the Irish Question shaped British politics through world wars, decolonization, and the Cold War. Distinguished from ancient Irish history by focus on post-Union political struggles. Connects to imperial history, nationalism, and constitutional politics.
highlights: A brief overview of the central themes in Irish history, politics, and culture over the last 200 years
Challenging new angles and under-researched areas are broached, such as women's history and the lives of the Catholic middle class
Senia Paseta comes to the subject as an historian with a refreshingly impartial (and sometimes acerbic) perspective on Irish history
Ideal introduction for newcomer to subject, but original treatment makes it also essential reading for those already familiar with aspects of Irish history

titles.title: Globalization
titles.author: Manfred Steger
titles.subject: Economics/Politics
titles.abstract: Globalization: A Very Short Introduction looks at the interconnected and accelerated processes changing how we see and experience the world. Is globalization really a new phenomenon? Is increased connection between people and nations inevitable, or are we witnessing the beginning of an era of ‘deglobalization’ or ‘anti-globalization’? Updated with new developments including advancing climate change, the Trump presidency, and the Mexico–USA border, this VSI explores the history and impact of globalization. Chapters on the cultural, economic, political, and ecological dimensions of globalization investigate the impact of new technologies, economic deregulation, and mass migration on our world and consider what we might expect from the future of globalization.
titles.summaryAI: Globalization as interconnected economic, political, cultural, and ecological processes accelerating worldwide integration. Covers the history of global trade and migration, economic deregulation, new technologies, the Trump presidency, the Mexico-USA border, and advancing climate change. Examines anti-globalization and deglobalization movements alongside mass migration and its impacts. Addresses the cultural dimensions including identity and homogenization, the political implications for national sovereignty, and ecological consequences of global economic integration. Distinguished from internationalism by its emphasis on transnational flows beyond state control. Connects to trade policy, environmental politics, cultural exchange, and debates about the future of the nation-state.

titles.title: The Cold War
titles.author: Robert J. McMahon
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Cold War: A Very Short Introduction discusses the Cold War, which dominated international life from the end of the Second World War to the fall of the Berlin Wall. But how did the dispute begin, and why did it move from its origins in post-war Europe to encompass virtually every corner of the globe? This VSI considers these questions and more, providing a truly international history of the Cold War and examining its enduring legacy. It draws on the most recent scholarship and documents to offer a full analysis of all aspects of the war. These include the Vietnam War and the changing global politics since the 1970s.
titles.summaryAI: The Cold War as a global geopolitical conflict from the end of World War II (1945) to the fall of the Berlin Wall (1989). Covers the origins in post-war Europe, expansion to encompass virtually every global region, NATO's formation, the Vietnam War, and shifting global politics from the 1970s onward. Examines the superpower rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union, nuclear deterrence, proxy wars, and ideological competition between capitalism and communism. Distinguished from conventional wars by its avoidance of direct superpower military confrontation. Connects to decolonization, international institutions, arms control, and the post-Cold War international order.

titles.title: The History of Astronomy
titles.author: Michael Hoskin
titles.subject: History of Science/Physics
titles.abstract: The History of Astronomy: A Very Short Introduction traces the history of Western astronomy, from prehistoric times to the origins of astrophysics in the mid-nineteenth century and the technical developments since the Second World War. Astronomy, perhaps the first of the sciences, was already well developed by the time of Christ — the arithmetical astronomy of the Babylonians was merged with the Greek geometrical approach. This legacy was transmitted to the West via Islam and led to the Copernican revolution, which in turn led to Kepler and Newton, who provided the principles on which the exploration of the solar system and the stars continued in the eighteenth- and nineteenth centuries.
titles.summaryAI: The history of Western astronomy from prehistoric observations through Babylonian arithmetical methods, Greek geometrical approaches, and Islamic transmission to the Copernican revolution and beyond. Key figures include Aristotle, Ptolemy (Almagest), Tycho Brahe, Kepler, Isaac Newton, and William Herschel. Covers calendar development, the heliocentric model, gravitational theory, solar system exploration, and stellar astronomy of the 18th-19th centuries. Traces the emergence of astrophysics in the mid-19th century and post-WWII technical developments. Distinguished from cosmology by its observational and instrumental focus. Connects to the history of science, mathematics, navigation, and religious-scientific debates.
highlights: The only short introduction to this subject
Focuses on the major developments of the 17th, 18th, and 19th centuries: Copernicus, Kepler, Galileo, Newton and beyond
Looks at astronomy in antiquity: the Egyptians and the Greeks: Aristotle, Plato, Eudoxus, Hipparchus
Final chapter on the directions that the subject has taken since 1950

titles.title: Schizophrenia
titles.author: Chris Frith, Eve C. Johnstone
titles.subject: Psychology/Social Work
titles.abstract: Schizophrenia: A Very Short Introduction considers a common mental disorder that has a devastating effect on sufferers and their families. While patients typically hear voices in their heads and hold bizarre beliefs, the schizophrenic patient presented to the public in sensational press reports and lurid films bears little resemblance to the reality of the illness. This VSI describes what schizophrenia is really like, how the illness progresses, and the treatments that have been applied. It summarizes the most up-to-date knowledge about the biological bases of schizophrenia and attempts to convey what it is like to suffer from it and what this disorder reveals about the relationship between mind and brain.
titles.summaryAI: Schizophrenia as a major mental disorder characterized by hallucinations, delusions, and negative symptoms. Covers the biological basis of schizophrenia including brain imaging through MRI, neurotransmitter systems, and pharmacological treatments. Examines the disorder's progression, diagnostic criteria, and the subjective experience of sufferers. Addresses misconceptions from sensationalized media portrayals and distinguishes schizophrenia from conditions like Alzheimer's disease and multiple sclerosis. Includes behaviour therapy approaches alongside drug treatments. Reveals what schizophrenia discloses about the relationship between mind and brain. Connects to neuroscience, psychiatric classification, psychopharmacology, and the philosophy of mental illness.
highlights: Gives the facts about Schizophrenia - an illness much discussed but widely misrepresented in media, literature, and film
Explains the truth about one of the most fascinating and devastating mental illnesses: patients hear voices in their heads and hold bizarre beliefs
Accessible accounts of cutting edge research
Detailed descriptions of patients and their histories

titles.title: The Earth
titles.author: Martin Redfern
titles.subject: Earth Sciences and Geography/Environment
titles.abstract: The Earth: A Very Short Introduction provides an introduction to the processes that govern the changing geology of the Earth. About 30 years ago, two things happened that began to revolutionize the understanding of these processes. First, geologists realized that the continents themselves were drifting across the surface of the globe and that the oceans were being created and destroyed. Secondly, satellites in space returned images of the entire planet and Earth began to be viewed as a single, dynamic entity. This VSI looks at how those ideas have transformed our understanding of the geological processes that are at work on our planet.
titles.summaryAI: Earth science encompassing plate tectonics, geological processes, and planetary formation within the solar system. Covers continental drift (Alfred Wegener), convection currents (Arthur Holmes), chemical weathering, radioactive decay as a dating tool, and ocean systems including the Gulf Stream conveyor belt. Examines how satellite imaging transformed Earth into a single dynamic system and how plate tectonics revolutionized geology since the 1960s. Addresses solar wind interactions, Earth's magnetic field, and Charles Darwin's geological observations. Distinguished from planetary science by its focus on terrestrial processes. Connects to oceanography, atmospheric science, geophysics, and the history of geological discovery.
highlights: Lively and informative look at the frontiers of geological science
Comprehensive and accessible introduction to the processes which govern the changing geology of the Earth
Leads the reader to understand the relationships between the various processes that make the Earth one dynamic, living planet

titles.title: Engels
titles.author: Terrell Carver
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Engels: A Very Short Introduction examines the man who invented Marxism. Engels' work did more than Marx to make converts to the most influential political movement of modern times. He was not only the father of dialectical and historical materialism (the official philosophies of history and science in many communist countries), he was also the first Marxist historian, anthropologist, philosopher, and commentator on early Marx. In his later years, Engels developed his materialist interpretation of history, his chief intellectual legacy. This VSI traces its source and its effect on the development of Marxist theory and practice, assesses its utility, and discusses the difficulties which Marxists have encountered in defending it.
titles.summaryAI: Friedrich Engels's intellectual contributions to Marxism, including the development of dialectical materialism, historical materialism, and political economy. Covers Engels's relationship with Karl Marx, his interpretation of Ludwig Feuerbach, and his role as the first Marxist historian, anthropologist, and philosopher. Examines Engels's analysis of class dynamics between the working class and middle class, his materialist interpretation of history as his chief intellectual legacy, and the adoption of his ideas as official philosophy in Soviet Union and other communist states. Distinguished from Marx's own writings by Engels's systematizing and popularizing role. Connects to socialist theory, philosophy of history, and 20th-century communist ideology.
highlights: The only short and accessible introduction to Engels
A lucid study of Engels's thought and its implications for contemporary social science and politics
Lively exploration of Engels's development of Maxist theory and practice

titles.title: British Politics
titles.author: Tony Wright
titles.subject: Politics – U.K.
titles.abstract: British Politics: A Very Short Introduction explores the history of British politics, looking at whether the present instability is an aberration, the result of long-standing fault lines, or both. Current events are placed within a longer, larger perspective, focusing on Britain’s constitution, its polarized political culture of debates and disagreements, the importance of party politics, and the meaning of representative democracy now. Following the financial crisis, a peacetime coalition government, and the fallout from the 2016 referendum, Britain’s political future is uncertain. However, even after the momentous changes leading up to and including the 2019 general election, it remains uncertain whether the character of British politics will fundamentally change.
titles.summaryAI: British politics as a constitutional and institutional system, covering parliamentary democracy, party politics, and political culture from historical foundations to the post-2019 era. Examines the 2016 Brexit referendum, the 2019 general election, the 2010 coalition government, and the aftermath of the financial crisis. Covers Britain's unwritten constitution, the polarized culture of political debate, representative democracy, and questions about fundamental political change. Distinguished from comparative politics by its focus on specifically British institutions and conventions. Connects to constitutional law, European integration, electoral systems, and debates about democratic legitimacy and political polarization.

titles.title: Linguistics
titles.author: P. H. Matthews
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Linguistics: A Very Short Introduction considers a subject that falls in the gap between arts and science, on the edges of which fascinating discoveries and important problems are found. Beginning with the oldest, ‘arts’ end of the subject, it moves chronologically through to the newest research — the ‘science’ aspects. Themes included are: the prehistory of languages and their common origins; language and evolution; language in time and space (the nature of change inherent in language) grammars and dictionaries (how systematic is language?) and phonetics. Explication of the newest discoveries relating to language in the brain completes the coverage of major aspects of linguistics.
titles.summaryAI: Linguistics as the scientific study of language, spanning historical linguistics, phonetics, syntax, semantics, and neurolinguistics. Covers the prehistory and common origins of languages, language evolution, language change over time and across geographic space, and the relationship between speech and writing. Examines grammar and dictionaries as systematizations of language, with examples from French, German, and Spanish. Includes discoveries about language processing in the brain. Distinguished from literary studies by its empirical and structural approach to language. Connects to cognitive science, anthropology, evolutionary biology, and the philosophy of language.
highlights: Provides a broad overview of the field, and guides the reader through specific examples
Conveys complex linguistic topics in a clear, unbiased, down-to-earth way
Takes an original approach to the presentation of linguistics, starting with the oldest 'arts' branch of the subject and moving chronologically through to the newer 'science' aspects of research
Uses short thematic chapters to break the subject down into its various fields, such as comparing languages, language and evolution, language in time and space, etc.
Written by one of the most distinguished linguists in the world today

titles.title: The Celts
titles.author: Barry Cunliffe
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Celts: A Very Short Introduction seeks to reveal the true nature of the Celts. Were they savage and bloodthirsty, or civilized and peaceable? The Celts have long been a subject of enormous fascination, speculation, and misunderstanding. From the ancient Romans to the present day, their real character has been obscured by a tangled web of preconceived ideas and stereotypes. This VSI aims to show what the Celts were really like, exploring subjects such as trade, migration, and the evolution of Celtic traditions. Along the way, it exposes how society's needs have shaped our visions of the Celts, and examines such colourful characters as St Patrick, Cú Chulainn, and Boudica.
titles.summaryAI: The Celts as an archaeological and linguistic cultural group spanning from the Iron Age through the Roman period to modern Celtic identities across Britain, Ireland, and continental Europe. Covers trade networks, migration, material culture including pottery and weapons, and the evolution of Celtic traditions. Examines Julius Caesar's accounts, Boudica, Cu Chulainn, and St Patrick. Addresses how stereotypes have shaped interpretations of Celtic culture from Roman ethnography to modern nationalism. Distinguished from Germanic and Roman cultures by language family and artistic style. Connects to archaeology, mythology, and ethnic identity.
highlights: A thought-provoking and entertaining new look at the Celts
Author is an acknowledged expert in this field
Uses up-to-the-minute evidence
'One of the most committed and gifted archaeological communicators, whose works are always a pleasure to read.' Dr Simon James, University of Leicester

titles.title: Ideology
titles.author: Michael Freeden
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Ideology: A Very Brief Introduction is an examination of the major theories of ideology and the ways they have enriched our comprehension of ideology. Ideology is one of the most controversial terms in the political vocabulary. It creates both revulsion and inspiration. This VSI examines the reasons for this reaction and explains why ideologies deserve respect as a major form of political thinking. It explores the changing understandings of ideology as a concept and the arguments of the main ideologies. It draws on a range of disciplines in order to show the potency of ideology as a resource at the disposal of societies.
titles.summaryAI: Ideology as a concept in political thought, covering liberalism, Marxism (Marx, Engels), fascism (Hitler), and other major political ideologies. Examines theories of ideology from Karl Mannheim's sociology of knowledge to contemporary analysis, including the concept of false consciousness. Addresses how ideology functions through language, political parties, and institutional structures. Explores why ideology generates both revulsion and inspiration as a form of political thinking. Distinguished from political philosophy by its focus on how ideas function in political practice rather than abstract normative theory. Connects to sociology of knowledge, political party systems, propaganda, and the relationship between ideas and political power.

titles.title: Prehistory
titles.author: Chris Gosden
titles.subject: History/Archaeology/Art
titles.abstract: Prehistory covers the period of some four million years before the start of written history, when our earliest ancestors, the Australopithecines, existed in Africa. Prehistory: A Very Short Introduction invites us to think about who we are by considering who we have been. There have been many archaeological discoveries over the last ten years, with a new framework for prehistory emerging. Greater understanding of Chinese and central Asian prehistory shows Eurasian prehistory in a different light, changing the traditional view of human progress around the invention of agriculture and development of cities. This VSI explores the new landscape of our prehistory, considering the way the different geographical locations weave together.
titles.summaryAI: Prehistory spanning approximately four million years from the earliest Australopithecines in Africa to the beginning of written records. Covers the Holocene epoch, the invention of agriculture, the development of cities, and the Clovis Culture in the Americas. Examines new archaeological frameworks incorporating Chinese and central Asian evidence that reframe traditional Eurasian narratives. Addresses Darwin's influence on evolutionary thinking about human origins and the different geographical trajectories of prehistoric development. Distinguished from ancient history by the absence of written records and reliance on material evidence. Connects to archaeology, physical anthropology, agricultural origins, and the comparative study of early human societies.
highlights: Looks at the story of human prehistory in different geographical locations, and the way it weaves together
Uses the latest evidence to chart how our picture of world prehistory has changed over recent years, showing why the old picture of progress is less accepted as new geographies emerge
Considers new theories on the ebb and flow of human cultural variety, and the joining together of cultural groups into larger blocks reflecting new forms of power
Covers global prehistory, including African and Eurasian evidence, and Australian, American, and Pacific prehistory
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over nine million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Political philosophy
titles.author: David Miller
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Political Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction introduces the concepts of political philosophy — authority, democracy, freedom and its limits, justice, feminism, multiculturalism, and nationality. It asks such questions as: Is it really true that what governments do profoundly affects the quality of our lives? Do states need to be able to coerce their subjects if they are to have political authority? What role should ordinary citizens play in a democracy? It looks at the reasons why we need politics at all, the limitations of politics, and whether there are areas of life that shouldn't be governed by politics.
titles.summaryAI: Political philosophy as a discipline examining authority, democracy, freedom, justice, human rights, feminism, multiculturalism, and nationality. Covers social contract theory from Hobbes and Locke, civil disobedience, the state of nature, and the moral foundations of political obligation. Addresses why political institutions are necessary, the limits of legitimate political authority, and areas of life that should remain free from political governance. Distinguished from political science by its normative rather than empirical approach. Connects to ethics, constitutional theory, international law, theories of distributive justice, and debates about the proper scope and limits of government power.
highlights: A lucid and non-technical introduction to the key issues in political philosophy
Asks questions such as 'could we live together in societies without politics?' and 'where should the limits of politics be set?'
Will appeal to readers curious about politics in general
Tackles fundamental questions about how we should judge political systems as being good or bad

titles.title: Postcolonialism
titles.author: Robert J.C. Young
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Postcolonialism: A Very Short Introduction describes how people around the world have increasingly challenged the idea that Western perspectives are the only ones that count. It examines the history of that challenge, outlining the ideas behind it, and exploring how the histories and cultures of the world can be rethought in new, productive directions. This VSI situates the discussion in wide cultural and geographical contexts. It draws on examples such as the situation of indigenous peoples, of those dispossessed from their lands, Algerian raï music, and global social and ecological movements. This new edition also includes updated material on race, slavery, decoloniality, and the postcolonial politics of gender and sexualities.
titles.summaryAI: Postcolonialism as an intellectual and political movement challenging Western-centric perspectives on history, culture, and knowledge. Covers the experiences of indigenous peoples, dispossessed populations, and colonized societies across Africa, Asia, and the Americas. Examines decoloniality, race, slavery, and gender politics within postcolonial frameworks, alongside cultural expressions such as Algerian rai music and global social movements. Addresses environmental and ecological dimensions of colonial legacies. Distinguished from postmodernism by its grounding in material histories of colonial exploitation. Connects to literary criticism, cultural studies, global history, racial justice, and the politics of knowledge production.

titles.title: Atheism
titles.author: Julian Baggini
titles.subject: Religion/Philosophy
titles.abstract: Atheism: A Very Short Introduction discusses the case for atheism. Atheism is often seen as simply a rejection of theism, but it encompasses so much more. Atheists are typically naturalists, who believe that meaning and morality are possible in a finite, natural world. ‘New Atheism’, a powerful new movement in atheism in the early 21st century, driven by books from authors such as Richard Dawkins and Sam Harris, has left a legacy. There is an important question to consider: whether East Asia has been historically atheist or not. Atheism can be located in recent European history. What is the position of atheists around the world today?
titles.summaryAI: Atheism as a philosophical position and cultural phenomenon encompassing naturalism, secular ethics, and the rejection of theistic belief. Covers the New Atheism movement of the early 21st century driven by Richard Dawkins and Sam Harris, the historical roots of atheism in European Enlightenment thought, and the question of whether East Asian traditions constitute historical atheism. Examines how atheists construct meaning and morality within a finite, natural world without supernatural foundations. Distinguished from agnosticism by its affirmative denial of deities. Connects to philosophy of religion, secularism, comparative religion, the relationship between science and faith, and global religious demographics.

titles.title: Evolution
titles.author: Brian Charlesworth, Deborah Charlesworth
titles.subject: Biology/Anthropology
titles.abstract: Evolution: A Very Short Introduction explains the crucial role of evolutionary biology in transforming our view of human origins and relation to the universe, and the impact of this idea on traditional philosophy and religion. It introduces some of the most important findings, concepts, and procedures of evolutionary biology, as it has developed since the publications of Darwin and Wallace on the subject, over 140 years ago. Evolution provides a unifying set of principles for the whole of biology. In addition, many aspects of evolution have practical importance; for instance, the rapid evolutionary resistance by bacteria to antibiotics and of HIV to antiviral drugs are pressing medical problems.
titles.summaryAI: Evolution as a unifying biological framework explaining the diversity and adaptation of life through natural selection, genetic drift, and other mechanisms. Covers Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace's foundational work, artificial selection, speciation, kin selection, and molecular evolution including protein and bacterial genetics. Examines practical implications including antibiotic resistance in bacteria and HIV antiviral resistance. Addresses the relationship between evolutionary theory and traditional philosophy and religion, including human origins. Uses Darwin's finches as a case study. Distinguished from creationism and intelligent design. Connects to genetics, medicine, ecology, anthropology, and the philosophy of science.
highlights: Introduces in a clear and concise way the processes of evolution by natural selection, and how this leads to complex adaptations and new species
Looks at the wide range of evidence for evolution
Considers some of the remaining questions in evolutionary theory
Explains adaptation and natural selection
This reissue includes some key updates to the main text and a completely updated Further Reading section.
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over eight million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Molecules
titles.author: Philip Ball
titles.subject: Biology/Chemistry
titles.abstract: Molecules: A Very Short Introduction investigates the nature of molecules and how molecular structures and activities underlie the properties of materials and the processes of life. A living cell is like a city teeming with molecular inhabitants that move, communicate, cooperate, and compete. This VSI considers how a single fertilized egg can grow into a multi–celled Mozart, what makes spider's silk insoluble in the morning dew, and how our understanding and synthesis of molecules has led to exciting new areas of research into the development of molecular machines and powerful yet tiny molecular computers.
titles.summaryAI: Molecular science spanning biochemistry, organic chemistry, materials science, and nanotechnology. Covers chemical bonding (covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds), the alpha helix and other protein structures, cellular metabolism, and molecular recognition. Examines how molecular structures underlie both the properties of materials (such as spider silk) and biological processes from fertilization to organismal development. Addresses emerging research in molecular machines and molecular computing. Distinguished from atomic physics by its focus on multi-atom structures and interactions. Connects to cell biology, genetics, pharmacology, polymer science, and the development of nanoscale technologies.
highlights: Popular science writing at its very best
Takes newcomers to the subject all the way up to current research in new areas of chemistry
A non-traditional approach to chemistry, focusing on what chemistry might become during this century, rather than a survey of its past
Concentrates on molecules in living systems, and on how synthetic chemistry often takes its inspiration from organic molecules

titles.title: Art history
titles.author: Dana Arnold
titles.subject: Art/History
titles.abstract: Art history encompasses the study of the history and development of painting, sculpture, and the other visual arts. Art History: A Very Short Introduction considers the issues, debates, and artefacts that make up art history. It explores the emergence of social histories of art and, using a wide range of images, it discusses key aspects of the discipline including how we write about, present, read, and look at art, and the impact this has on our understanding of art history. This second edition includes a new chapter on global art histories, considering how the traditional emphasis on periods and styles in art originated in Western art and can obscure other critical approaches and artwork from non-Western cultures.
titles.summaryAI: Art history as a discipline studying the development of painting, sculpture, and visual arts from ancient times (Apollo Belvedere) through the International Gothic and Renaissance (Gentile da Fabriano, Ghiberti, Cennini) to Impressionism (Monet) and contemporary art (Damien Hirst). Covers methods of writing about, presenting, and interpreting art, including the camera obscura's influence on representation. Examines the emergence of social histories of art and global art histories that challenge Western-centric emphasis on period styles. Distinguished from art theory by its historical and contextual approach. Connects to cultural history, museum studies, visual culture, and debates about canon formation and non-Western artistic traditions.
highlights: Introduces the study of art history considering the artefacts and debates which it covers
Discusses how we write, present, read, and look at art
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide
newToThisEdition: This new edition includes a new chapter on global art histories, expanding the discussion of art from non-western cultures
Includes new material on the relationship between art and history, and the ways in which art can tell a different history from the one narrated by texts

titles.title: Presocratic Philosophy
titles.author: Catherine Rowett
titles.subject: Philosophy/Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Presocratic Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction introduces the first thinkers to explore ideas about the nature of reality, time, and the origin of the universe. It begins by discussing a new papyrus fragment of Empedocles, and uses the story of its discovery and interpretation to highlight the way our understanding of early philosophers is marked by their presentation in later sources. Generations of philosophers have traced their inspiration back to the Presocratics. The fragmentary remains of thinkers from Thales to Pythagoras, and from Heraclitus to Protagoras, are explored to try to fill in the bits of a jigsaw that has been rejigged many times and in many different ways.
titles.summaryAI: Presocratic philosophy encompassing the earliest Greek thinkers from Thales to Protagoras (roughly 6th-5th centuries BC), who explored the nature of reality, time, and the origin of the universe. Key figures include Parmenides, Heraclitus, Democritus, Anaxagoras, Empedocles, and Pythagoras. Covers first principles, the relationship between reason and perception, early atomic theory, and proto-scientific inquiry. Examines how fragmentary sources and later reinterpretation (including Nietzsche's) shape understanding of these thinkers. Distinguished from classical Athenian philosophy (Plato, Aristotle) by its cosmological focus. Connects to the history of science, metaphysics, epistemology, and the foundations of Western philosophical inquiry.
highlights: A colourful and authoritative introduction to presocratic philosophy
Uses as a starting point, the recent findings of the ancient papyrus fragment carrying a poem by the Greek magician and philosopher Empedocles
Discusses early Greek philosophy thematically rather than chronologically
There is no other book on the subject at this level and price

titles.title: The Elements
titles.author: Philip Ball
titles.subject: Chemistry/History of Science
titles.abstract: The Elements: A Very Short Introduction traces the history and cultural impact of the elements on humankind, and examines why people have long sought to identify the substances around them. Looking beyond the Periodic Table, our relationship with matter is examined, from the uncomplicated vision of the Greek philosophers who believed there were four elements — earth, air, fire, and water — to the work of modern–day scientists in creating elements such as hassium and meitnerium. This VSI is an exploration of the fundamental question: what is the world made from?
titles.summaryAI: The chemical elements traced from the ancient Greek four-element theory (earth, air, fire, water) through Lavoisier's modern chemical nomenclature to contemporary synthesis of transuranic elements like hassium and meitnerium. Covers atomic structure, atomic weight, isotopes, noble gases, quantum mechanics, and the periodic table as an organizing framework. Examines the Big Bang as the origin of elements, heavy water and nuclear science, and the cultural significance of elements like gold and oxygen. Distinguished from molecular chemistry by its focus on fundamental atomic species. Connects to nuclear physics, cosmology, materials science, the history of chemistry, and quantum theory.
highlights: 'A delight of a book.... Elegantly written...it's far-reaching, entertaining and salted with anecdote.... It could become a classic. Hold on to your first edition.' Roy Herbert, New Scientist.

An engaging chronology of the elements, from the Greek philosophers who thought the world was made only from earth, air, fire, and water, to the work of twentieth-century radiochemistry in extending the Periodic Table.
An exciting and non-traditional approach to understanding the terminology, properties, and classification of chemical elements.
Includes chapters on particular elements (gold, iron, oxygen, etc), showing how they shaped culture and technology.
Philip Ball is a high-profile science writer, who writes science extremely well for non-scientists.

titles.title: Dada and Surrealism
titles.author: David Hopkins
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Dada and Surrealism: A Very Short Introduction focuses on the many debates surrounding these movements: the Marquis de Sade's Surrealist deification, issues of quality, the idea of the ‘readymade’, attitudes towards the city, the impact of Freud, attitudes to women, and primitivism. Where does the Dadaist obsession with the body-as-mechanism come from? Why are the Surrealists obsessed with the fetishized/eroticized body? Dada and Surrealism continue to have a huge influence on cultural practice, especially in contemporary art, with its obsession with sexuality, fetishism, and shock tactics. A huge range of media is employed by both movements (collage, painting, found objects, performance art, photography, film), but there are aesthetic differences between them.
titles.summaryAI: Dada and Surrealism as avant-garde art movements from the 1910s through the mid-20th century influencing contemporary practice. Key figures include De Chirico, Magritte, Miro, Gorky, and the Young British Artists. Examines the readymade concept, Freudian psychoanalysis in art, the eroticized body, primitivism, and Sade's role in Surrealist thought. Covers collage, painting, found objects, performance art, photography, and film. Distinguished from each other by Dada's nihilistic anti-art stance versus Surrealism's exploration of the unconscious. Connects to psychoanalysis, modernism, sexuality, and shock in contemporary art.
highlights: A very popular subject, not least because of its influence on contemporary art
Discusses work by well-known artists such as Magritte, Dali, Duchamp, and Man Ray
Explores Dada and Surrealism's obsession with sexuality, issues of identity, fetishism, and shock tactics
Examines the many debates surrounding these movements, including the Marquis de Sade's position as a Surrealist deity, attitudes towards the city, the impact of Freud, and attitudes to women
Covers Zurich, New York, Berlin, Cologne, Barcelona, Paris, London, and recently discovered examples in Eastern Europe

titles.title: Egyptian Myth
titles.author: Geraldine Pinch
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Egyptian Myth: A Very Short Introduction explores the cultural and historical background behind a wide variety of sources and objects on Egyptian mythology, from Cleopatra's Needle and Tutankhamun's golden statue, to a story on papyrus of the gods misbehaving. The world of Egyptian myth is complex. Mythology was an integral part of Egyptian culture for much of its timespan. What did the myths mean, and how have they been interpreted? How have the myths of deities such as Isis and Osiris influenced contemporary culture and become part of our cultural heritage?
titles.summaryAI: Egyptian mythology as an integral component of ancient Egyptian religion and culture spanning thousands of years. Covers major deities including Osiris, Horus, and Seth, with key texts such as the Coffin Texts and the Contendings of Horus and Seth. Examines mythological narratives found on papyri, temples, and artifacts from sites including Deir El-Medina, connected to pharaohs Amenhotep III and Ramesses II. Addresses the cultural significance of deities like Isis in contemporary reception. Distinguished from Greek and Roman mythology by its integration with ritual practice and royal ideology. Connects to Egyptology, comparative religion, ancient literature, and cultural heritage studies.
highlights: Written by an expert but with the general reader in mind
Thematically arranged
Emphasizes diversity of source material
Simply and concisely explains and illuminates a fascinating and complex world

titles.title: Christian art
titles.author: Beth Williamson
titles.subject: Religion/Art
titles.abstract: Christian Art: A Very Short Introduction explores the work of major artists such as Memling, Holbein, El Greco, and Rossetti, and well-known examples including the frescoes of St Francis at Assisi. How do didactic and consciously devotional works compare with the controversial work of contemporary artists such as Andres Serrano and Chris Ofili? Christian images have a long history within the Western art tradition, from the narrative and devotional works of the Medieval and Renaissance periods, to the radical new interpretations of the twenty-first century. Over the centuries the nature of the representation of key themes and subjects found in Christian art such as the Eucharist, the crucifixion, the Virgin Mary, and the saints has changed considerably.
titles.summaryAI: Christian art spanning from early medieval narrative and devotional works through the Renaissance to radical contemporary interpretations. Covers major artists including Memling, Holbein, El Greco, and Rossetti, alongside controversial contemporary figures like Andres Serrano and Chris Ofili. Examines key themes including the Eucharist, crucifixion, Virgin Mary, saints, the Fall, St Francis of Assisi frescoes, and the Virgin of Guadalupe. Addresses the relationship between Christian imagery and pagan and Jewish visual traditions. Distinguished from secular Western art by its liturgical and devotional functions. Connects to theology, iconography, the history of Western visual culture, and debates about sacred imagery and blasphemy.
highlights: The only concise, general introduction to Christian Art available - providing the tools needed to decode the signs and symbols used in Christian art and architecture
Features such well-known artists as Michelangelo, Rosetti, and Chris Ofili, as well as famous buildings, such as the Sistine Chapel, and Westminster Abbey
A broad-ranging historical account - from the Medieval period to the present day
Examines the development of the cult of the Virgin Mary
Explores the ongoing fascination with the blood of Christ, and the image of the crucifixion
Discusses the role of art in campaings for canonization, and the cult of relics

titles.title: Capitalism
titles.author: James Fulcher
titles.subject: Politics/Economics
titles.abstract: The word ‘capitalism’ is heard and used frequently, but what is capitalism about, and what does it mean? Capitalism: A Very Short Introduction discusses the history and development of capitalism through several detailed case studies, ranging from the ‘tulipomania’ of 17th-century Holland, the Great Depression of the 1930s, and the impact of the global financial crisis that started in 2007–8. It looks at the different forms that capitalism takes in Britain, Japan, Sweden, and the United States, and explores whether capitalism has escaped the nation-state by going global. It asks whether there is an alternative to capitalism, discussing socialism, communal and cooperative experiments, and the alternatives proposed by environmentalists.
titles.summaryAI: Capitalism as an economic system traced from 17th-century Dutch tulipomania through industrialization to the 2007-8 global financial crisis. Covers market forces, capital accumulation, production, trade, ownership structures, and financial systems including Wall Street. Examines comparative capitalisms in Britain, Japan, Sweden, and the United States. Addresses the Great Depression of the 1930s, economic deregulation, and globalization of capital beyond nation-states. Considers alternatives including socialism, communal and cooperative experiments, and environmentalist proposals. Distinguished from feudalism by its reliance on market exchange and wage labor. Connects to political economy, economic history, industrial development, and debates about economic inequality.
highlights: Provides a detailed answer to the question 'what is capitalism?'
Brings together sociological and historical approaches to the study of capitalism, drawing on material from a wide range of historical and contemporary societies
Looks at the origins and developments of capitalism and the various forms it takes worldwide
Analyses historical cases of crisis within capitalism
Discusses the global financial crisis of 2007-8 and its on-going effects
Highlights the problems of financial capitalism by identifying two crisis mechanisms - overproduction/underconsumption and financial crisis of credit expansion and contraction
Explores the deflationary consequences of quantitative easing (QE), the contraction of credit, and the role of China in the global economy
Includes an updated further reading section

titles.title: Particle physics
titles.author: Frank Close
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Particle Physics: A Very Short Introduction takes a journey into the atom and presents an overview of the fundamental particles and forces that make up the universe. The VSI discusses the features and functions of particles such as quarks, electrons, and neutrinos, and of gluons and bosons. It includes the key role and significance of the Higgs boson in moulding these foundational elements and giving structure to matter. It highlights how discoveries in particle physics were made and have radically changed understanding of the material universe. The book concludes by considering the implications of the discovery of the Higgs boson, and asks whether massive neutrinos might be key to resolving the mystery surrounding the absence of antimatter.
titles.summaryAI: Particle physics examining the fundamental constituents of matter and forces of the universe. Covers quarks, electrons, neutrinos, gluons, bosons, and the Higgs boson's role in giving mass to particles. Examines atomic structure, antimatter-matter asymmetry, and whether massive neutrinos explain the absence of antimatter. Traces experimental discoveries that transformed understanding of the material universe. Distinguished from nuclear physics by its focus on subatomic constituents rather than atomic nuclei. Connects to cosmology, quantum field theory, high-energy experimental methods, and fundamental questions about matter.

titles.title: Free will
titles.author: Thomas Pink
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Free Will: A Very Short Introduction asks: Are our choices really free? Every day we seem to make and act upon all kinds of free choices. Are these choices ours, or are we compelled to act the way we do by factors beyond our control? Is the feeling that we could have made different decisions just an illusion? And if our choices are not free, why should we be held morally responsible for our actions? This VSI looks at a range of issues surrounding this fundamental philosophical question, exploring it from the ideas of the Greek and medieval philosophers through to the thoughts of present-day thinkers.
titles.summaryAI: Free will as a central problem in philosophy spanning from ancient Greek thought through medieval theology to modern debates. Covers determinism, compatibilism, and libertarian free will, with key thinkers including Hobbes, Kant, and contemporary philosophers. Examines the relationship between free will and moral responsibility, the role of randomness versus self-determination in human action, and skeptical challenges to the experience of genuine choice. Addresses whether the subjective sense of choosing freely is illusory. Distinguished from political freedom by its focus on metaphysical and psychological dimensions. Connects to ethics, philosophy of mind, neuroscience of decision-making, and theories of moral and legal responsibility.

titles.title: Myth
titles.author: Robert Segal
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Where do myths come from? What is their function and what do they mean? Myth: A Very Short Introduction introduces a wide array of approaches to understanding myth from disciplines as varied as anthropology, sociology, psychology, literary criticism, philosophy, science, and religious studies. It uses the famous ancient myth of Adonis to analyse the ideas and individual approaches and theories of theorists such as Sigmund Freud, Claude Lévi-Strauss, Albert Camus, and Roland Barthes. This new edition considers the interactions of myth theory with cognitive science, the implications of the myth of Gaia, and the differences between story-telling and myth, as well as the future study of myth.
titles.summaryAI: Myth as a subject studied across anthropology, sociology, psychology, literary criticism, philosophy, science, and religious studies. Covers theoretical approaches from Freud's psychoanalytic interpretation to Levi-Strauss's structuralism, Camus's existentialism, Barthes's semiotics, and Propp's narrative morphology. Uses the ancient Adonis myth as a central analytical case. Examines the distinction between myth, legend, and story-telling, the relationship between myth and cognitive science, and the Gaia hypothesis as modern myth. Distinguished from folklore by its cosmological and explanatory functions. Connects to comparative religion, narrative theory, cultural anthropology, and the intersection of scientific and mythological worldviews.
highlights: Looks at the origins of the study of myths, and how they have been applied to the arts and sciences
Reveals how a variety of theories can be applied to ancient myths, but also to modern mythology
Explores the function of myths, and their subject matter
Considers the work of prominent thinkers such as Albert Camus, Claude Lévi-Strauss, Roland Barthes, C. G. Jung, and Sigmund Freud
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide
newToThisEdition: Considers myth as modern science, including 20th and 21st century attempts to find evidence of astronomical observations, of volcanoes, and of other natural disasters
Looks at myth as a story, by considering the latest approaches to narrative theory
Examines myth and its interaction with society, including nationalism and fascism
Explores the approaches of cognitive science to myth

titles.title: Ancient Egypt
titles.author: Ian Shaw
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Ancient Egypt: A Very Short Introduction explores the history and culture of pharaonic Egypt, including ideas about Egyptian kingship, religion, ethics, customs, and writing systems. The ancient Egyptians are an enduring source of fascination and mystery; for generations, mummies and pyramids, curses and rituals have captured the imagination. This VSI draws on the latest archaeological discoveries and scholarship on ancient Egypt. It considers issues relating to the history of Egyptology, ethnicity, race, gender, and sexual relations. Moreover, it examines the impact of the Arab Spring on approaches to Egyptian museums and cultural heritage all over the world.
titles.summaryAI: Ancient Egypt spanning pharaonic civilization from the earliest dynasties through the New Kingdom and beyond. Covers Egyptian kingship, religion, ethics, customs, and writing systems, alongside the history of Egyptology as a discipline. Examines issues of ethnicity, race, gender, and sexual relations in ancient Egyptian society. Addresses the impact of the Arab Spring on approaches to Egyptian museums and cultural heritage worldwide. Distinguished from other ancient Near Eastern civilizations by its distinctive monumental architecture, hieroglyphic script, and religious practices. Connects to archaeology, museum studies, postcolonial approaches to heritage, and the comparative study of ancient civilizations and their modern political contexts.

titles.title: Hieroglyphs
titles.author: Penelope Wilson
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Hieroglyphs: A Very Short Introduction explores the cultural significance of this script with an emphasis on previously neglected areas such as cryptography and the continuing decipherment into modern times. It examines the powerful fascination hieroglyphs still hold for us today. The people of Ancient Egypt left behind many monuments and objects, covered in the writing now known as Egyptian hieroglyphs. Hieroglyphs were far more than a language. They were an omnipresent and all-powerful force in communicating the messages of ancient Egyptian culture for over three thousand years; used as monumental art, as a means of identifying Egyptianness, and for rarefied communication with the gods.
titles.summaryAI: Egyptian hieroglyphs as a writing system spanning over three thousand years from the Early Dynastic Period through the Old, Middle, and New Kingdoms. Covers the Rosetta Stone and modern decipherment, the Pyramid Texts, Coffin Texts, and their significance as monumental art and ritual communication. Examines hieroglyphic cryptography, the relationship between hieroglyphs and Egyptian cultural identity, and their function in communicating with the gods. Distinguished from other ancient scripts (cuneiform, Linear B) by their pictographic aesthetic and sacral function. Connects to epigraphy, Egyptology, linguistics, art history, and the study of ancient literacy and textual transmission.

titles.title: Medical ethics
titles.author: Michael Dunn, Tony Hope
titles.subject: Philosophy/Medical Ethics
titles.abstract: Medical ethics—from thorny moral questions such as euthanasia and the morality of killing to political questions such as the fair distribution of healthcare resources—is rarely out of today’s media. Medical Ethics: A Very Short Introduction explores the ethical reasoning used to approach medical ethics, introducing the most important ‘tools’ of ethical reasoning, and discussing how argument, thought experiments, and intuition can be combined in the consideration of medical ethics. It also considers its practical application by health professionals in clinical settings and the increasingly important place of medical ethics in the wider social context, in healthcare policy, discussions in the media, pressure group and activism settings, and in legal judgements.
titles.summaryAI: Medical ethics as an applied branch of philosophy addressing moral questions in healthcare. Covers euthanasia, consent, fair distribution of healthcare resources, and health policy. Draws on ethical reasoning tools including thought experiments, intuitive moral judgments, and systematic argumentation, with references to Socrates and Isaiah Berlin's value pluralism. Examines medical ethics in clinical settings, public discourse, media, pressure group activism, and legal judgments. Distinguished from bioethics by its traditional emphasis on physician-patient relationships. Connects to political philosophy of justice, law, public health policy, and debates about autonomy, beneficence, and the limits of medical intervention.
highlights: Covers a wide range of the contemporary issues in medical ethics
Explains the 'tools' of ethical reasoning
Discusses the social uses and abuses of medical ethics worldwide
Shows how medical ethics supports health professionals in their decision-making
Considers the impact of pressure groups, activism, and the media on the ethical issues at the heart of medicine
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over nine million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Kafka
titles.author: Ritchie Robertson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Attending both to Kafka's crisis-ridden life and to the subtleties of his art, Kafka: A Very Short Introduction shows how his work explores such characteristically modern themes as the place of the body in culture, the power of institutions over people, and the possibility of religion after Nietzsche had proclaimed ‘the death of God’. Kafka is among the most intriguing and influential writers of the 20th century. During his lifetime he worked as a civil servant and published only a few short stories. All three of his novels, The Trial, The Castle, and The Man Who Disappeared [Amerika], were published after his death and helped to found Kafka's reputation as a uniquely perceptive interpreter of the 20th century.
titles.summaryAI: Franz Kafka's literary works and their significance as interpretations of 20th-century modernity. Covers major novels The Trial, The Castle, and The Man Who Disappeared (Amerika), alongside his short stories. Examines themes of institutional power over individuals, the body in culture, and religion after Nietzsche's proclamation of the death of God. Contextualizes Kafka among literary figures including Dickens, Flaubert, Tolstoy, Thomas Mann, and Byron. Addresses Kafka's civil service career and posthumous publication through Edwin Muir's translations. Distinguished from expressionism by Kafka's precise realist prose style. Connects to existentialism, modernist literature, bureaucratic theory, and the philosophy of absurdity.
highlights: This is the most up-to-date book on Kafka, a much-read and much-studied writer of enduring appeal
A short, accessible, and attractive book for general readers which focuses on the themes and motifs in Kafka's work

titles.title: Anarchism
titles.author: Alex Prichard
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Anarchism: A Very Short Introduction explores key anarchist thinkers from Kropotkin to Chomsky and considers anarchism from a variety of perspectives: theoretical, historical, and international. What exactly is anarchism and who are the anarchists? Can anarchy ever function effectively as a political force? Is it more ‘organized’ and ‘reasonable’ than is currently perceived? The word ‘anarchism’ tends to conjure up images of aggressive protest against government, and — recently — of angry demonstrations against bodies such as the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. But is anarchism inevitably linked with violent disorder? Do anarchists adhere to a coherent ideology?
titles.summaryAI: Anarchism as a political philosophy advocating the abolition of the state and hierarchical authority. Covers key thinkers including Bakunin, Kropotkin, Godwin, Goldman, Paine, Stirner, Tolstoy, and Chomsky. Examines anarchist theory spanning individualist, collectivist, anarcho-communist, and federalist traditions. Addresses anarchism's relationship to Marxism, its historical manifestations from 19th-century labor movements to contemporary anti-globalization protests against the World Bank and IMF. Explores whether anarchism requires violence or can function as organized political practice. Distinguished from libertarianism by its anti-capitalist orientation. Connects to socialist theory, direct democracy, mutual aid, and debates about the necessity of governmental authority.

titles.title: Ancient warfare
titles.author: Harry Sidebottom
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Ancient Warfare: A Very Short Introduction examines all aspects of ancient warfare, from philosophy and strategy to the technical skills needed to fight. How did wars shape classical society? How was the individual's identity constructed by war? Can a war be just? Why was siege warfare particularly bloody? What role did divine intervention play in the outcome of a battle? Greek and Roman warfare differed from other cultures and was unlike any other forms of warfare. The key difference is often held to be that the Greeks and Romans practised a ‘Western Way of War’, where the aim is an open, decisive battle, won by courage instilled in part by discipline. What is this ‘Western Way of War’?
titles.summaryAI: Ancient warfare in Greek and Roman civilizations, covering military philosophy, strategy, tactics, and combat experience. Examines the Western Way of War emphasizing open decisive battle, discipline, and courage. Covers siege warfare, divine intervention in battle, and just war theory. Key figures include Alexander the Great, Julius Caesar, and religious-military thinkers including Muhammad and Origen. Addresses how warfare shaped classical identity and society. Distinguished from modern military history by its integration with civic and religious life. Connects to political theory, ethics of war, and comparative military studies.
highlights: All aspects of ancient warfare thoroughly examined from philosophy to the technical skills needed to fight.

titles.title: Climate change
titles.author: Mark Maslin
titles.subject: Earth Sciences and Geography/Environment
titles.abstract: Climate Change: A Very Short Introduction examines the science, the history, and the politics of climate change. Drawing on the latest science from the recent IPCC reports, this VSI examines the potential catastrophic impacts of climate change in the future. Global awareness of climate change has grown very rapidly, as shown by the wide support for campaigners like Greta Thunberg and groups like Extinction Rebellion, and the declaration by many governments that we are now in a climate emergency. It is a threat that forces us to examine the whole basis of modern society. This VSI explores the geopolitical, economic, technological, and social solutions to climate change, and argues for new modes of thinking in tackling the climate crisis.
titles.summaryAI: Climate change science, history, and politics covering global warming, extreme weather events, and the climate emergency. Draws on IPCC reports for scientific evidence on temperature rise, sea level change, and ecological disruption. Examines geopolitical dimensions, global health impacts, and economic consequences of climate change. Covers climate activism including Greta Thunberg and Extinction Rebellion, alongside government declarations of climate emergency. Addresses technological, economic, and social solutions to decarbonization. Distinguished from natural climate variability by its focus on anthropogenic causes. Connects to energy policy, environmental justice, international diplomacy, public health, and the restructuring of industrial economies.

titles.title: Christianity
titles.author: Linda Woodhead
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: At a time when Christianity is flourishing in the Southern hemisphere but declining in much of the West, Christianity: A Very Short Introduction offers an important overview of the world's largest religion. Exploring the cultural and institutional dimensions of Christianity, and tracing its course over two millennia, it provides a candid portrait of Christianity's past and present. Addressing topics including the competition for power between different forms of Christianity, the churches' use of power, and its struggles with modernity, this VSI includes up-to-date information on the growth and geographical spread of Eastern Christianity, reflecting the global nature of Christianity in our ever-shifting contemporary culture.
titles.summaryAI: Christianity as a world religion spanning two millennia, covering its doctrinal development, institutional forms, and global spread. Examines the Bible (Old and New Testaments), the Gospels (Mark, Luke, John), Acts of the Apostles, and key figures including Jesus, St Thomas Aquinas, and Martin Luther. Covers the Roman Catholic Church, Orthodox Church, and Protestant traditions including the Second Vatican Council and Brethren of the Free Spirit. Addresses the growth of Eastern Christianity, competition for power among denominations, and Christianity's contemporary flourishing in the Southern hemisphere while declining in the West. Connects to theology, church history, ethics, and the sociology of religion.
highlights: A short and accessible guide to the world's largest religion
Considers the rise and growth of Christianity throughout history
Outlines the main principles and beliefs of Christian thought
Demonstrates the diversity of Christian faith and practice in the modern world
Explores their struggle with modernity and gender
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over six million copies worldwide
newToThisEdition: Explores the continued expansion of Christianity throughout some parts of the world
Looks at how and why Christianity has declined in the West
Updated to reflect the changing nature and challenges Christianity faces in an increasingly globalized world
Includes an updated further reading section

titles.title: Modern art
titles.author: David Cottington
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Modern Art: A Very Short Introduction examines many key aspects of this subject, including the issue of controversy in modern art, from Manet's Dejeuner sur L'Herbe (1863) to Picasso's Les Demoiselles, and Tracey Emin's Bed, (1999); and the role of the dealer. Public interest in modern art continues to grow, as witnessed by the spectacular success of Tate Modern and the Bilbao Guggenheim. What makes a work of art qualify as modern (or fail to)? How is this selection made? What is the relationship between modern and contemporary art? Is ‘postmodernist’ art no longer modern, or just no longer modernist? Why, and what does this claim mean, both for art and the idea of ‘the modern’?
titles.summaryAI: Modern art from the mid-19th century through the 20th century, covering avant-garde movements, institutional frameworks, and critical debates about what qualifies as modern. Examines key artists including Manet, Picasso, Van Gogh, Braque, Leger, Mondrian, Marinetti, Tatlin, Oldenburg, and the Young British Artists including Tracey Emin. Covers the role of museums (MoMA, Tate Modern, Bilbao Guggenheim), dealers, and critics like Clement Greenberg. Addresses the relationship between modern and contemporary art, modernism versus postmodernism, and the definition of artistic modernity. Distinguished from academic art by its rejection of traditional conventions. Connects to aesthetics, cultural institutions, art market economics, and cultural modernity.
highlights: Much-needed, accessible approach that grapples clearly and provocatively with the complex idea of modern art
Cottington is the author of the best-selling book, Cubism
The book covers all main artists and movements, as well as the new developments in modern art
Examines key aspects of modern art using examples such as Manet's Dejeuner sur L'Herbe (1863) to Picasso's Les Demoiselles, and Tracey Emin's Bed (1999)
Looks at the role of the art dealer, from the main Cubist dealer, Kahnweiler to Charles Saatchi

titles.title: Consciousness
titles.author: Susan Blackmore
titles.subject: Philosophy/Psychology
titles.abstract: Consciousness is ‘the last great mystery for science’. How can a physical brain create our experience of the world? What creates our identity? Do we really have free will? Could consciousness itself be an illusion? Exciting new developments in brain science are continuing these debates, and the field has now expanded to include biologists, neuroscientists, psychologists, and philosophers. Consciousness: A Very Short Introduction clarifies the potentially confusing arguments, and the major theories, whilst also outlining the amazing pace of neuroscience discoveries. Covering areas such as construction of self in the brain, mechanisms of attention, neural correlates of consciousness, and physiology of altered states of consciousness, it highlights the latest findings.
titles.summaryAI: Consciousness as studied across philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience. Covers the hard problem of consciousness, neural correlates of consciousness, mechanisms of attention, and construction of self in the brain. Examines the grand illusion theory, whether consciousness itself may be illusory, the evolution of consciousness, and the relationship between free will and subjective experience. Addresses altered states of consciousness and their physiological basis. Distinguished from mere wakefulness or responsiveness by its subjective experiential quality (qualia). Connects to the philosophy of mind, cognitive neuroscience, artificial intelligence, meditation research, and debates about the explanatory gap between physical processes and subjective experience.
highlights: A lively and authoritative introduction to the hot topic of consciousness
A much-needed launch pad for the further exploration of this complicated, controversial, and unresolved issue
Challenges readers to reconsider key concepts such as personality, free will, and the soul
Combines the perspectives of philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience on the subject of consciousness
Considers new research in key areas such as the physiology of altered states of consciousness, and the construction of self in the brain
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over eight million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Foucault
titles.author: Gary Gutting
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Foucault: A Very Short Introduction presents a wide-ranging, but non-systematic exploration of some highlights of Foucault’s life and thought. Beginning with a brief biography of the philosopher to set the social and political stage, it then tackles Foucault’s thoughts on literature, in particular the avant-garde scene; his philosophical and historical work; his treatment of knowledge and power in modern society; and his thoughts on sexuality. This new edition includes feminist criticisms of Foucault’s apparently sexist treatment of the Jouy case, as well as a new chapter offering a unified overview of the Collège de France lectures, now a major focus of interest in Foucault.
titles.summaryAI: Michel Foucault's philosophy spanning literature, history, knowledge, power, and sexuality. Covers Foucault's analysis of discourse, institutional power, and the construction of the modern subject through disciplines and surveillance. Examines his engagement with avant-garde literature, philosophical and historical methodology, and the relationship between knowledge and power in modern society. Addresses feminist criticisms of his treatment of the Jouy case and his College de France lectures on governmentality and biopolitics. Key intellectual context includes Kant, Spinoza, and Adam Smith. Distinguished from traditional philosophy by its genealogical method. Connects to social theory, queer studies, criminology, medical history, and poststructuralism.
highlights: Provides a wide-ranging but non-systematic treatment of some highlights of Foucault's life and thought
New edition includes feminist criticisms of Foucault's apparently sexist treatment of the Jouy case
Includes a new chapter offering a unified overview of the Collège de France lectures, now a major focus of interest in Foucault
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Spanish Civil War
titles.author: Helen Graham
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Spanish Civil War: Very Short Introduction offers an explanation of the war's origins and course, explores its impact on a personal and international scale, and provides an ethical reflection on the war. How has the war inspired some of the greatest writers of our time? In what ways does it continue to resonate today in Britain, continental Europe, and beyond? The war can be seen as an arena of social change where ideas about culture were forged or resisted, and in which both Spaniards and non-Spaniards participated alike. During the Second World War, these conflicts would stretch from Franco's regime, which envisaged itself as part of the Nazi new order, to Europe and beyond.
titles.summaryAI: The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) as a military conflict, social revolution, and international crisis. Covers the war's origins in Spanish political polarization, the Catholic Church's role, international intervention including Mussolini's Italy, and propaganda campaigns. Examines personal and civilian impact, the war's influence on major writers, and its connection to rising fascism. Addresses Franco's regime and its alignment with the Nazi order during World War II. Distinguished from other interwar conflicts by attracting international volunteers. Connects to European fascism, anti-fascist movements, cultural production, and Cold War McCarthyism.

titles.title: The Marquis de Sade
titles.author: John Phillips
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: The Marquis de Sade: A Very Short Introduction aims to disentangle the ‘real’ Marquis de Sade from his mythical and demonic reputation of the past two hundred years by examining Sade's libertine novels, his championing of atheism, and his uniqueness in bringing the body and sex back into philosophy. Were it not for the Sade's explicit use of language and complete disregard for the artificially constructed taboos of a religious morality he despised, the novelty and profundity of his thought, and above all, its fundamental modernity, would have long since secured him a place alongside the greatest authors and thinkers of the European Enlightenment.
titles.summaryAI: The Marquis de Sade (1740-1814) as a writer and philosopher of the French Enlightenment. Covers his libertine novels, radical atheism, and philosophical project of reintegrating the body and sexuality into philosophical discourse. Examines his relationship to Enlightenment thinkers including Diderot and Rousseau, and his influence on later figures including Freud, Bataille, and Pasolini. Addresses the construction of Sade's mythical and demonic reputation over two centuries versus his actual philosophical contributions. Distinguished from pornography by his systematic philosophical materialism. Connects to the history of sexuality, Enlightenment philosophy, censorship, moral philosophy, and the relationship between literature and transgression.
highlights: Highlights the fact that Sade is studied not just as a pornographer but for his important contributions to literature, philosophy and the arts
Reviews his novels and lesser-known works, and their reception throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, taking account of current theoretical debates
Explains his profound challenge to the taboos of religious morality, and shows how his work can be seen as an expression of the cult of the body
This will be the most reliable and best informed introduction to his work

titles.title: Habermas
titles.author: James Gordon Finlayson
titles.subject: Philosophy/Sociology/Biography
titles.abstract: Habermas: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of Jürgen Habermas's forbiddingly complex philosophy using concrete examples. Habermas is arguably the most influential German philosopher alive today. He has influenced a variety of fields, including philosophy, political and social theory, cultural studies, sociology, and literary studies. He has commented widely on subjects such as Marxism, the importance and effectiveness of communication, the reunification of Germany, and the European Union. What are the theoretical underpinnings of Habermas's social theory? How can his social theory be applied to the fields of ethics, politics, and law? How have Habermas's social and political theories informed his writing on contemporary, political, and social problems?
titles.summaryAI: Jurgen Habermas's social and political philosophy spanning critical theory, discourse ethics, and democratic theory. Covers the Frankfurt School intellectual tradition, communicative action theory, the relationship between validity and meaning, and the application of discourse ethics to law and politics. Examines Habermas's engagement with Marxism, German reunification, the European Union, and contemporary political problems. Addresses the concept of civil society and the public sphere. Distinguished from poststructuralism by its commitment to rational consensus and universal norms. Connects to sociology, philosophy of language, political theory, legal philosophy, and debates about modernity and communicative rationality.
highlights: Provides a completely up to date and much-needed entry into the long and forbiddingly technical works of the most influential German philosopher alive today
Covers Habermas's influence on a wide variety of fields, including philosophy, political and social theory, cultural studies, sociology, and literary studies
Explains how Habermas's ideas are applied to present day social and political situations
Unlike other writings on Habermas, this explains his intellectual framework and technical vocabulary rather than simply adopting it

titles.title: Socialism
titles.author: Michael Newman
titles.subject: Politics/Economics/History
titles.abstract: Socialism: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of socialism and its contemporary relevance. Is socialism an outdated ideology today? This VSI provides an overview of socialism’s origins, its relationships to communism and social democracy, its different manifestations around the world and throughout history, and its intersections with issues of ethnicity and gender. Examples of the practical implementation of socialist values are provided by case studies of social democracy in Sweden and communism in Cuba. The rise of the ‘New Left’ midway through the 20th century included feminist and green movements, followed up by radical anti-capitalist and climate justice protests and political movements.
titles.summaryAI: Socialism as a political ideology and movement traced from its 19th-century origins through communism and social democracy to contemporary progressive movements. Covers case studies of Swedish social democracy and Cuban communism.
