titles.abstract: The Orchestra: A Very Short Introduction considers the structure, roots, and day-to-day functioning of the modern philharmonic society and explores topics ranging from the life of a musician in a modern orchestra, the recent wave of new hall construction, threats of bankruptcies and strikes, and the high salaries of conductors and general managers. Can such a seemingly anachronistic organization long survive? Does the symphony matter in contemporary culture? The orchestra remains a potent political and social force. It has adapted well to the digital revolution, and it continues to be seen with civic pride. In a time of upheaval in how classical music is created, heard, distributed, and evaluated, the orchestra has managed to retain its historic role.
titles.summaryAI: The modern symphony orchestra as an institution, covering its structure, historical roots from Bach and Mozart through Beethoven, and contemporary functioning. Examines the daily life of orchestral musicians, conductor roles and compensation, concert hall construction, and financial challenges including bankruptcies and labor disputes. Addresses major ensembles including the Berlin Philharmonic, Boston Symphony, and Los Angeles Philharmonic. Explores the orchestra's adaptation to digital technology, its enduring role as a political and social force, and debates about its relevance in contemporary culture. Covers how classical music creation, distribution, and evaluation continue evolving.
highlights: Surveys the structure, roots, and day-to-day functioning of the modern philharmonic society
Disputes prevalent notions of the death of the orchestra
Argues for the centrality of the symphony orchestra in cultural diplomacy and civic pride
Welcomes new media for the consumption of orchestral music
Updates orchestral history with accounts of recent strikes and bankruptcies on the one hand, and the conspicious success of several massive new concert halls, on the other

titles.title: Governance
titles.author: Mark Bevir
titles.subject: Politics
keywords: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
titles.abstract: Governance: A Very Short Introduction explains the main theories of governance and their impact in the corporate, public, and global spheres. The many uses of the term ‘governance’are expolored: the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund make loans conditional on ‘good governance’; climate change and avian flu appear as issues of ‘global governance’; the US Forest Service calls for ‘collaborative governance’. Why is the term used so pervasively and to what does it refer? What makes good or bad governance?
titles.summaryAI: Governance theories and their application across corporate, public, and global spheres. Examines the proliferation of governance discourse in international organizations including the World Bank, IMF, United Nations, WHO, WTO, OECD, and European Union. Covers concepts of good governance as conditions for international development lending, global governance addressing transnational challenges like climate change and pandemic disease, and collaborative governance in public administration. Explores neoclassical economic frameworks underlying governance reforms, the role of interest groups, accountability mechanisms, and state capacity. Addresses why governance has become a pervasive organizing concept across institutional contexts.
highlights: Explores the definitions of one of the most widely used and yet least understood concepts
Considers the main contemporary theories of governance and the interaction with corporate, public, and global spheres
Considers issues such as what makes good or bad governance
Part of the best-selling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: American History
titles.author: Paul S. Boyer
titles.subject: History – U.S.
keywords: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
titles.abstract: American History: A Very Short Introduction spans the earliest migrations to the present looking at the United States's failures to live up to its oft-stated ideals. The establishment of the North American colonies had very different meanings for colonists, native peoples, and enslaved Africans. The late nineteenth century saw industrial expansion, appalling conditions, and an exploited labor force. The twentieth-century emergence of a suburban society of consumer abundance meant a better life for many, yet left behind crime-ridden inner cities and spawned a stultifying mass culture. While American popular culture has demonstrated global appeal, the projection of U.S. military power abroad has sometimes failed in its purpose and damaged the nation's international standing.
titles.summaryAI: United States history from earliest migrations through the present, covering colonial establishment and its divergent meanings for colonists, Native Americans, and enslaved Africans. Examines nineteenth-century industrialization, labor exploitation, abolitionism under William Lloyd Garrison, and women's suffrage activism led by Elizabeth Cady Stanton. Addresses the rise of suburban consumer society, inner-city decline, and mass culture in the twentieth century. Covers racism, the NAACP, Cold War institutions including NATO and the National Security Council, and populist politics of William Jennings Bryan. Explores tensions between American ideals and persistent social inequalities.
highlights: Coverage goes beyond the traditional themes of politics, diplomacy and war to encompass the full spectrum of social, cultural, and scientific developments
Religious beliefs, from the colonial era to the present, receive full attention
Seeks to present a balanced picture of the achievements and failures that comprise the story of the American past

titles.title: Networks
titles.author: Guido Caldarelli, Michele Catanzaro
titles.subject: Computing/Mathematics/Engineering/Science
titles.abstract: Networks: A Very Short Introduction considers the basic elements of network theory and its applications using everyday examples from society, technology, nature, and history. It is impossible to understand the spread of an epidemic, a computer virus, large-scale blackouts, or massive extinctions without taking into account the network structure that underlies all these phenomena. The ubiquitous role of networks; how networks self-organize; why the rich get richer; and how networks can spontaneously collapse are all considered. The findings of complex network theory have very wide and important applications in genetics, ecology, communications, economics, and sociology.
titles.summaryAI: Network theory covering fundamental concepts including topology, degree distribution, connectivity, components, and optimization in complex systems. Examines network structures underlying epidemics, computer viruses, power grid blackouts, and mass extinctions. Addresses self-organization in networks, preferential attachment explaining why the rich get richer, and mechanisms of spontaneous network collapse. Applies network analysis across genetics, ecology, communications technology, economics, and sociology. Covers random networks, scale-free networks, and the mathematical frameworks for understanding how interconnected systems behave, emphasizing the ubiquitous role of network structure in natural, social, and technological phenomena.
highlights: Considers the basic elements of network theory and its applications
Uses examples from everyday life - from nature, technology, society, and history - to explain the basic theory
An engaging introduction for anyone trying to understand complex networks
Part of the bestsellings Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Spirituality
titles.author: Philip Sheldrake
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Spirituality: A Very Short Introduction explores the historical foundations of spirituality and considers how it came to have the significance it is developing today. It has been suggested that ‘spirituality’ has become a word that ‘can define an era’. Why? Because paradoxically, alongside a decline in traditional religious affiliations, the growing interest in spirituality is a striking aspect of contemporary western cultures. Spirituality refers to the deepest values and sense of meaning by which people seek to live. Sometimes these values are conventionally religious. Sometimes they are associated with what is understood as ‘the sacred’ in a broader sense — that is, of ultimate rather than merely instrumental importance.
titles.summaryAI: Spirituality as a concept encompassing the deepest values and meanings by which people live, spanning both religious and secular frameworks. Examines historical foundations across major traditions: Christianity, Buddhism, Hinduism, Islam, and Judaism. Explores why interest in spirituality has grown alongside declining traditional religious affiliation in contemporary Western cultures. Addresses connections between spirituality and architecture, sacred spaces, labyrinths, and contemplative practices influenced by figures like Thich Nhat Hanh. Covers the relationship between spirituality and transpersonal psychology, Freudian psychoanalysis, and the broader distinction between instrumental and ultimate values in human experience.
highlights: Written by one of the most eminent scholars and writers on spirituality
Looks at the impact of spirituality on religion and philosophy, but also on anthropology and psychology
Considers the historical foundations of spirituality in the major world religions
Explores the concept of the spiritual 'experience' and considers the impact and transformation it has on society
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Work
titles.author: Stephen Fineman
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: Work: A Very Short Introduction tells the story of work — how we strive for security, reward, and often, meaning. The image of a job captures our imagination from an early age, usually prompted by the question: ‘What do you want to be when you grow up?’. Work, whether it be paid, unpaid, voluntary, or obligatory, is woven into the fabric of human society. For many it forms our identity. For others it is a tedious necessity. What is ‘work’? What cultural and social factors influence work? Globalization and technology both affect the evolution of work. Living is problematic without paid work, and for many it is catastrophic.
titles.summaryAI: Work as a social, economic, and psychological phenomenon encompassing paid employment, unpaid labor, voluntary activities, and obligatory tasks. Examines how work forms identity, provides meaning, and shapes human society. Covers the cultural and social factors influencing work including capitalism, Weber's Protestant ethic, and management theory. Addresses globalization and technology as forces transforming employment patterns, career structures, and job satisfaction. Explores emotional dimensions of work, the gendered nature of housework, and the catastrophic consequences of unemployment. Considers how childhood aspirations about occupations reflect deeper cultural values about purpose and social worth.
highlights: An authoritative but accessible short introduction to the concept of work
Explores the concepts that have shaped 'work' in different societies at different times
Considers the organization of working - from employment and labour - to their social class and power implications
Explores the types of work and their moral implications
Looks at the cultural aspects of gender issues and work
Highlights how the concept of work continues to change and how it will develop in future
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Martyrdom
titles.author: Jolyon Mitchell
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Martyrdom: A Very Short Introduction provides a historical analysis of the concept of martyrdom to shed light on how it has evolved and the different ways in which it is used today. A sharply contested term and act, martyrdom has a long history of provoking controversy. Suicide attacks have made recurring questions about martyrdom more pertinent to current discussions. What is martyrdom? Why are some people drawn towards giving up their lives as martyrs? How are martyrs made and what place does religion play in inciting and creating martyrs? Why are some martyrs and martyrdoms remembered more than others? How helpful is the distinction between active and passive martyrdoms?
titles.summaryAI: Martyrdom as a concept and practice across history, examining its evolution from early religious contexts to contemporary usage. Explores what motivates individuals to sacrifice their lives, how martyrs are constructed through media and cultural memory, and why certain martyrdoms are remembered more than others. Addresses the role of religion in inciting and creating martyrs across Christianity, Islam, and other traditions. Covers the distinction between active and passive martyrdom, connections to the Holocaust and Henry VIII's religious persecutions, and the relevance of martyrdom to modern suicide attacks and political violence debates.
highlights: Provides a clear and concise discussion of the debates surrounding martyrdom
Includes specific cases of martyrdoms from various different historical contexts and religious traditions
Addresses the most pertinent questions: What is martyrdom?, Why are some people drawn towards giving up their lives as martyrs?, What place does religion play in inciting and creating martyrs?
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Colonial America
titles.author: Alan Taylor
titles.subject: history – U.S.
titles.abstract: Colonial America: A Very Short Introduction presents the current scholarly understanding of the subject. During the past generation, historians have broadened that understanding by adopting both a trans-Atlantic and a trans-continental perspective, examining the interplay of Europe, Africa, and the Americas through the flows of goods, people, plants, animals, capital, and ideas. Colonial America produced an unprecedented mixing of radically diverse peoples — African, European, and Indian — under stressful circumstances for all. The colonial intermingling of peoples, microbes, plants, and animals from different continents was unparalleled in speed and volume. Everyone had to adjust to a new world of unpredictable social and cultural hybrids that compromised and complicated the ambitious plans of empire-builders.
titles.summaryAI: Colonial America examined through trans-Atlantic and trans-continental perspectives, covering the interplay of European, African, and indigenous American peoples from 1492 through approximately 1790. Addresses flows of goods, people, plants, animals, capital, and ideas across continents. Examines the unprecedented biological, demographic, and cultural mixing of radically diverse populations under colonial conditions. Covers mapping and territorial claims, the role of the West Indies trade, key figures including Benjamin Franklin and military leaders like Jeffrey Amherst and James Wolfe. Explores social and cultural hybridization that complicated imperial ambitions of European colonizers.
highlights: Concisely covers the colonial experience on a continental scale, including English, Dutch, French and Spanish colonies throughout North America
Highlights the encounters of native with colonial peoples
Focuses on the relationship of people to their environments
Examines slavery and freedom in different colonial empires

titles.title: Rastafari
titles.author: Ennis B. Edmonds
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Rastafari: A Very Short Introduction provides an account of this widespread but often poorly understood movement looking at its essential history, principles and practices, and its internal character and configuration. What has caused its global spread? Why has it had such far-reaching influence on cultural and artistic production in the Caribbean and beyond? From its obscure beginnings in Jamaica in the early 1930s, Rastafari has grown into an international socio-religious movement. It is estimated that 700,000 to 1 million people worldwide have embraced Rastafari, and adherents of the movement can be found in most major population centres and many outposts of the world.
titles.summaryAI: Rastafari as a socio-religious movement originating in Jamaica in the early 1930s and spreading globally to an estimated 700,000 to one million adherents. Covers essential history, core principles, ritual practices, and internal configurations of the movement. Examines the concept of Babylon, connections to the biblical Tribes of Israel, and the development of Rastafari consciousness. Addresses the movement's profound influence on Caribbean and global cultural and artistic production, particularly through music and media. Explores reasons for Rastafari's international spread and its significance as a distinctive religious and cultural identity movement.
highlights: A timely introduction to a rapidly growing religious movement
Covers all aspects of the movement - its history, practice, and its global spread
Discusses gender issues within Rastafari
Examines its influence on cultural and artistic production in the Caribbean and beyond
Part of the best-selling Very Short Introduction series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Comedy
titles.author: Matthew Bevis
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Comedy: A Very Short Introduction studies written forms of comedy such as prose fiction, poetry, caricatures, and cartoons. There are also performance forms of comedy such as comic drama, pantomime, comic opera, silent cinema, popular music, Broadway shows, music-hall, stand-up and circus acts, rom-coms, sketch shows, sit-coms. To consider comedy in its incarnations is to raise diverse questions: what is humour, and how may it be used (or abused)? When do we laugh, and why? What is it that writers and speakers enjoy — and risk — when they tell a joke, indulge in bathos, talk nonsense, or encourage irony? Comedy is both a literary genre and a forum for a range of non-literary phenomena, experiences, and events.
titles.summaryAI: Comedy as both a literary genre and a broader cultural phenomenon, covering written forms including prose fiction, poetry, caricatures, and cartoons, alongside performance traditions: comic drama, pantomime, opera, silent cinema, music hall, stand-up, circus, sitcoms, and romantic comedies. Examines theoretical approaches from Bergson, Frye, and Wittgenstein. Explores the nature of humor, irony, parody, bathos, and nonsense. Addresses Restoration Comedy and the history of comedic conventions. Considers when and why people laugh, the risks and pleasures of joke-telling, and the relationship between comic art and non-literary experiences.
highlights: Explores many different forms of comedy from the Greeks to the present
Takes examples from both high and low culture, and from several different literatures and languages
Develops a comparitive approach which allows comic theory and practice to shed new light upon one another
Offers both close readings and broad overviews of comic styles, tropes and impulses
Written in a witty and engaging style
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Avant-Garde
titles.author: David Cottington
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: The Avant Garde: A Very Short Introduction explores the concept of the ‘avant-garde’ and examines its wider context through the development of western modernity, capitalist culture, and the global impact of both. The avant-garde is perhaps the most important and influential concept in the history of modern culture. What is the relation between the ‘avant-garde’ and ‘avant-garde’ qualities in a work of art, design, architecture, or any other cultural product? What does avant-gardism mean? Now that contemporary art has broken all taboos and is at the centre of a billion-pound art market, is there still an ‘avant-garde’? If so, what is the point of it and who are the artists concerned?
titles.summaryAI: The avant-garde as a concept in modern Western culture, tracing its development within capitalist modernity and its global impact. Examines key figures and movements: Baudelaire, Seurat, Picasso, Marinetti, Cocteau, and critic Clement Greenberg. Covers institutional contexts including the Salon des Refuses, Salon des Independants, and the Museum of Modern Art. Explores the relationship between avant-garde ideology and qualities in art, design, and architecture. Addresses the tension between avant-garde transgression and the contemporary billion-pound art market, questioning whether genuine avant-gardism persists when all artistic taboos have been broken.
highlights: Explores one of the most influential concepts in modern culture
Considers its different definitions and uses, and its history as an ideology
Examines the relevance of the avant-garde in the wider context of western modernity and our capitalist culture
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Thought
titles.author: Tim Bayne
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Thought: A Very Short Introduction looks at the nature of thought. Beginning with questions about what thought is and what distinguishes it from other kinds of mental states, it examines various interpretations of thought from philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, and anthropology. There is no denying that thinking comes naturally to human beings. There are many things we know and don't know about thoughts. How is thought realized in the brain? Does thinking occur in public or is it a purely private affair? Do young children and non-human animals think? Is human thought the same everywhere, or are there culturally specific modes of thought? What is the relationship between thought and language? What kind of responsibility do we have for our thoughts?
titles.summaryAI: The nature of thought examined through philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, and anthropology. Explores what distinguishes thought from other mental states including perception and consciousness. Addresses how thought is realized in brain processes, whether thinking is public or private, and questions about thought in young children and non-human animals. Examines the relationship between thought and language, culturally specific modes of reasoning versus universal cognitive patterns, and the ethics of responsibility for one's thoughts. Covers self-deception, counting, attitudes, and the boundaries between thinking, perceiving, and other forms of mental activity.
highlights: A lively and accessible introduction to the nature of thought
Establishes the fundamental differences between thought and other mental states
Explores and highlights our amazing capacity for thought
Looks at the interaction between thought, rationality, and our concept of the abstract
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series

titles.title: The Napoleonic Wars
titles.author: Mike Rapport
titles.subject: History/Military
titles.abstract: The Napoleonic Wars: A Very Short Introduction provides a brief outline of these conflicts, introducing the tactics, strategies, and weaponry of the time. What were the origins of the wars? In what ways and means were the wars fought? What are the political and social legacies of the wars today? The Napoleonic Wars have an important place in the history of Europe, leaving their mark on European and world societies in a variety of ways. In many European countries they provided the stimulus for radical social and political change — particularly in Spain, Germany, and Italy — and are frequently viewed in these countries as the starting point of their modern histories.
titles.summaryAI: The Napoleonic Wars covering their origins in the French Revolution, military tactics, strategies, and weaponry of the era. Examines major campaigns including the Peninsular War and conflicts involving Britain, Prussia, Russia, and the Holy Roman Empire. Addresses political and social legacies across Europe, particularly as catalysts for radical change in Spain, Germany, and Italy, where these wars mark the beginning of modern national histories. Covers the Confederation of the Rhine, the Papal States, and connections to the Revolutions of 1848. Explores the wars' lasting impact on European state formation and international relations.
highlights: Introduces the tactics, strategies, and weaponry used in the wars
Discusses the social, political, and institutional aspects of warfare in the Napoleonic era
Addresses the controversy as to how far the Napoleonic Wars can be differentiated from the Revolutionary Wars
Sets the wars in their broader historical context - in both European and global terms - exploring their social and political impact
Explains the significance of the wars, and their legacy, in both specific national contexts and from a wider global perspective
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over six million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Medical law
titles.author: Charles Foster
titles.subject: Medicine/Law
titles.abstract: Medical law is concerned with our bodies, and what happens to them during and after our lives. The core issues of medical law — organ transplantation, abortion, withdrawal of treatment, euthanasia, confidentiality, research on humans — affect us all. Medical Law: A Very Short Introduction explores different examples of these issues to illustrate the key problems and principles of medical law. When things go wrong with our bodies, we want to know what our rights are, and what governs the conduct of the clinicians into whose hands we put our lives and limbs. Dealing with matters of life and death, it can therefore have a fundamental impact on medical practice.
titles.summaryAI: Medical law governing the human body during and after life, covering core issues: organ transplantation and donation, abortion, withdrawal of treatment, euthanasia, confidentiality, consent, and research on human subjects. Examines the legal frameworks for medical negligence, best interests determinations for incapacitated patients, and children's medical rights. Addresses ownership of body parts, the European Convention on Human Rights as applied to medical practice, and the relationship between medical ethics and legal obligations. Explores how legal principles shape clinical practice and patients' rights in matters of life, death, and bodily autonomy.
highlights: An engaging and accessible account of the main principles of medical law
Examines a wide range of areas from abortion to the availability of IVF, and the withdrawal of medical treatment
Uses case studies from international jurisdictions to fully understand the main principles
Assumes no previous knowledge of the subject
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Rhetoric
titles.author: Richard Toye
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Rhetoric is often seen as a synonym for shallow, deceptive language, and therefore as something negative. But if it is viewed in more neutral terms, as the ‘art of persuasion’, it is clear that we are all forced to engage with it at some level, if only because we are constantly exposed to the rhetoric of others. Rhetoric: A Very Short Introduction explores the purpose of rhetoric and shows that it is the foundation-stone of civil society, and an essential part of democracy. Wide-ranging examples from Ancient Greece, medieval Islamic preaching, and modern cinema, are used to consider why we should all have an appreciation of the art of rhetoric.
titles.summaryAI: Rhetoric as the art of persuasion, its history, theory, and practice from ancient Greece through medieval Islamic preaching to modern cinema and political discourse. Challenges the negative perception of rhetoric as mere deception, arguing it serves as a foundation of civil society and democracy. Examines classical concepts including oratory, pathos, and ideological persuasion. Explores rhetoric's relationship to civil society, political communication, and public deliberation. Covers the historical development of rhetorical theory and its practical applications across cultures, demonstrating why understanding persuasive communication remains essential in democratic societies.
highlights: Includes the key rhetorical concepts and techniques
Helps the reader develop a critical appreciation of the rhetoric of others
Uses historical examples to illustrate the main issues
Considers the use of rhetoric in a number of different contexts
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series

titles.title: Education
titles.author: Gary Thomas
titles.subject: Education
titles.abstract: Education: A Very Short Introduction examines the ideas that have shaped education over the years, showing how and why today’s schools have grown into their current form. It explores how education has been moulded by politics, philosophy, and social science, and focuses especially on arguments over the competing benefits of formality and freedom in teaching. This new edition examines in greater depth the inequalities perpetuated by current education systems, asking whether education makes for social mobility, as often claimed, or, in reality, actually reinforces existing inequity. Looking forward, it evaluates the impact of controversial legislation on future students, and considers how teaching must evolve to keep pace with an increasingly digital world.
titles.summaryAI: Education systems examined through historical, philosophical, and social scientific perspectives. Covers how schools evolved into their current institutional forms, shaped by political agendas and cultural change. Explores the tension between formal instruction and progressive freedom in teaching methods. Addresses educational inequality, questioning whether schooling promotes social mobility or reinforces existing inequity. Examines the impact of controversial legislation on students and the challenge of adapting pedagogy for an increasingly digital world. Covers philosophical debates about education's purposes, the relationship between education and democratic citizenship, and systemic barriers to equitable educational outcomes.
highlights: Explores how the evolution of education has been shaped by ideas in politics, philosophy, and the social sciences
Considers controversy over progressive versus formal teaching
Examines the work of important education theorists, including Dewey, Piaget and Vygotsky
Reflects on the curriculum and how it can be shaped to meet the needs of a rapidly changing world
Assesses reasons for the repeated false starts in moves to inclusivity
Questions the oft-made claim of education to be a force for social mobility

titles.title: Mao
titles.author: Delia Davin
titles.subject: Politics - China/Biography
titles.abstract: Mao: A Very Short Introduction chronicles Mao's journey from peasant child to ruler of China. He was a founder of both the Chinese Communist Party and the Red Army. His revolution unified China and began its rise to world power status. He was the architect of the Great Leap Forward that he hoped would make China both prosperous and egalitarian, but instead ended in economic disaster resulting in millions of deaths. It was Mao's growing suspicion of his fellow leaders that led him to launch the Cultural Revolution, and his last years were dogged by ill-health and his despairing attempts to find a successor whom he trusted.
titles.summaryAI: Mao Zedong's life and political career from peasant origins to ruler of China. Covers his role as co-founder of the Chinese Communist Party and the Red Army, his leadership of the revolution that unified China, and China's rise to world power status. Examines the Great Leap Forward's catastrophic economic failure resulting in millions of deaths, and Mao's subsequent launch of the Cultural Revolution driven by suspicion of fellow leaders including Liu Shaoqi and Deng Xiaoping. Addresses the roles of Jiang Qing, Lin Biao, the Gang of Four, and Mao's relationship with the Soviet Union.
highlights: Ideal for anyone wishing to know more about one of the most defining figures of the 20th century Asian history
Fascinating story of Mao's life - from his childhood to his time as leader of China
Considers Mao the man as well as Mao the leader - exploring the major events in his life and his revolutionary writings
Examines the events that led to the Cultural Revolution and its historical impact
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over five million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The British Constitution
titles.author: Martin Loughlin
titles.subject: Politics - U.K.
titles.abstract: The British Constitution: A Very Short Introduction explores one of the most unusual constitutional arrangements in the world. It considers the scope and nature of the British constitution and the challenges that have been generated by this approach. This VSI examines the origins of the British constitution and the alterations made to it in response to the changing nature of its state — from England, to Britain, to the United Kingdom, to the impact of Britain's membership of the European Union. Lastly, it examines the future prospects of the British constitution in an ever-changing political environment.
titles.summaryAI: The British constitutional system as one of the world's most unusual unwritten constitutional arrangements. Traces its origins and evolution through the transformation from England to Britain to the United Kingdom. Covers key constitutional documents and principles including the Bill of Rights, Petition of Right, Act of Settlement, and common law traditions. Examines the roles of the House of Commons, House of Lords, civil service, and major political parties. Addresses the impact of the European Communities Act and EU membership on constitutional sovereignty. Explores future prospects for constitutional reform in a changing political environment.
highlights: Explains the scope and nature of the British constitution
Examines how this unique set of constitutional arrangements has evolved
Reflects on the future prospects of the British constitution
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide
newToThisEdition: Includes a discussion of the impact of developments over the decade since its first publication

titles.title: American politics
titles.author: Richard M. Valelly
titles.subject: Politics - U.S.
titles.abstract: American Politics: A Very Short Introduction introduces the vital elements of American politics. Using an historical-institutional approach, it explains how the American political system is, and always has been, a work in progress — one unfolding within, and also constantly updating, an eighteenth-century constitutional framework. It explores the issue of parties and polarization and surveys the basic institutions: the Presidency, Congress, the judicial branch, the unelected bureaucracy of the independent agencies, and state and local governments. It also deals with political communication, public opinion, voting and the boundaries of the electorate, and the politics of government steering of the economy.
titles.summaryAI: The American political system analyzed through a historical-institutional framework, examining how an eighteenth-century constitutional structure continuously adapts. Covers core institutions: the Presidency, Congress, the judiciary, independent agencies including the Environmental Protection Agency and Federal Election Commission, and state and local governments. Examines the Electoral College, party polarization, political communication, public opinion, and voter participation including Asian American political engagement. Addresses the politics of economic management, the expanding and contracting boundaries of the electorate, and the legacies of figures including Lincoln and Franklin Roosevelt in shaping institutional development.
highlights: Provides a balanced consideration of both strengths and weaknesses of the American political system
Incorporates a historical perspective on the American political system's evolution and its major institutions
Presents an original treatment of the presidency's evolution, with an emphasis on how American social science has played a role in that process

titles.title: The Silk Road
titles.author: James A. Millward
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Silk Road: A Very Short Introduction looks beyond the initial exotic impression conveyed by the phrase ‘silk road’ to sketch the historical background against which the silk road flourished, and consider the significance of old-world intercultural exchange to Eurasian and world history generally. This VSI outlines the dynamics of Central Eurasian history that promoted silk road interactions, especially the role of nomad empires; and highlights the biological, technological and cultural as well as economic exchanges on the silk road. It also discusses the ideological connotations and abstract uses of the concept and disputes the common assertion of a ‘silk road decline’ from around 1500.
titles.summaryAI: The Silk Road as a network of Central Eurasian trade and cultural exchange routes, covering biological, technological, economic, and cultural transfers across the old world. Examines the historical dynamics of Central Eurasian history that promoted intercontinental exchange, particularly the role of nomad empires. Addresses the trade in silk, paper, and other commodities alongside the transmission of ideas and technologies. Covers figures including explorers like Anthony Jenkinson and archaeologist Aurel Stein. Disputes the common assertion of silk road decline from around 1500, and examines the ideological and abstract uses of the silk road concept in modern scholarship.
highlights: Highlights biological, technological, and cultural as well as economic exchanges on the silk road
Explains the dynamics of silk road communication as a function of the rise of Central Eurasian nomad empires and their interaction with sedentary agrarian states
Disputes the common assertion of a "silk road decline" from around 1500

titles.title: Bacteria
titles.author: Sebastian G. B. Amyes
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Bacteria form a fundamental branch of life. They are the oldest forms of life as we know it, and they are still the most prolific living organisms. Bacteria: A Very Short Introduction explores the nature of bacteria, their origin and evolution, bacteria in the environment, and bacteria and disease. Bacteria inhabit every part of the Earth's surface, its ocean depths, and even terrains such as boiling hot springs. They are most familiar as agents of disease, but benign bacteria are critical to the recycling of elements and all ecology, as well as to human health. The resistance of bacteria to antibiotics is also considered.
titles.summaryAI: Bacteria as the oldest and most prolific life forms on Earth, covering their biology, origin, evolution, and ecological roles. Examines bacterial habitation across all environments from ocean depths to boiling hot springs. Addresses bacteria as disease agents including tuberculosis, leprosy, and Escherichia coli infections, alongside their critical ecological functions in element recycling and maintaining environmental balance. Covers the immune system's response to bacterial infection. Explores the growing crisis of antibiotic resistance and its implications for treating infectious disease. Addresses beneficial bacteria essential to human health and broader ecosystem functioning.
highlights: Considers the important role of bacteria in the environment and its role in human civilisation
Explores the origins and evolution of bacteria over time
Looks at various types of bacteria and disease, and our efforts to manage bacteria
Examines the symbiotic relationship between bacteria and humans
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series
newToThisEdition: A new chapter on 'Microbiota and Microbiome in Man' examines the symbiotic relationship between bacteria and humans
Outlines the role of rapid bacterial diagnosis in controlling antibiotic resistance

titles.title: Symmetry
titles.author: Ian Stewart
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Symmetry: A Very Short Introduction provides an introduction to the formal theory of symmetry: group theory. Now a branch of abstract algebra, this subject first arose in the theory of equations. Symmetry is an immensely important concept in mathematics and throughout the sciences, and its applications range across the entire subject. Symmetry governs the structure of crystals, innumerable types of pattern formation, how systems change their state as parameters vary; and fundamental physics is governed by symmetries in the laws of nature. It is highly visual, with applications that include animal markings, locomotion, evolutionary biology, elastic buckling, waves, the shape of the Earth, and the form of galaxies.
titles.summaryAI: Group theory as the mathematical formalization of symmetry, originating in the theory of equations and now a branch of abstract algebra. Covers rotational symmetry, conjugacy classes, and connections to crystallography, pattern formation, and bifurcation theory. Applications span physics through symmetries in natural laws, biology through animal markings and locomotion, evolutionary biology, elastic buckling, wave phenomena, geomorphology including Earth's shape, and galactic structure. Bridges pure mathematics with physical sciences and life sciences through geometric and algebraic frameworks.
highlights: Explores one of the most important concepts in mathematics and science
Examines the role of symmetry in mathematics, physics, chemistry, and biology
Considers the scientific applications of symmetry
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series

titles.title: Marine biology
titles.author: Philip V. Mladenov
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: The oceans are our planet’s most distinctive and imposing natural habitat. Marine Biology: A Very Short Introduction explores the marine environment and the nature of life in the oceans, emphasizing their significance to our planet and human society. The new edition updates the discussion of the impacts of human activities on ocean life, including overfishing, plastic pollution, and ocean warming and acidification. It discusses the threats these pose to our welfare, and the actions required to put us on a path to a more sustainable relationship with our oceans so that they can be restored and protected for future generations. A new chapter offers an aspirational vision for the state of the Global Ocean in 2050.
titles.summaryAI: Ocean ecosystems as planetary habitats, covering marine organism diversity, ecological interactions, and biogeochemical processes including ocean circulation patterns such as the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation. Addresses human impacts on marine environments: overfishing, plastic pollution, ocean warming, and ocean acidification. Examines aquaculture as food production technology, sea ice dynamics, and sustainability frameworks for ocean resource management. Connects marine ecology to climate science, conservation policy, and projections for global ocean health through 2050.
highlights: Introduces the marine environment and the nature of life in the oceans, emphasizing their significance to our planet and human society
Analyses the impacts of human activities on ocean life and the actions required to restore ocean ecosystems
Includes a new final chapter providing an aspirational vision for the state of the Global Ocean in 2050
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The British Empire
titles.author: Ashley Jackson
titles.subject: History - U.K.
titles.abstract: The British Empire: A Very Short Introduction explores how the British Empire became so powerful and far-reaching. From the eighteenth century until the 1950s the British Empire was the biggest political entity in the world. What was the British Empire and what were its main constituent parts? How was the Empire ruled? What were its economic effects? What were the cultural implications of empire, in Britain and its colonies? These questions are answered in this VSI and the life of the people living under imperial rule is examined. The legacies of the Empire and how it should be viewed in world history are also explored.
titles.summaryAI: Rise, governance, and decline of the British Empire from the eighteenth century through the 1950s, encompassing its global territorial extent. Covers imperial administration, the role of the East India Company, and the slave trade. Examines economic effects of empire on colonies and metropole, cultural implications in both Britain and colonized territories, and the experiences of peoples under imperial rule. Addresses decolonization, postcolonial legacies including immigration debates, and the Empire's place in world historical interpretation.
highlights: Considers all aspects of the British Empire - military, social, cultural, and its legacy
Hugely important topic with modern-day resonance and influence
Explores how the British Empire became so powerful and far-reaching
Looks at how the Empire was ruled and the effects on the colonies
Examines the legacy and mark of rule it has left on the world today
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introduction series

titles.title: The Trojan War
titles.author: Eric H. Cline
titles.subject: History/Archaeology/Classical Studies
titles.abstract: The Trojan War: A Very Short Introduction investigates two major questions: did the Trojan War take place and, if so, where? This VSI suggests that, although a Trojan War (or wars) probably did take place, it was fought for economic and political reasons. This formed the nucleus for the story that was eventually recorded by Homer. In addition to Homer, this VSI examines other treatments of the story from classical times. It also surveys the archaeological attempts to document the Trojan War through excavations at Hissarlik, Turkey, especially the work of Heinrich Schliemann and his successors.
titles.summaryAI: Archaeological and literary investigation of the Trojan War, examining whether it occurred and its probable economic and political causes in the Late Bronze Age eastern Mediterranean. Covers Homer's Iliad as literary nucleus, Hittite interactions with Anatolia, and the Sea Peoples phenomenon. Traces archaeological excavations at Hissarlik, Turkey, from Heinrich Schliemann through Carl Blegen and successors. Surveys classical literary treatments beyond Homer and the war's cultural legacy through Alexander the Great and later reception.
highlights: Brings together all of the evidence available from archaeology, Hittite texts, and Greek legend to investigate the question of whether the Trojan War was a real historical event and whether the site of ancient Troy has been found
Challenges the assumption that Helen's abduction was the cause of the war
Offers a concise yet original perspective on a timeless epic of love and war, rivalry and greed, heroes and cowards

titles.title: Malthus
titles.author: Donald Winch
titles.subject: Sociology/Biography
titles.abstract: Thomas Robert Malthus was an English cleric whose ideas, as expounded in his most famous work the Essay on the Principle of Population, caused a storm of controversy. Malthus: A Very Short Introduction explains and clarifies Malthus's ideas, assessing the profound influence he has had on modern economic thought. Concentrating on his writings, it sheds light on the context in which he wrote and why his work has remained controversial. Looking at Malthus's early life as well as the evolution of his theories from population to political economy, it considers why and how Malthus's writings have been so influential in the thought of later figures such as Darwin and Keynes.
titles.summaryAI: Thomas Robert Malthus's intellectual biography and the development of his population theory within late eighteenth and early nineteenth-century English political economy. Covers the Essay on the Principle of Population, the law of diminishing returns, and the evolution of Malthus's thought toward broader political economy alongside contemporaries David Ricardo and John Stuart Mill. Examines his influence on Charles Darwin's natural selection theory and John Maynard Keynes's economics. Contextualizes Malthus within Enlightenment debates involving Condorcet and ongoing controversies about population, resources, and economic growth.
highlights: A short introduction to the work of Thomas Robert Malthus, an influential English cleric
Clearly explains Malthus's ideas and main theories
Assesses the profound influence Malthus has had on modern economic thought
Considers the impact he had on later theorists, such as Darwin and Keynes
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series

titles.title: Climate
titles.author: Mark Maslin
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: Climate: A Very Short Introduction considers all aspects of the global climate system, exploring and explaining the different components that control the climate on Earth. Climate affects everything we do in life, from the clothes we wear to the illnesses we might catch. How does energy reach the Earth? How does the ocean–atmosphere system redistribute energy around the plane? What is the relationship and what are the differences between climate and the weather? Climate affects life on Earth and human settlements in many different ways. Humans have to live their lives aware of the risk of cyclic and quasi-cyclic features of climate such as the Milankovitch cycles and El Nino.
titles.summaryAI: Earth's climate system components and their interactions, including atmospheric dynamics, ocean-atmosphere energy redistribution, and the carbon dioxide greenhouse effect. Distinguishes climate from weather. Covers Milankovitch orbital cycles, El Nino oscillations, and quasi-cyclic climate variability. Addresses global warming, the Greenland Ice Sheet, and the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change assessments. Examines climate's influence on human settlement patterns, agriculture, health, and extreme weather events. Connects atmospheric science with oceanography, solar energy input, and the Gaia hypothesis.
highlights: A wide-ranging look at all aspects of the global climate system
Discusses the relationship and differences between climate and weather
Considers how climate has affected human settlements and life on Earth
Part of the Very Short Introductions series – over ten million copies sold worldwide
newToThisEdition: Updated information on the science of climate, weather, and climate change, including recent examples of extreme weather events

titles.title: The Palestinian-Israeli Conflict
titles.author: Martin Bunton
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: The Palestinian–Israeli Conflict: A Very Short Introduction explains the history of this conflict, reducing it to its very essence — a modern territorial contest between two nations and one geographical territory. It is one of the most highly publicized and bitter struggles in history. Over the last 120 years the conflict has had many facets, most tangibly the sharing of land. It is historically very complex involving changes in relationships among people and events. What significance have the 1897 Basle Congress, the 1917 Balfour Declaration and British occupation of Palestine, and the 1947 UN Partition Plan and the war for Palestine had on the conflict as a whole? How and why has partition been so difficult? How do efforts to restore peace continue today?
titles.summaryAI: Modern territorial conflict between Palestinian and Israeli national movements over a single geographic territory spanning approximately 120 years. Covers the 1897 Basle Congress, Zionism under Theodor Herzl, the 1917 Balfour Declaration, British Mandate governance of Palestine, and the 1947 UN Partition Plan. Examines subsequent wars, the status of the Gaza Strip and West Bank, International Court of Justice rulings, and ongoing peace efforts. Addresses the difficulty of partition and the interplay of nationalism, diplomacy, and sovereignty.
highlights: Explains one of the most publicized, but little understood, modern conflicts
Chapters are arranged by successive decades - to highlight the complex nature of the conflict over time
Gets to the heart of the problem - a struggle between two nations over land
Examines the historical background to the problem through to the present day
Clearly explains why partition has been such a problem and what is being done to try and resolve it
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series

titles.title: Happiness
titles.author: Daniel M. Haybron
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Happiness: A Very Short Introduction considers the true nature of happiness and the current thinking on the subject, from psychology to philosophy. What is happiness? Is it subjective or can we put an objective value on it? How can and should we pursue happiness? Happiness is an everyday term in our lives, and most of us strive to be happy, but defining happiness can be difficult. There are many diverse routes to happiness. How much of our view on what constitutes happiness is influenced by secular Western ideas on the contemporary pursuit of a good life?
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and psychological perspectives on happiness, examining subjective versus objective conceptions and their measurement. Covers major philosophical theories from John Stuart Mill and Ludwig Wittgenstein, alongside psychological research on personality, depression, and emotional well-being. Explores the relationship between happiness, meaning, love, and attention. Addresses genetic influences on subjective well-being, the pursuit of the good life, and secular Western cultural assumptions about happiness. Considers ethical dimensions including suicide and genetic engineering in relation to human flourishing.
highlights: Considers our definitions of happiness, and its importance
Provides an overview of contemporary and historical thinking about happiness
Looks at both the philosophical and psychological aspects of happiness
Considers not just what happiness is, and what brings it, but how happiness fits into an ethical life
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series

titles.title: Diaspora
titles.author: Kevin Kenny
titles.subject: Sociology/History/Politics
titles.abstract: Diaspora: A Very Short Introduction examines the origins of diaspora as a concept, its changing meanings over time, its current popularity, and its strengths and limitations as an explanatory device. The book proposes a flexible approach to diaspora that can provide insights into the motives for migration; the networks through which migrants travel; the political, economic, and cultural connections they form among themselves, with their homelands, and with fellow diasporas in other locations around the world; the idea of return to a homeland; and recent developments concerning refugees and globalization. Diaspora emerges as a concept that helps people to make sense of the experience of migration.
titles.summaryAI: Diaspora as an analytical concept: its origins, evolving meanings, and applications for understanding migration, displacement, and transnational identity. Covers motivations for migration, networks connecting dispersed communities, and political, economic, and cultural ties maintained with homelands. Examines the intersections of diaspora with nationalism, citizenship, race, caste, slavery, and genocide. Addresses contemporary developments in refugee movements and globalization. Connects migration studies with questions of identity formation, community belonging, and cross-cultural exchange across multiple geographic locations.
highlights: Explores a concept that helps migrants, scholars, and social commentators alike to make sense of the experience of migration
Examines the origins of diaspora as a concept, its changing meanings over time, its current popularity, and its strengths and limitations as an explanatory device
Proposes a flexible approach to diaspora that can provide insights into many dimensions of the term

titles.title: Contemporary Fiction
titles.author: Robert Eaglestone
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Contemporary Fiction: A Very Short Introduction explores a wide and diverse field, now global in dimension, with an enormous range of novels and writers that continues to grow at a fantastic speed. This VSI provides an exploration of the major themes, patterns, and debates of contemporary fiction. From genre, form, and experimentalism to the legacies of modernism and postmodernism, the relationship between globalization and terrorism, and the impact of technology, it examines how works both reflect the world in which we live and the artistic concerns of writers and readers alike.
titles.summaryAI: Major themes, forms, and debates in contemporary global fiction from the late twentieth century onward. Covers genre conventions, literary experimentalism, and the legacies of modernism and postmodernism in the novel. Examines relationships between fiction and globalization, terrorism, and digital technology. Addresses historical fiction, the Booker Prize as a cultural institution, and the expanding global dimensions of literary production. Explores how narrative forms reflect contemporary social realities while engaging with evolving artistic concerns of writers and readers.
highlights: A must-read for all students of A level and undergraduate English Literature courses
Wide appeal for the general reader in reading groups
Considers the impact of technology on contemporary fiction
Explores the changing nature of fiction in a globalized world

titles.title: Modern War
titles.author: Richard English
titles.subject: Military
titles.abstract: Modern War: A Very Short Introduction draws on a variety of areas including history, political science, economics, theology, and psychology, explores the assumptions we make about modern warfare and considers them against the backdrop of their historical reality. Warfare is the most dangerous threat faced by modern humanity. It is also one of the key influences that has shaped the politics, economics, and society of the modern period. But what do we mean by modern war? What causes modern wars to begin? Why do people fight in them, why do they end, and what have they achieved?
titles.summaryAI: Nature, causes, conduct, and consequences of warfare from the modern period to the present, drawing on history, political science, economics, theology, and psychology. Covers chemical warfare, nuclear proliferation, terrorism, and guerrilla tactics. Examines specific conflicts including the Spanish Civil War, World War II under Churchill, the Cold War, and the Falklands War. Analyzes why wars begin, why combatants fight, how conflicts end, and their political, economic, and social outcomes. Addresses the relationship between military strategy and state formation.
highlights: consider how it can be misunderstood or misrepresented
Looks at definition of modern war and how it has evolved
Considers the impact of modern warfare, including its social and cultural legacy

titles.title: The Beats
titles.author: David Sterritt
titles.subject: Literature/History
titles.abstract: The Beats: A Very Short Introduction offers an overview of the social, cultural, and aesthetic sensibilities of the Beats, bringing out the similarities that connected them, and also the many differences. In the late 1950s and early 1960s, the Beat Generation writers revolutionized American literature with their iconoclastic approach to language and their angry assault on the conformity and conservatism of postwar society. They and their followers took aim at the hypocrisy and taboos of their time. Members of the Beat Generation hoped to inspire others to purify their lives and souls, yet they urged the remaking of consciousness on a profoundly inward-looking basis. The idea was to revolutionize society by revolutionizing thought, not the other way around.
titles.summaryAI: Beat Generation literary movement of the late 1950s and early 1960s in American culture, encompassing writers Jack Kerouac, Lawrence Ferlinghetti, Diane di Prima, and John Clellon Holmes. Covers their iconoclastic literary innovations, rejection of postwar conformity and conservatism, and connections to jazz culture. Examines the philosophical emphasis on consciousness transformation, spiritual purification, and inward revolution rather than political activism. Addresses the movement's social sensibilities, its challenge to cultural taboos, and its lasting influence on American popular culture and literature.
highlights: Takes advantage of the latest historical research
Relates the Beats' writing to twentieth-century music, film, and painting
Explores the Beats within the larger context of American culture and history
Examines interplay between Beat aesthetics and the political outlook of their day

titles.title: Sociolinguistics
titles.author: John Edwards
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Sociolinguistics: A Very Short Introduction deals with the social life of language: language in its sociocultural context. It draws from sociolinguistics, the sociology of language, and psycholinguistics. It explains the differential social evaluations of languages and dialects, how names (and naming) are much more than simple designations, and why some languages come to dominate others. It also explores the relationship between language and gender, sexist language, the language of poverty, the intertwining of language and religion, and politically driven language planning and policy. It demonstrates the connections and continuities that exist within the language arena in which we all participate.
titles.summaryAI: Language in its sociocultural context, drawing on sociolinguistics, sociology of language, and psycholinguistics. Covers social evaluations of languages and dialects, language dominance and shift, and the significance of naming practices. Examines relationships between language and gender, sexist language, the language of poverty, and the intertwining of language and religion. Addresses language planning and policy as politically driven processes. Traces historical linguistics from Franz Bopp and Jacob Grimm through Edward Sapir and Ludwig Wittgenstein, connecting communication systems to social stratification and cultural identity.
highlights: Acquaints the educated non-specialist with the social life of language

Offers the most up-to-date brief overview of sociolinguistics, in a nuanced approach which considers perspectives both historical and contemporary, "micro" and "macro," and the ways in which these intertwine
Addresses the social and political dynamics of names and naming, as well as questions about the relationship between language, gender, and religion

titles.title: Food
titles.author: John Krebs
titles.subject: Biology/Science
titles.abstract: Food: A Very Short Introduction provides a brief history of man's relationship with food, spanning from the time of our remote ancestors 3 million years ago to the present day. Food has been a topic of great interest for humanity across cultures and over generations. Why do some people like some kinds of foods and not others? How do our senses contribute to flavour? How important are genetics in our likes and dislikes? We are all affected by issues such as diet, nutrition, and health; the disparity between malnutrition in some places and overconsumption in others; obesity; sustainable agriculture; and genetic modification. How will it be possible to feed a population of 9 billion in 2050, without destroying our natural environment?
titles.summaryAI: Human relationship with food from prehistoric ancestors three million years ago to present-day challenges. Covers sensory perception of flavor, genetic influences on food preferences, and the science of diet and nutrition. Examines malnutrition, overconsumption, obesity, and food poisoning as public health issues. Addresses agricultural technology including sustainable farming and genetic modification of crops. Explores the challenge of feeding nine billion people by 2050 without environmental destruction, connecting evolutionary biology, food science, agricultural production, and global health policy.
highlights: Includes a brief history of human food and examines the four great transitions of food development
Considers the various biological senses involved in food
Looks at the reasons why people like some food and not others - including the genetic influences and role of learning and culture
Separates myth from the fact about food safety
Considers the reasons for the obesity epidemic and what can be done about it
Explores the future of food security in an ever-growing worldwide population

titles.title: Fractals
titles.author: Kenneth Falconer
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Fractals: A Very Short Introduction looks at the roots of the ‘fractal revolution’ that occurred in mathematics in the 20th century. It presents the ‘new geometry’ of fractals, explains the basic concepts, and explores the wide range of applications in science, and in aspects of economics. Many are familiar with the beauty and ubiquity of fractal forms within nature. Unlike the study of smooth forms, fractal geometry describes more familiar shapes and patterns, such as the complex contours of coastlines, the outlines of clouds, and the branching of trees. This is essential introductory reading for students of mathematics and science, and those interested in popular science and mathematics.
titles.summaryAI: Fractal geometry as a twentieth-century mathematical revolution pioneered by Benoit Mandelbrot, covering fractal dimension, self-similarity, and iterative constructions such as the Koch curve and Mandelbrot set. Contrasts fractal geometry with classical smooth-form mathematics. Explores applications in modeling natural phenomena including coastlines, clouds, and branching patterns. Addresses connections to Brownian motion, power laws, and economic modeling. Bridges pure mathematics with physics, earth sciences, biology, and economics through geometric description of irregular and complex natural forms.
highlights: An essential topic of study for mathematics students
Looks at the historical roots of fractal theory
Explains the basic concepts of fractal geometry with the minimum of mathematics
Helps explain the concepts by illustrating a number of different fractals
Examines a wide range of the applications across the sciences, and in aspects of economics

titles.title: Management
titles.author: John Hendry
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Management: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of management theory and modern practice, considers management in a social and cultural context, and explores issues of morality and accountability. Tracing the development of management over the last century, it looks not only at what managers do, but also provides an insight into modern management theory. It considers the influences of national and organizational culture, the relationship between power and domination, and management in different cultures. It also looks at approaches to management, accountability of managers, and morality.
titles.summaryAI: History and theory of management from the early twentieth century to the present, covering organizational behavior, motivation, job satisfaction, and leadership. Examines contributions of Elton Mayo, Kurt Lewin, Rosabeth Moss Kanter, and Michael Porter. Addresses management in its social and cultural context, including influences of national and organizational culture on management practices. Explores power dynamics and domination within organizations, cross-cultural management differences, accountability of managers, and moral dimensions of managerial decision-making. Connects business administration to sociology and ethics.
highlights: An authoritative guide to the main principles of management
Looks at the history of management theory and modern practice
Considers management in a social and cultural context
Explores the issues of morality and accountability

titles.title: International security
titles.author: Christopher S. Browning
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: International Security: A Very Short Introduction shows that the subjects of war and peace, military strategy, the proliferation of nuclear weapons, and revisionist states remain central to the subject of international security, but burgeoning concerns such as climate change, migration, poverty, health, and international terrorism have complicated the field. So what really matters? The traditional prioritization of state security or the security needs of individuals, humanity, and the biosphere? Are states themselves as much a part of the problem as the solution for people's security needs? It outlines the nature of the key debates about contemporary international security challenges, and discusses the inherent difficulties that exist in tackling them.
titles.summaryAI: Contemporary international security encompassing traditional military concerns and emerging nontraditional threats. Covers war and peace, nuclear weapons proliferation, state security versus human security, and the roles of NATO, the European Union, and the United Nations. Examines the Cold War legacy, the Iraq War, and terrorism alongside climate change, migration, poverty, and global health as security challenges. Debates whether states are solutions or sources of insecurity. Addresses the politics of fear and tensions between state-centric and individual-centric security frameworks in international relations.
highlights: Uses a broad range of international examples to illustrate concepts
Focuses on contemporary security developments and arguments
Highlights the politically contested and ethical nature of all the international security debates

titles.title: Astrobiology
titles.author: David C. Catling
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: What fascinates people about astrobiology is that it seeks answers to long-standing unsolved questions: How quickly did life evolve on Earth and why did life persist here? Is there life elsewhere in the Solar System or beyond? Astrobiology: A Very Short Introduction explores some of the big unanswered questions about the universe, considers the origins of life on Earth and its evolution, and brings together the ideas of microbiologists, astronomers, planetary scientists, and geologists. It introduces the origins of astrobiology and demonstrates its impact on current astronomical research and potential future discoveries.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary study of life's origins and potential distribution in the universe, integrating microbiology, astronomy, planetary science, and geology. Covers the origin of life on Earth, early evolution, and conditions enabling life's persistence including plate tectonics, atmospheric oxygen, and the greenhouse effect. Examines the search for life elsewhere in the solar system and beyond, applying principles of natural selection and environmental adaptation. Addresses water as a prerequisite for life, the role of the Moon in Earth's habitability, and the co-evolution of life and planetary environments.
highlights: Explores some of the big unanswered questions about the universe
Considers the origins of life on Earth and its evolution, to understand the possibility of life elsewhere
Brings together the ideas of microbiologists, astronomers, planetary scientists, and geologists
Looks at the origins of astrobiology and the discoveries it is making
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over six million sold worldwide

titles.title: Causation
titles.author: Stephen Mumford, Rani Lill Anjum
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Causation is the most fundamental connection in the universe. Without it, there would be no science or technology. There would be no moral responsibility either, as none of our thoughts would be connected with our actions and none of our actions with any consequences. Any intervention we make in the world around us is premised on there being causal connections that are, to a degree, predictable. It is causation that is at the basis of prediction and also explanation. Causation: A Very Short Introduction introduces the key theories of causation and also the surrounding debates and controversies.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical analysis of causation as a fundamental metaphysical concept, covering major theories from Aristotle through modern approaches. Examines constant conjunction accounts, counterfactual theories, and debates between necessity and contingency in causal relations. Addresses causation's role in scientific explanation, prediction, moral responsibility, and technological intervention. Explores connections to physicalism, pluralism, and theories of space and time. Bridges metaphysics with philosophy of science, ethics, and epistemology through analysis of how causal connections underpin knowledge, agency, and rational action.
highlights: Thought-provoking introduction to a core topic in philosophy
An engaging introduction to the key theories of causation and the surrounding debates and controversies
Explores the integral part causation plays in a number of different areas, including physics, biology, law, technology, medicine, and economics
Explains the key issues through a series of questions relating to the big issues
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over six million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Entrepreneurship
titles.author: Paul Westhead, Mike Wright
titles.subject: Business
titles.abstract: Entrepreneurship: A Very Short Introduction navigates the debates about entrepreneurship, providing a guide to the entrepreneurial process. There has been an explosion of interest in entrepreneurs in the popular media, as well as in business, policy, and education. The actions of entrepreneurs have always been shaped by the external environment and the availability of resources. They can be found in all sorts of organizations and they come from a diversity of backgrounds. Entrepreneurs have a significant impact on modern market economies and there is a certain future for entrepreneurship in our increasingly globalized world. But what do entrepreneurs do? What is entrepreneurship and why is it important? What is distinctive about entrepreneurs? And where do they come from?
titles.summaryAI: Entrepreneurial processes, behaviors, and impacts on modern market economies. Covers definitions of entrepreneurship, characteristics of entrepreneurs, and the role of human capital, education, and environmental factors in venture creation. Examines entrepreneurship across diverse organizational contexts and demographic backgrounds. Addresses the relationship between entrepreneurship and economic development, technological innovation, health sector ventures, and resource availability. Connects individual enterprise to broader questions of globalization, market dynamics, and policy frameworks supporting entrepreneurial ecosystems.
highlights: A guide to what entrepreneurship is and why it is important
Considers the factors that affect the entrepreneurial process
Looks at the array of organizational modes in which entrepreneurship takes place
Explores the role entrepreneurship plays in a successful market economy
Part of the Very Short Introduction series - over six million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Tibetan Buddhism
titles.author: Matthew T. Kapstein
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Tibetan Buddhism: A Very Short Introduction provides the essential features of Tibetan Buddhist history, teachings, and practice. The Tibetan Buddhist tradition has known over thirteen centuries of continuous development. During that time, it has spread among the neighbouring peoples – the Mongol, Himalayan, and Siberian peoples, Manchus and Chinese – and is now also practised in Europe and America. This VSI considers what this religion teaches; the position of the religious leader, the Dalai Lama, and how his succession will be assured; and political contestation and suppression of Tibetan Buddhism in China.
titles.summaryAI: Thirteen centuries of Tibetan Buddhist development, covering core teachings including karma, rebirth, the bodhisattva ideal, Tantra, and the role of the guru. Examines the institution of the Dalai Lama and Panchen Lama, including succession mechanisms and the figure of Avalokitesvara. Traces the spread of Tibetan Buddhism among Mongol, Himalayan, Siberian, Manchu, and Chinese peoples, and its modern presence in Europe and America. Addresses political contestation, Chinese suppression, and the Enlightenment tradition within Tibetan religious practice.
highlights: Provides an objective overview of the history and doctrines of the Tibetan Buddhist tradition
Looks into what the position of the Dalai Lama is
Explores how Tibetan Buddhism has spread across the world
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over six million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Ancient Near East
titles.author: Amanda H. Podany
titles.subject: History/Archaeology
titles.abstract: The Ancient Near East: A Very Short Introduction describes a momentous time in human history and explains why the ancient Near East is known as the ‘cradle of civilization’. Mesopotamia, Syria, and Anatolia were home to an extraordinarily rich and successful culture. Indeed, it was a time and place of earth-shaking changes for humankind: the beginnings of writing and law, kingship and bureaucracy, diplomacy and state-sponsored warfare, mathematics and literature. The three thousand years of this era — from around 3500 bce, with the founding of the first Mesopotamian cities, to the conquest of the Near East by the Persian king Cyrus the Great in 539 bce — represent a period of incredible innovation, from the invention of the wheel to early achievements in astronomy, law, and diplomacy.
titles.summaryAI: Mesopotamian, Syrian, and Anatolian civilizations from approximately 3500 to 539 BCE, covering the founding of the first cities through the Persian conquest under Cyrus the Great. Examines the invention of writing, codified law, kingship, bureaucracy, diplomacy, and state warfare. Covers Assyrian and Babylonian empires, temple institutions, priestly roles, religious festivals, and the status of women. Addresses archaeological evidence and innovations including the wheel, early astronomy, mathematics, and literature. Contextualizes the region as the foundational cradle of civilization.
highlights: Explores an area of ancient history not well covered for general readers
Illuminates the influential role of gods in the personal lives of the region's inhabitants during the period discussed
Closely examines cuneiform documents such as personal letters, treaties, a contract, and a hymn
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introduction series - over six million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: American Legal History
