It explores the major developments of modern drama, covering two decades per chapter, from early modernist theatre through post-war developments to more recent and contemporary theatre. The emergence of new theories from the likes of Brecht and Beckett are tracked alongside groundbreaking productions to illuminate the fascinating evolution of modern drama.
titles.summaryAI: Development of theatrical drama from 1880 to the present as an international phenomenon. Covers naturalism, realism, symbolism, and metatheatre as evolving dramatic modes. Examines major theorists and practitioners including Brecht and Beckett and their influence on both text and performance. Addresses the relationship between audience experience and theatrical innovation, groundbreaking productions across decades, and the emergence of new dramatic forms. Traces the evolution from early modernist experimentation through post-war developments to contemporary theatre, highlighting drama's diversity in global contexts.
highlights: Traces the development and evolution of modern drama since 1880
Considers drama from a diverse range of cultures and societies
Emphasizes the dual nature of drama, exploring how it behaves on both the page and the stage

Covers the works of key playwrights including Ibsen, Strindberg, Chekhov, Shaw, Wilde, Glaspell, O'Neill, Osborne, Delaney, Hansberry, Williams, Miller, Baraka, and Churchill, and considers how they share themes of hostility and contrarianism
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Mexican Revolution
titles.author: Alan Knight
titles.subject: History - Mexico
titles.abstract: The Mexican Revolution defined the socio-political experience of 20th-century life in Mexico. Its subsequent legacy has provoked debate between those who interpret the ongoing myth of the Revolution and those who adopt the more middle-of-the-road reality of the regime after 1940. The Mexican Revolution: A Very Short Introduction addresses the causes of the upheaval, outlines the armed conflict between 1910 and 1920, explains how a durable regime was consolidated in the 1920s, and sums up the social reforms of the Revolution, culminating in the radical 1930s. The conflict is placed alongside other ‘great’ revolutions, and Mexico is compared with the Latin American countries that avoided violent upheaval.
titles.summaryAI: Mexican Revolution from its causes through armed conflict between 1910 and 1920, regime consolidation in the 1920s, and radical social reforms of the 1930s. Covers key figures including Pancho Villa, constitutionalist movements, and military dynamics. Examines the revolution's socio-political legacy for twentieth-century Mexico and ongoing interpretive debates about its mythology versus post-1940 regime reality. Compares the Mexican Revolution with other major revolutions and contrasts Mexico's experience with Latin American countries that avoided violent upheaval. Addresses army, government, and depression-era impacts.
highlights: Traces the Mexican Revolution from the origins of conflict to the ensuing social reforms
Offers a reliable account of events, based on a mass of carefully considered evidence and sources
Accounts for a broad range of interpretations
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Founding Fathers
titles.author: R. B. Bernstein
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: The Founding Fathers: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of the brilliant, flawed, and quarrelsome group of lawyers, politicians, merchants, military men, and clergy known as “the Founding Fathers,” what they did, and what history has made of them. It traces the dynamic forces that molded Washington, Franklin, Jefferson, Adams, Hamilton, and their contemporaries as British colonists in North America and as intellectual citizens of the Atlantic civilization’s Age of Enlightenment. It analyzes the American Revolution, the framing and adoption of state and federal constitutions, and the key concepts and problems that both shaped and circumscribed the founders’ achievements as the United States sought its place in the world.
titles.summaryAI: Collective biography and political analysis of the American Founding Fathers including Washington, Franklin, Jefferson, Adams, Hamilton, Jay, and Madison. Examines their formation as British colonists and intellectual participants in the Atlantic Enlightenment. Covers the Declaration of Independence, the American Revolution, and the framing and adoption of state and federal constitutions. Addresses key constitutional concepts, political quarrels among the founders, and the early republic's efforts to establish its place internationally. Explores how subsequent generations have reinterpreted and mythologized these figures.
highlights: Contemporary relevance: In an era when interpreters of the Constitution refer to themselves as "strict constructionists," we need to know who the Founding Fathers actually were, what they believed, and what they meant
Provides important context for understanding the founding fathers, as well as their impact on the present day

titles.title: Hollywood
titles.author: Peter Decherney
titles.subject: Cinema
titles.abstract: Hollywood: A Very Short Introduction tells the story of Hollywood, from its nineteenth-century origins to the emergence of internet media empires. It recounts how the studio system rose out of the ashes of Thomas Edison’s trust to create the handful of companies that have dominated global screens and imaginations for over 100 years. It reveals that the elements taken to be a natural part of the Hollywood experience—stars, genre-driven storytelling, blockbuster franchises—are the product of cultural, political, and commercial forces. The challenges of new technologies, including sound, home video, and computer graphics, are explored and Hollywood’s responses to World War II, independent film movements, and regulations imposed by Washington are also examined.
titles.summaryAI: History of the Hollywood film industry from nineteenth-century origins through Edison's trust era to modern internet media empires. Covers the rise of the studio system, star culture, genre-driven storytelling, and blockbuster franchise models. Examines technological transitions including sound, home video, and computer graphics. Addresses Hollywood's responses to World War II, government regulation from Washington, and independent film movements. Analyzes how cultural, political, and commercial forces shaped elements perceived as natural features of the Hollywood experience and its dominance of global screens.
highlights: Brings all of Hollywood history together in a short, readable text
Covers technological innovation from pre-film technology through the internet
Explains how Hollywood works, including the star system, the genre system, the formation of the studio system, and the impact of new technologies
Puts YouTube, Netflix, and the internet in the context of earlier Hollywood history
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - millions of copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Goethe
titles.author: Ritchie Robertson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Goethe: A Very Short Introduction covers the life and work of Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (1749–1832): scientist, administrator, artist, art critic, and supreme literary writer in a vast variety of genres. Looking at Goethe’s poetry, novels, and drama pieces, as well as his travel writing, autobiography, and essays on art and aesthetics, it analyses some of the key themes in his works: love, nature, religion, and tragedy. Dispelling the misconception of Goethe as a sedate Victorian sage, it shows how much of his art was rooted in turbulent personal conflicts, and draws on recent research to present a complete portrait of the scientific work and political activity that accompanied Goethe’s writings.
titles.summaryAI: Life and works of Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (1749-1832) spanning poetry, novels, drama, travel writing, autobiography, and essays on aesthetics. Examines central themes of love, nature, religion, and tragedy across his literary output. Addresses Goethe's scientific research, political activity as an administrator, and art criticism. Challenges the perception of Goethe as a sedate sage by revealing the turbulent personal conflicts underlying his art. Discusses his influence on German literature and later thinkers including Nietzsche and Thomas Mann and his significance within European literary and intellectual culture.
highlights: Covers the life of Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (1749-1832), and all his major works
Discusses the (often controversial) research which has changed the image of Goethe in the last few decades
Places Goethe in his historical context (the Holy Roman Empire; the Napoleonic Wars), and considers how this aids our understanding of his works
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Medieval Philosophy
titles.author: John Marenbon
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Medieval philosophy is one of the most exciting and diversified periods in the history of thought. Introducing the coexisting strands of Christian, Muslim, and Jewish philosophy, Medieval Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction shows how these traditions all go back to the Platonic schools of late antiquity and explains the complex ways in which they are interlinked. Providing an overview of some of the main thinkers, such as Boethius, Abelard, al-Fārābī, Avicenna, Maimonides, and Gersonides, and the topics, institutions, and literary forms of medieval philosophy, it discusses in detail some of the key issues in medieval thought: universals; mind, body, and mortality; foreknowledge and freedom; and society and the best life.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy of the medieval period encompassing interlinked Christian, Muslim, and Jewish intellectual traditions rooted in late antique Platonic schools. Covers major thinkers including Boethius, Abelard, al-Farabi, Avicenna, Maimonides, Alexander of Hales, William of Ockham, and Gersonides. Examines key philosophical problems including universals, mind-body relations, mortality, divine foreknowledge and human freedom, and political philosophy concerning society and the best life. Addresses the distinctive institutions and literary forms through which medieval philosophical thought was produced and transmitted.
highlights: Gives a comprehensive overview of the many traditions of medieval philosophy, over the full chronological range of the period
Discusses the importance of the religious and historical contexts of medieval philosophy
Shows how the philosophical traditions in the Middle Ages all stem from the Platonic schools of late antiquity and explains their evolution

Looks at key phlosophical figures of the time such as the Latin writers Boethius, Abelard, Duns Scotus, Dante and Pomponazzi; the Arabic writers al-Fârâbî, Avicenna, ibn Tufayl, Averroes and Maimonides, and Gersonides, who wrote in Hebrew
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Earth System Science
titles.author: Tim Lenton
titles.subject: Science
titles.abstract: The concept of the Earth’s atmosphere, biosphere, oceans, soil, and rocks operating as a closely interacting system has rapidly gained ground in science. Earth System Science: A Very Short Introduction considers how a world in which humans could evolve arose; how, as a species, we are now reshaping that world; and what a sustainable future for humanity within the Earth system might look like. Drawing on elements of geology, biology, chemistry, physics, and mathematics, it asks whether Earth system science can help guide us on to a sustainable course before we alter the Earth system to the point where we destroy ourselves and our current civilization.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary study of Earth as an integrated system of atmosphere, biosphere, oceans, soil, and rocks. Draws on geology, biology, chemistry, physics, and mathematics to examine how habitable conditions emerged and how human activity now reshapes planetary systems. Covers the Gaia hypothesis, carbon dioxide cycling, greenhouse effects, climate sensitivity, snowball Earth events, solar energy inputs, and boreal forest dynamics. Addresses the concept of sustainability, the risks of anthropogenic disruption to Earth systems, and prospects for maintaining a viable human civilization within planetary boundaries.
highlights: Introduces Earth System Science as an important and growing interdisciplinary area of research
Traces how Earth System Science has emerged as a field in its own right since the 1960s alongside concerns about global change
Outlines the tipping points that are currently recognised in the Earth system
Places the Earth system in wider context, including the latest insights into other habitable planets
Explains how understanding the fragility of the Earth system and its past history can help humanity achieve sustainability
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Slang
titles.author: Jonathon Green
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Slang, however one judges it, shows us at our most human. It is used widely and often, typically associated with the writers of noir fiction, teenagers, and rappers, but also found in the works of Shakespeare and Dickens. It has been recorded since at least 1500 AD, and today’s vocabulary, taken from every major English-speaking country, runs to over 125,000 slang words and phrases. Slang: A Very Short Introduction explores this fascinating subset of the English language. It considers the meaning and origins of the word ‘slang’ itself, the ideas that make a word ‘slang’, the long-running themes that run through slang, and the history of slang’s many dictionaries.
titles.summaryAI: Informal, nonstandard vocabulary in the English language, documented since at least 1500 AD, encompassing over 125,000 words and phrases across all major English-speaking countries. Covers the linguistic definition and boundaries of slang, distinguishing it from dialect, jargon, and colloquialism. Examines recurring thematic patterns in slang vocabulary, the social functions of code-switching and register variation, and the lexicographic history of slang dictionaries. Connects to sociolinguistics, cultural identity, and the evolution of vernacular speech communities.
highlights: Approaches the words of slang thematically, considering their various users and settings
Discusses the lexicography of slang and traces the 500 year history of slang dictionaries
Considers the future of slang in light of the digital revolution
Part of the best selling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Computer Science
titles.author: Subrata Dasgupta
titles.subject: Computing/Mathematics/science
titles.abstract: Over the past sixty years, there has been spectacular growth of the technologies associated with the computer. Yet, the science underpinning this technology is less visible and little understood outside the professional computer science community. As a scientific discipline, computer science stands alongside the likes of molecular biology and cognitive science as one of the most significant new sciences of the post-Second World War era. Computer Science: A Very Short Introduction considers the conceptual basis and core principles of computer science. Discussing algorithms, programming, and sequential and parallel processing, it considers emerging modern ideas such as biological computing and cognitive modelling, challenging the idea of computer science as a science of the artificial.
titles.summaryAI: Foundational principles of computer science as a post-World War II scientific discipline. Covers algorithms, programming, computational complexity, sequential and parallel processing, and the Turing machine model of computation. Examines the conceptual contributions of Babbage, Lovelace, and Turing. Addresses emerging paradigms including artificial intelligence, biological computing, and cognitive modelling. Distinguishes computer science from mere technology by framing it as a formal science of computation with connections to logic, mathematics, and cognitive science.
highlights: Introduces the essential idea of automatic computation in the form of the Turing machine
Considers the core principles of computer science
Traces the important conceptual developments in computer science over the course of the past century
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Shakespeare's Comedies
titles.author: Bart van Es
titles.subject: Literature
titles.abstract: Shakespeare’s Comedies: A Very Short Introduction explores the full range of the playwright’s comic writing over the course of a career spanning nearly a quarter century of theatrical change, from the neat classical plotting of early works like The Comedy of Errors to the corrupt world of the so-called problem plays, written in Shakespeare’s middle years of life. It examines Shakespeare’s influences and sources; compares his plays to those of his rivals; identifies the key qualities that make Shakespearean comedy distinctive; and looks at the history of the plays in performance, from the biographies of Shakespeare’s original actors to the plays’ endless reinvention in modern stage productions and in films.
titles.summaryAI: William Shakespeare's comic dramatic works spanning his career from the 1590s through the early 1600s, a period of significant theatrical evolution in England. Covers early classically structured comedies, mature romantic comedies, and later problem plays. Examines literary sources and influences, comparisons with rival playwrights including Jonson, Chapman, and Dekker, and distinctive qualities of Shakespearean comedy. Traces performance history from original actors like Robert Armin through modern stage and film adaptations, connecting dramatic literature to theatrical practice and cultural reinvention.
highlights: Identifies the five distinctive qualities of Shakespearean comedy, and makes connections across all of Shakespeare's comic plays, including so-called 'romances' such as The Tempest or The Winter's Tale
Analyses Shakespeare's comedic plays in the context of their time
Explains theories of comedy from Aristotle to Freud and Bergson
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Welfare State
titles.author: David Garland
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: The programmes that make up the welfare state vary from nation to nation and from time to time, and the balance between markets and government, and free enterprise and social protection, is perennially in question. In contemporary political debate the welfare state seems mostly to be viewed as a problem rather than a solution, and welfare programmes appear constantly on the defensive. The Welfare State: A Very Short Introduction describes the modern welfare state, explaining its historical and contemporary significance and arguing that far from being ‘a failure’ or ‘a problem’, welfare states are an essential element of contemporary capitalism, and a vital concomitant of democratic government.
titles.summaryAI: Modern welfare state systems encompassing social insurance, public services, healthcare, and unemployment protections across different nations. Covers historical development from the New Deal era onward, examining variation in welfare programs between countries and over time. Analyzes the relationship between welfare states and democratic capitalism, the balance between free enterprise and social protection, and contemporary political debates about privatization and reform. Connects social policy to labor markets, economic systems, and government functions, arguing welfare states are essential structural components of modern capitalist democracies.
highlights: Offers a succinct account of the social and political issues surrounding the welfare state
Provides a new basis for thinking about a familiar set of institutions
Discusses the welfare state as a whole, setting it in its historical and sociological context
Places US and UK institutions against the comparative backdrop of European and Nordic welfare regimes
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Crystallography
titles.author: A. M. Glazer
titles.subject: Chemistry
titles.abstract: Crystals have fascinated us for centuries with their beauty and symmetry, and have often been invested with magical powers. The development of X-ray diffraction heralded the scientific study of crystals, leading to an understanding of their atomic arrangements at a fundamental level. The new discipline—X-ray crystallography—has subsequently evolved into a formidable science that underpins many other scientific areas. Crystallography: A Very Short Introduction traces the history of crystallography and shows how the discoveries in this field have been applied to the creation of new and important materials, to drugs and pharmaceuticals, and to our understanding of genetics, cell biology, proteins, and viruses.
titles.summaryAI: Scientific study of crystal structures through X-ray, electron, and neutron diffraction techniques. Covers the history of crystallography from aesthetic fascination with crystal symmetry to the development of X-ray diffraction methods. Examines Bravais lattices, symmetry operations, Fourier transforms, and atomic arrangement determination. Explores applications in pharmaceuticals and drug design, materials science, genetics, cell biology, protein structure determination, and virology. Connects solid-state physics and chemistry to biological and medical sciences through structural analysis at the atomic level.
highlights: Introduces the field of crystallography, and explains its basic concepts
Gives an outline of the historical development of crystallography, and its key role in the early days of molecular biology
Describes recent advances, and the importance of crystallography in the modern world in areas such as genetics and pharmaceuticals
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Astrophysics
titles.author: James Binney
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Astrophysics is the physics of the stars and, more widely, the Universe. It explores the structure and evolution of planetary systems, stars, galaxies, interstellar gas, and the cosmos as a whole. The field has expanded rapidly in the past century, with vast quantities of data gathered by telescopes exploiting the full electromagnetic spectrum, combined with the rapid advance of computing power, allowing increasingly effective mathematical modelling. Astrophysics: A Very Short Introduction illustrates how the application of fundamental principles of physics—the consideration of energy and mass, and momentum—along with relativity and quantum mechanics, has provided insights into phenomena ranging from supernovae and accretion discs to pulsars and spiral galaxies.
titles.summaryAI: Physics of stars, galaxies, interstellar gas, and the cosmos, applying fundamental physical principles to celestial phenomena. Covers planetary system structure, stellar evolution, galactic dynamics, and cosmological models. Examines the role of energy, mass, momentum, angular momentum, relativity, and quantum mechanics in explaining phenomena including supernovae, accretion discs, pulsars, spiral galaxies, and black holes. Addresses observational methods across the electromagnetic spectrum and the rapid expansion of computational modelling. Connects particle physics and thermodynamics to large-scale cosmic structure and evolution.
highlights: Provides an authoritative introduction to astrophysics, demonstrating how the laws of physics we can observe here on Earth apply to our entire universe
Introduces the physics of phenomena such as supernovae, accretion discs and jets, and planetary systems
Provides a succinct account of the application of special and general relativity to astrophysics
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The BRICS
titles.author: Andrew F. Cooper
titles.subject: Politics/International relations
titles.abstract: In the wake of the post-Cold War era, the aftermath of 9/11, the 2008 global financial crisis, and the emergence of the G20, few commentators expected a reshaping of the global system towards multipolarity, and away from the United States. And yet, the BRICS—encompassing Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa—has emerged as a challenge to the international status quo. But what is its capacity as a transformative force? The BRICS: A Very Short Introduction explores the emergence of the BRICS as a concept. It provides a contemporary analysis of the BRICS’ practice and influence as a forum and a lobby group in advancing a distinctive, but amorphous agenda among global politics.
titles.summaryAI: Emergence and geopolitical significance of the BRICS grouping (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa) in post-Cold War international relations. Covers the conceptual origins of BRICS, its development as a diplomatic forum and lobby group following the 2008 global financial crisis and rise of the G20. Analyzes BRICS as a challenge to Western-dominated institutions including the IMF, World Bank, UN Security Council, and WTO. Examines the shift toward multipolarity in global governance and the group's capacity as a transformative force in reshaping international economic and political order.
highlights: Provides an informative, analytical and comprehensive overview of the BRICS
Addresses a wide range of issues concerning the BRICS in our contemporary political landscape
Offers an explanation of what the BRICS have been able to accomplish as a hybrid club, forum, and lobby group in global politics, and their potential trajectory going forwards
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Decolonization
titles.author: Dane Kennedy
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Decolonization is the term commonly used to refer to this transition from a world of colonial empires to a world of nation-states in the years after World War II. Decolonization: A Very Short Introduction demonstrates that this process involved considerable violence and instability. It highlights three themes: first, that global war between empires precipitated decolonization, creating the economic and political crises that gave colonial subjects the opportunity to seek independence. Second, that the nation-state was not the only option pursued by anti-colonial activists; and the third, that the struggle to escape imperial subjugation and create nation-states generated widespread violence and produced huge refugee populations, leading to political problems that persist today.
titles.summaryAI: Transition from colonial empires to independent nation-states primarily after World War II. Covers how global wars between empires created economic and political crises enabling independence movements. Examines anti-colonial activism across Asia, Africa, and other regions, including alternatives to the nation-state model pursued by independence leaders like Nehru and Nkrumah. Analyzes the widespread violence, refugee crises, and political instability generated by decolonization processes. Connects imperialism, nationalism, and self-determination to persistent contemporary political problems arising from the legacy of colonial rule.
highlights: Leads to a better understanding of the process of decolonization, as well as its impact on contemporary international relations.
Provides a long historical perspective that notes prior waves of decolonization.
Makes a case for empires' abilities to adjust to the age of the nation-state.

titles.title: Agriculture
titles.author: Paul Brassley, Richard Soffe
titles.subject: Agriculture/History
titles.abstract: Agriculture: A Very Short Introduction explains what farmers do and why they do it. Beginning with the most basic resource, the soil, it shows why it is important, and how farmers can increase its productivity, before turning to the plants and animals that grow on it, and tracing the connections between their biology and the various ways in which farmers work with them. It concludes by looking at some of the controversial issues facing contemporary agriculture: its sustainability; its impact on wildlife and landscape; issues of animal welfare; and the effect of climate change and the development of genetically modified organisms on farmers.
titles.summaryAI: Agricultural science and practice covering soil management, crop cultivation, and animal husbandry. Examines soil productivity and enhancement techniques, plant biology and breeding, livestock management, and the connections between biological science and farming methods. Addresses contemporary controversies including agricultural sustainability, environmental impact on wildlife and landscapes, animal welfare standards, climate change effects on farming, and genetically modified organisms. Connects land use and food production to ecology, economics, and environmental policy, spanning both traditional practices and modern industrial agriculture.
highlights: Introduces readers to the vast range of global agriculture, covering both industrial and developing countries
Considers both livestock and crop farming, and the multiple issues facing the different forms of farming
Explains the principles of local and international agricultural markets, and how they determine what farmers produce
Identifies the universally applicable principles of farming
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven millions copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Combinatorics
titles.author: Robin Wilson
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Combinatorics is the branch of mathematics concerned with selecting, arranging, and listing or counting collections of objects. Dating back some 3000 years, and initially consisting mainly of the study of permutations and combinations, its scope has broadened to include topics such as graph theory, partitions of numbers, block designs, design of codes, and latin squares. Combinatorics: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of the field and its applications in mathematics and computer theory, considering problems from the shortest routes covering certain stops to the minimum number of colours needed to draw a map with different colours for neighbouring countries.
titles.summaryAI: Branch of mathematics concerned with selecting, arranging, listing, and counting collections of objects, with origins dating back three thousand years. Covers permutations, combinations, graph theory, partitions of numbers, block designs, coding theory, and Latin squares. Examines classic problems including the travelling salesman problem, minimum connector problem, assignment problem, and map coloring. Connects to number theory, geometry, and computer science through algorithmic applications. Spans both pure mathematical foundations and practical optimization problems in discrete mathematics.
highlights: Introduces Combinatorics through a problem-solving approach
Covers the core aspects of the subject such as permutations, combinations, and latin squares
Explores a variety of classic and modern problems, from the Könisberg bridges to Sudoku puzzles
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Adolescence
titles.author: Peter K. Smith
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Adolescence is a turbulent period to live through. Marking the transition from the world of childhood to adult life, the adolescent faces many challenges and opportunities, including puberty, cognitive changes, forming their own identity, relating to often conflicting demands from parents and peers, and negotiating first romantic relationships. Adolescence: A Very Short Introduction provides a guide to the classical research that has informed our knowledge on adolescence, as well as the results of modern research into the contemporary adolescent experience. It also examines a number of aspects of adolescence, including the cultural and historical context, the biological changes to the adolescent brain, and the controversies that adolescence brings.
titles.summaryAI: Developmental psychology of the adolescent period marking the transition from childhood to adulthood. Covers biological changes including puberty and adolescent brain development, cognitive maturation, identity formation, and social dynamics with parents and peers. Examines first romantic relationships, risk behaviors, delinquency, bullying, and obesity in adolescent populations. Addresses cultural and historical variation in adolescent experience. Integrates classical developmental research with contemporary findings, connecting biological neuroscience, psychology, and sociology to understand this distinct life stage.
highlights: Defines what adolescence is and when it occurs

Considers adolescence as a life stage - encompassing puberty, cognitive changes, and the search for identity; relationship changes; leisure activities; risk-taking and anti-social behaviours; adolescent mental health and romantic development
Adopts an interdisciplinary perspective to the topic, incorporating approaches from biology, anthropology, developmental psychology, and sociology
Explores the cultural and historical context of adolescence
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Isotopes
titles.author: Rob Ellam
titles.subject: Chemistry
titles.abstract: An isotope is a variant form of a chemical element, containing a different number of neutrons in its nucleus. Most elements exist as several isotopes. Many are stable, others are radioactive, and some may only exist fleetingly before decaying into other elements. The study of isotope ratios has become central to many areas of science, and used to probe the age and history of the Earth and the origins of the Solar System. Radioactive isotopes find wide applications, from nuclear power to medicine and carbon dating. Isotopes: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of the nature of stable and radioactive isotopes, considering their wide range of modern applications.
titles.summaryAI: Variant forms of chemical elements differing in neutron number, encompassing both stable and radioactive isotopes. Covers nuclear structure, radioactive decay processes, and isotope ratio measurement techniques. Examines applications in geochronology and Earth age determination, solar system origins, nuclear power generation, medical diagnostics and treatment, and radiocarbon dating for archaeology. Connects atomic physics and nuclear chemistry to geology, cosmochemistry, medicine, and environmental science through isotopic analysis as a cross-disciplinary analytical tool.
highlights: Explains the role of isotopes in tackling challenges such as climate change, cancer treatment, Earth's age and origin, and nuclear accidents
Considers the use of isotopes in archaeology for carbon dating
Describes the various technologies used to measure the proportions of radioactive and stable isotopes
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Savannas
titles.author: Peter A. Furley
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Savannas form one of the largest and most important of the world’s ecological zones. Covering one-fifth of the Earth’s land surface, they are home to some of the world’s most iconic animals and form an extremely important global resource for plants and wildlife. Savannas: A Very Short Introduction considers savannas as landscapes and explains the variety of the vegetation, wildlife, and environments. Discussing their origin, topography, and global distribution, it explores the dynamic nature of savannas and illustrates how they have shaped human evolution and movements. It goes on to discuss the unrelenting pressures that confront conservation and management, and considers the future for savannas.
titles.summaryAI: Tropical and subtropical savanna ecosystems covering one-fifth of Earth's land surface. Covers savanna origins, topography, global distribution, vegetation types including campo cerrado and caatingas, and characteristic wildlife communities. Examines climate-vegetation interactions, fire ecology, soil dynamics, and the role of savannas in shaping human evolution and migration patterns. Addresses conservation challenges, land management pressures, and future prospects. Connects biogeography, ecology, climatology, and human prehistory through analysis of these dynamic landscapes spanning Africa, South America, and Australia.
highlights: Explains the nature and variety of the vegetation, wildlife, and environments of tropical savannas
Discusses the evolution of savannas and their role in the emergence and distribution of human populations
Illustrates the characteristics of each of the continental formations, including the lesser-known non-savanna inclusions
Outlines the key issues and concerns over current developments to savannas
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Hebrew Bible As Literature
titles.author: Tod Linafelt
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Hebrew Bible, or Christian Old Testament, can be celebrated as among the classics of world literature. The stories of Jacob and David present the earliest surviving examples of literary characters whose development the reader follows over the length of a lifetime. There are also examples of lyric poetry, from the praise and lament of the Psalms to the double entendres in the love of poetry of the Song of Songs. The Hebrew Bible as Literature: A Very Short Introduction describes the Bible’s two primary literary modes—narrative and poetry—foregrounding the nuances of plot, character, metaphor, structure and design, and intertextual allusions.
titles.summaryAI: Literary analysis of the Hebrew Bible (Christian Old Testament) focusing on narrative and poetic modes. Covers character development in stories of Jacob and David as early examples of sustained literary characterization. Examines lyric poetry including Psalms of praise and lament, and erotic imagery in the Song of Songs. Analyzes plot construction, metaphor, structural design, and intertextual allusions across biblical texts. Connects ancient Near Eastern literary traditions to world literature, distinguishing literary-critical approaches from theological or historical readings of scripture.
highlights: Provides a short, digestible, lively introduction to the Hebrew Bible as literature
Gives readers the tools to examine and appreciate the Hebrew Bible's literary achievements
Fully distinguishes between the narrative and poetic modes in the Hebrew Bible
Allows readers to approach the Hebrew Bible from a new direction

titles.title: The Harlem Renaissance
titles.author: Cheryl A. Wall
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: The Harlem Renaissance was a cultural awakening among African Americans between the two world wars. It was the cultural phase of the “New Negro” movement, a social and political phenomenon that promoted a proud racial identity, economic independence, and progressive politics. The Harlem Renaissance: A Very Short Introduction identifies the issues and strategies that engaged writers, musicians, and visual artists alike. It introduces key figures such as Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Claude McKay, and Jean Toomer, along with such signature texts as Home to Harlem and Their Eyes Were Watching God; photographer James Van Der Zee and painters Archibald Motley and Laura Wheeler are also mentioned.
titles.summaryAI: African American cultural movement between World War I and World War II centered in Harlem, New York. Covers the literary, musical, and visual arts dimensions of the New Negro movement, which promoted racial pride, economic independence, and progressive politics. Examines key figures including Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Claude McKay, and Jean Toomer in literature, and James Van Der Zee, Archibald Motley, and Laura Wheeler in visual arts. Connects cultural production to racial identity, social activism, and the broader transformation of American arts and letters in the interwar period.
highlights: Only comprehensive single-volume history of the Harlem Renaissance in the last twenty-five years.
Provides a broad overview of the cultural landscape during the Harlem Renaissance, including literature, music, theather, and visual arts.
Understands the Harlem Renaissance as part of a diverse and international cultural awakening.
Wall's writing is lively and elegant.

titles.title: Babylonia
titles.author: Trevor Bryce
titles.subject: Classical Studies
titles.abstract: Babylonia: A Very Short Introduction takes us on a journey of more than 2,000 years across the history and civilization of ancient Babylonia, from the emergence of its chief city, Babylon, as a village on the Euphrates in the 3rd millennium bc through phases of triumph, decline, and resurgence until its royal capital faded into obscurity in the Roman imperial era. Exploring key historical events as well as the day-to-day life of an ancient Babylonian—including the great Hammurabic, Kassite, and Nebuchadnezzar’s Neo-Babylonian empires as well as the periods of Persian and Macedonian rule under Alexander the Great—it provides a comprehensive guide to one of history’s most profound civilizations.
titles.summaryAI: Ancient Babylonian civilization spanning over two thousand years from the third millennium BC to the Roman imperial era, centered on the city of Babylon on the Euphrates River. Covers the Hammurabic dynasty, Kassite period, Nebuchadnezzar's Neo-Babylonian empire, and periods under Persian and Macedonian rule including Alexander the Great. Examines political history, daily life, writing systems, law, religion, and social organization. Connects Mesopotamian civilization to broader ancient Near Eastern, Greek, and Parthian cultural interactions and imperial succession.
highlights: Offers a concise introduction to the history and civilization of Babylonia
Explores how the Babylonians lived and thought, and how their fears, frustrations, hopes, and achievements are communicated across the centuries
Uses ancient sources to describe the Babylonians in their own words
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Learning
titles.author: Mark Haselgrove
titles.subject: Education
titles.abstract: What is learning? How does it take place? What happens when it goes wrong? The topic of learning has been central to the development of the science of psychology since its inception. Without learning there can be no memory, no language, and no intelligence. Learning: A Very Short Introduction describes learning from the perspective of associative theories of classical and instrumental conditioning, and considers why these are the dominant, and best described analyses of learning in contemporary psychology. Tracing the origins of these theories, it discusses the techniques used to study learning in both animals and humans, and considers the importance of learning for animal behaviour and survival.
titles.summaryAI: Psychological science of learning examined through associative theories of classical and instrumental conditioning. Covers the origins and development of conditioning theories, experimental techniques for studying learning in both animals and humans, and the relationship between learning and memory, language, and intelligence. Examines Pavlovian conditioning, operant conditioning, reinforcement, and stimulus-response associations. Connects behaviorist psychology to neuroscience and cognitive science, analyzing how learning mechanisms underpin animal behavior, survival strategies, and adaptive responses to environmental contingencies.
highlights: Discusses the centrality of learning to our lives, and its role in memory, language and intelligence
Considers the significance of learning to survival for animals, who need strategies to recognize changes in their environment, both opportunities and threats, and to respond appropriately
Comments on psychology experiments and recent studies in the field of learning
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Public health
titles.author: Virginia Berridge
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Public Health: A Very Short Introduction explores the areas that fall under the remit of public health, and explains how the individual histories of different countries have come to cause great differences in the perception of the role and responsibilities of public health organizations. In its narrowest sense, public health can refer to the health of a population, the longevity of individual members, and their freedom from disease, but it can also be anticipatory, geared to the prevention of illness, rather than simply the provision of care and treatment. Drawing on wide-ranging international examples, this VSI demonstrates the central role of history in understanding the amorphous nature of public health today.
titles.summaryAI: Population-level health management encompassing disease prevention, sanitation, health education, and public policy. Covers the historical development of public health institutions and practices across different countries, examining how national histories shape organizational structures and responsibilities. Analyzes the distinction between preventive and curative approaches, addressing epidemiology, lifestyle interventions, and health promotion. Uses international comparative examples to illustrate variation in public health philosophy and practice. Connects medicine, government policy, social conditions, and historical context to explain the evolving scope and contested boundaries of population health.
highlights: Places public health in its historical context, and analyses how the changing face of public health is essential to understanding its contemporary concerns
Discusses the demographic, institutional, scientific, political, and professional influences, which have shaped views about the health of the public

Considers the differences in approaches to public health across the globe, from biosecurity and the defence of the nation against terrorist attack in the US, to health promoting, and 'well-being' aspects of health in the UK
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Indian Cinema
titles.author: Ashish Rajadhyaksha
titles.subject: Cinema
titles.abstract: One film out of every five made anywhere comes from India. From its beginnings under colonial rule through to the heights of Bollywood, Indian cinema has challenged social injustices such as caste, the oppression of Indian women, religious intolerance, rural poverty, and the pressures of life in the burgeoning cities. Indian Cinema: A Very Short Introduction delves into the political, social, and economic factors which have shaped Indian cinema into a fascinating counterculture. Covering everything from silent cinema through to the digital era, it examines how the industry reflects the complexity and variety of Indian society through the dramatic changes of the 20th century, and into the beginnings of the 21st.
titles.summaryAI: Film industry and cinematic culture of India from silent cinema through the digital era, encompassing Bollywood and regional cinema traditions. Covers the political, social, and economic factors shaping Indian filmmaking under colonial and postcolonial conditions. Examines how cinema has addressed caste discrimination, gender oppression, religious intolerance, rural poverty, and urbanization. Analyzes relationships between film and other media including theatre, radio, television, and information technology. Connects Indian cinema to broader questions of censorship, national identity, cultural production, and dramatic social transformation across the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.
highlights: Locates the Indian film industry within a larger Cinema Studies framework
Frames the history of Indian cinema within its economic and cultural context, from the influence of colonialism to the different moments of independent India
Focuses on Bollywood as a key player in the creative economy
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Public Administration
titles.author: Stella Z. Theodoulou, Ravi K. Roy
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Public administration ensures the development and delivery of the essential public services required for sustaining modern civilization. Covering areas from public safety and social welfare to transportation and education, the services provided through the public sector are inextricably part of our daily lives. Public Administration: A Very Short Introduction offers practical insight into the major challenges confronting the public sector in the globalized era. Tackling some of the most hotly debated issues of our time, including the privatization of public services and government surveillance, it takes the reader on a global journey through history to examine the origins, development, and continued evolution of public administration.
titles.summaryAI: Theory and practice of government bureaucracy responsible for developing and delivering public services including safety, welfare, transportation, and education. Covers the historical origins and evolution of public administration from its emergence to the globalized era. Examines the relationship between public and private sectors, privatization of government services, government surveillance, and management reform. Analyzes comparative models of administration across different nations and political systems. Connects organizational theory, political science, and policy implementation to the practical challenges of governing in complex modern democracies.
highlights: Covers all areas of public administration, including public safety, social welfare, public transport and state provided education
Offers a global perspective, drawing on real case studies taken from a wide array of countries
Considers the issues and challenges which confront the public sector worldwide
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Molecular Biology
titles.author: Aysha Divan, Janice Royds
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Molecular biology is the story of the molecules of life, their relationships, and how these interactions are controlled. Its applications are wide and growing; the power of molecular biology can now be harnessed to treat diseases, solve crimes, map human history, and produce genetically modified organisms and crops. Starting with the building blocks established by Darwin, Wallace, and Mendel, and the discovery of the structure of DNA in 1953, Molecular Biology: A Very Short Introduction considers the wide range of applications for molecular biology today, including the development of new drugs and DNA fingerprinting, and looks forward to two key areas of evolving research: personalized medicine and synthetic biology.
titles.summaryAI: Science of biological molecules and their interactions at the cellular and subcellular level. Covers the discovery of DNA structure by Watson and Crick building on work by Franklin, and foundational contributions of Darwin, Wallace, and Mendel to hereditary theory. Examines nucleic acid and protein structure, gene expression, and molecular mechanisms of cellular processes. Addresses applications including pharmaceutical drug development, DNA fingerprinting for forensic science, genetic engineering, personalized medicine, and synthetic biology. Connects genetics, biochemistry, and cell biology to medicine and biotechnology.
highlights: Covers the main concepts of the field of molecular biology, including regulatory effects, epigenetics, and gene-environment interactions
Describes the variety of applications of molecular biology, from biotechnology to molecular forensics
Considers the future possibilities of the field, including personalized medicine and synthetic biology
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Blood
titles.author: Chris Cooper
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Blood is vital to most animals. In mammals it transports oxygen and food, carries away waste, and contains the white cells that attack invading microbes. Playing a central role in life, blood has profound cultural and historical significance and plays an important role in religious ritual. Blood was one of the four humours in early Western medicine and is still a major diagnostic tool in the doctor’s armoury. Blood: A Very Short Introduction analyses the components of blood, explains blood groups, and looks at transfusions, blood tests, and blood-borne diseases. It considers the future, including the possibility of making artificial blood, and producing blood from stem cells in the laboratory.
titles.summaryAI: Biology and medical science of blood in mammals, covering its composition, functions, and clinical significance. Examines hemoglobin and oxygen transport, nutrient delivery, waste removal, and immune function through white blood cells. Covers blood groups, transfusion medicine, blood-borne diseases, diagnostic blood testing, and blood pressure physiology. Addresses historical and cultural significance of blood including its role in early Western humoral medicine and religious ritual. Explores future developments including artificial blood and stem cell-derived blood production. Connects physiology, immunology, and hematology to medical practice and cultural history.
highlights: Offers a comprehensive exploration of blood's many uses in biology and medical sciences
Explains how blood functions and the different components contained in blood
Provides an insight into blood's fascinating historical role in culture and religion
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Copernicus
titles.author: Owen Gingerich
titles.subject: History of Science/Science/Biography
titles.abstract: Nicolaus Copernicus (1473–1543) was the astronomer whose shocking vision of a sun-centered universe turned out to be the essential blueprint for a physical understanding of celestial motions. Copernicus: A Very Short Introduction offers a fascinating portrayal of the man who launched the modern vision of the universe. It sets Copernicus in the context of a rapidly changing world, where the recent invention of printing with movable type not only made sources more readily available to him, but also fueled Martin Luther’s transformation of the religious landscape. Copernicus’s heliocentric revolution is revealed as an aesthetic achievement not dictated by observational “proofs,” but another new way of looking at the ancient cosmos.
titles.summaryAI: Nicolaus Copernicus (1473-1543) and the heliocentric revolution in astronomy during the Renaissance. Covers the development of the sun-centered cosmological model as an aesthetic and mathematical achievement rather than one driven by observational proof. Examines the intellectual context of early modern Europe including the impact of movable-type printing on scientific communication and Martin Luther's religious reformation. Traces influence on later astronomers including Kepler and Galileo. Connects the history of astronomy to the history of science, technology, philosophy, and the broader cultural transformation of the early modern period.
highlights: Owen Gingerich is the world authority on Copernicus
Provides a stimulating view of Copernicus' life and work and a fresh perspective on the "Copernican" revolution
Frames Copernicus in the political background of his time

titles.title: Military Justice
titles.author: Eugene R. Fidell
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Military Justice: A Very Short Introduction presents an accessible and honest assessment of the strengths and weaknesses of military justice in both common law countries and those with other legal traditions, with particular emphasis on the United States, the United Kingdom, and Canada. It integrates a description of the US military justice system with a comparative view of civilian and foreign models for the administration of justice, including the increasingly important focus on human rights. The military is the rare part of contemporary society that enjoys the privilege of policing the behavior of its own members, with special courts and a separate body of rules.
titles.summaryAI: Legal systems governing military personnel, including courts-martial, military tribunals, and separate bodies of military law. Focuses primarily on common law traditions in the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada while incorporating comparative perspectives from other legal systems. Examines the unique privilege of military self-policing, human rights considerations, terrorism-related legal challenges, and the role of institutions like the International Criminal Court. Addresses the Guantanamo Bay detention facility. Connects military law to constitutional law, international humanitarian law, and broader questions of civil-military relations and due process.
highlights: Presents a global picture of military justice, comparing the US system with foreign and civilian models
The perfect tool to understand how military justice works around the world and why it is so different from civilian justice
Provides a much-needed context for the current controversy over the role of military commanders in the military justice system

titles.title: Cognitive Neuroscience
titles.author: Richard Passingham
titles.subject: Neuroscience/Psychology
titles.abstract: Cognitive neuroscience was born in the late 1970s when the study of patients who had suffered brain damage or injury to limited parts of the brain revealed different components and the processes taking place in the brain. Cognitive Neuroscience: A Very Short Introduction outlines the nature and scope of this relatively new field and the techniques available, focusing on investigation of the human brain. It explains what brain imaging shows, points out common misconceptions, and gives a brief overview of the different aspects of human cognition: perceiving, attending, remembering, reasoning, deciding, and acting. It concludes with a discussion of the exciting advances that may lie ahead.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary study of the neural basis of mental processes, emerging in the late 1970s from brain lesion research. Covers brain imaging techniques and their interpretation, including common misconceptions about neuroimaging data. Examines the neuroscience of perception, attention, memory, reasoning, decision-making, and motor action. Analyzes how brain damage reveals distinct cognitive components and processing pathways. Connects neurology, experimental psychology, and computational approaches to understanding human cognition. Distinguishes cognitive neuroscience from behavioral psychology and philosophy of mind while addressing future research directions.
highlights: A provocative and exciting account from a distinguished cognitive neuroscientist of the nature and scope of this field
Looks at the latest imaging technologies such as fMRI, and clarifies what brain imaging tells us
Discusses the latest understanding of the brain processes behind perceiving, thinking, remembering, and decision making
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Leibniz
titles.author: Maria Rosa Antognazza
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Leibniz: A Very Short Introduction discusses the philosophy of the German polymath Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646–1716) and his desire for systematic reform and advancement of all sciences and establishment of a stable and just political order. It introduces the fundamental principles of Leibniz’s thought and his theories of truth and knowledge. Exploring Leibniz’s contributions to logic, mathematics, physics, and metaphysics, it considers how his theories sat alongside his concerns with politics, diplomacy, and a range of practical reforms. Discussing Leibniz’s theories of possible worlds, it concludes by looking at what is ultimately real in the world we experience, and Leibniz’s response to the problem of evil through his theodicy.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophy of Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716), German polymath who sought systematic reform across all sciences and a just political order. Covers Leibniz's fundamental principles, theories of truth and knowledge, contributions to logic and the characteristica universalis, mathematics including calculus, physics, and metaphysics. Examines the theory of possible worlds, monads, and the theodicy as a response to the problem of evil. Addresses Leibniz's engagement with politics, diplomacy, and practical reforms. Connects rationalist philosophy to early modern science, theology, and political thought.
highlights: Introduces the German polymath and philosopher Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz and discusses his philosophy, and how his intellectual visions were shaped by his own experiences in life

Discusses Leibniz's great master project, the systematic reform and advancement of all the sciences, to be undertaken as a collaborative enterprise supported by an enlightened ruler, with the goal of the improvement of the human condition and the celebration thereby of the glory of God in His creation
Uncovers what it was, for Leibniz, to be a substance, and how this conception informed his doctrine of simple substances (or monads), and his theory of corporeal substances and bodies

Examines Leibniz's philosophy as the product of a multi-layered conversation with centuries of past thought and hundreds of individuals of his own era, whose insights were reshaped and remodelled into a strikingly original outlook
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: War and Technology
titles.author: Alex Roland
titles.subject: Military/Engineering
titles.abstract: Warfare and its technologies predate human history. Their dynamic relationship drives the transformation of war and countless realms of technological innovation. Yet even now the newest, best, and highest technology cannot guarantee victory. Rather, technology and warfare remain in a timeless dialectic, driving change without ever stabilizing a military balance of power or capping a trajectory of technological creativity. New technologies continue to push warfare in unexpected directions, while warfare pulls technology into new realms and new possibilities. War and Technology: A Very Short Introduction explores this continually evolving story from the ancestors of Homo sapiens to the age of net-centric warfare and autonomous weapons.
titles.summaryAI: Dialectical relationship between military conflict and technological innovation from prehistoric ancestors of Homo sapiens to contemporary net-centric and autonomous warfare. Covers the co-evolution of weapons systems, military tactics, and technological capabilities across land, sea, air, and digital domains. Examines nuclear weapons, information warfare, and autonomous weapons systems. Analyzes why technological superiority does not guarantee military victory and how warfare drives innovation while technology transforms combat. Connects military history to engineering, industrial development, and the broader trajectory of technological change across civilizations.
highlights: A compact history of 300,000 years of military technology
Shows that technology is the primary driver of changes in warfare, and that military needs constantly generate technological innovation
Reveals the modern trend toward dual-use technologies, such as airplanes, radio, rockets, and computers, which have both military and civilian applications
Explains why technology has always favoured victory in warfare but has never guaranteed it

titles.title: Pandemics
titles.author: Christian W. McMillen
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: The 2014 Ebola epidemic demonstrated the power of pandemics and their ability not only to destroy lives locally but also to capture imaginations worldwide. Pandemics: A Very Short Introduction provides a concise yet comprehensive account of pandemics throughout human history, illustrating the ways in which pandemic disease—including plague, tuberculosis, malaria, smallpox, cholera, influenza, HIV/AIDS, and Covid 19—has shaped history and how human history has shaped pandemic disease. By considering the explosion of medical research and the varying state responses, such as quarantine and travel restrictions, this Very Short Introduction shows why pandemics are both interesting from a medical standpoint and provide insight into the culture and politics of their time.
titles.summaryAI: History and science of pandemic diseases across human civilization, covering plague, tuberculosis, malaria, smallpox, cholera, influenza, HIV/AIDS, and Covid-19. Examines how pandemic disease has shaped demographic, political, and social history, and how human activity has reciprocally influenced disease emergence and spread. Analyzes medical research responses, public health interventions including quarantine and travel restrictions, and varying state policies. Connects epidemiology, microbiology, and immunology to cultural attitudes, political decision-making, and international cooperation in disease control across different historical periods and geographic regions.
highlights: Describes all the major pandemics in human history, including plague, tuberculosis, malaria, smallpox, cholera, HIV/AIDS, and COVID-19
Deftly combines scientific explanations with historical and cultural analysis
Deals with current discussions over epidemic disease, such as COVID-19, the Ebola epidemic, the Zika virus, and the continued concern about avian flu

titles.title: Translation
titles.author: Matthew Reynolds
titles.subject: Linguistics
titles.abstract: Translation is everywhere, and matters to everybody. Translation doesn’t only give us foreign news, dubbed films, and instructions for using the microwave: without it, there would be no world religions, and our literatures, our cultures, and our languages would be unrecognizable. Translation: A Very Short Introduction provides an authoritative and thought-provoking account of the field, from ancient Chinese to World English, from St Jerome to Google Translate. It shows how translation determines meaning; how it matters in commerce, empire, conflict, and resistance; and why it is fundamental to literature and the arts.
titles.summaryAI: Theory and practice of translation across languages, cultures, and historical periods from ancient Chinese traditions to contemporary machine translation. Covers how translation determines and transforms meaning, its role in the development of world religions, literature, and cross-cultural exchange. Examines translation in commerce, empire, political conflict, and resistance movements. Addresses sign language translation and the impact of technologies from printing to automated systems. Connects linguistics, literary studies, philosophy of language, and communication theory to the practical challenges of rendering meaning across linguistic and cultural boundaries.
highlights: Offers an authoritative account of the field of translation, covering the whole history of translation
Provides a range of fascinating examples from many languages
Puts forward fresh arguments about why translation matters and how it is changing in the digital age
Part of the bestselling Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Modern Italy
titles.author: Anna Cento Bull
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Modern Italy: A Very Short Introduction addresses the question of what modernity means to Italy and explores the extent to which modernity still represents a shared vision among Italian intellectuals, political leaders, and ordinary people. It covers the history of Italy from the Risorgimento (Resurgence), the movement leading to the Italian Unification in 1861, to the present. Italy’s political system and style of government is considered along with its economic modernization and issues with emigration, internal migration, and immigration. This volume concludes by looking at Italian culture and lifestyle, including modern art and architecture, cinema, literature, gastronomy, fashion, and sport.
titles.summaryAI: Italian history and society from the Risorgimento and 1861 unification to the present, examining modernity as a contested concept among Italian intellectuals, political leaders, and citizens. Covers political systems and governance including the Berlusconi era, economic modernization and industrial development, and patterns of emigration, internal migration, and immigration. Examines Italian cultural contributions in modern art, architecture, cinema including Fellini, literature, gastronomy, fashion, and sport. Addresses the role of the Roman Catholic Church in Italian society. Connects national identity formation to political, economic, and cultural transformation.
highlights: Covers the history of Italy from the Risorgimento (Resurgence), the movement leading to Italian Unification in 1861, to the present

Addresses the question of whether modern Italy is a modern country, and explores the extent to which modernity still represents a shared vision among Italian intellectuals, political leaders, and ordinary people
Contemplates themes of identity and asks what Modern Italy stands for by looking at the different cultural and political projects of modernity which have unfolded in Italy since the Risorgimento
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Eugenics
titles.author: Philippa Levine
titles.subject: History of Science
titles.abstract: The word “eugenics” was coined in 1883 by Francis Galton to express his dream of perfecting the human race by applying the laws of genetic heredity. Eugenics: A Very Short Introduction tackles the intricate and controversial history of eugenics. It synthesizes the enormous range of policies and experiments carried out in the name of eugenics around the world throughout the twentieth century. It questions the widespread belief that eugenics disappeared after World War II and evaluates the impact of eugenics on current reproductive and genetic sciences. Charting the development of such controversial practices as artificial insemination, sperm donation, and population control, this VSI offers a timely reflection on the frequent interplay between genetics and ethics.
titles.summaryAI: History and legacy of eugenics from Francis Galton's 1883 coinage through twentieth-century global implementation and its continuing influence on reproductive and genetic sciences. Covers the range of eugenic policies including selective breeding, forced sterilization, artificial insemination, sperm donation, and population control programs carried out worldwide. Examines the relationship between genetic heredity, intelligence, race, and social engineering. Challenges the assumption that eugenics ended after World War II. Connects genetics, ethics, public policy, and human rights to ongoing debates about reproductive technology, genetic screening, and the boundaries of scientific intervention.
highlights: The first synthetic analysis of eugenics to adopt a global perspective
Explores often suppressed cases of eugenic practices in the United States
Debunks the myth that eugenics begins and ends with Nazism and the Holocaust
Reveals the effects of eugenics on current reproductive and genetic sciences

titles.title: Ageing
titles.author: Nancy A. Pachana
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Ageing: A Very Short Introduction discusses the lifelong dynamic changes in biological, psychological, and social functioning involved in ageing. Increased lifespans have created an urgent need to find ways to enhance our functioning and well-being in the later decades of life. This is reflected in relevant policies and action plans from the WHO and the UN. Looking to the future, this VSI considers advancements in provision for our ageing populations, including revolutionary models of nursing-home care. Understanding the process of ageing is not only important for individuals but also for societies and nations if the full potential of those entering later life is to be realized.
titles.summaryAI: Biological, psychological, and social dimensions of human ageing across the lifespan, viewed through the lens of gerontology. Covers physiological changes in later life, cognitive aging, mental health including suicide risk, employment and retirement, family relationships and marriage, and institutionalization. Examines WHO and UN policy frameworks addressing aging populations and increased life expectancy. Addresses innovative nursing-home care models and strategies for enhancing well-being and functioning in later decades. Connects developmental psychology, medicine, social policy, and demographics to the challenges and opportunities of population aging.
highlights: Explores the biological, psychological, and social changes humans undergo as they age, and discusses contemporary scientific research into these changes
Considers sensitive questions about how best to care for the oldest parts of our society, and looks forward to recent innovations in the care profession for the elderly

Discusses the key to positive and succesful ageing and the importance of the continuing participation of older adults in the social, economic, cultural, spiritual, and civic affairs of their communities
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Asian American History
titles.author: Madeline Y. Hsu
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: Asian American History: A Very Short Introduction explores the complex history of Asian immigrants in the United States, from their arrival during the Gold Rush to their role in constructing the Transcontinental Railroad. Despite being perceived as highly successful and well-assimilated in modern America, Asian Americans have historically faced exclusion and discrimination. This VSI examines key moments such as the incarceration of Japanese Americans during World War II and the rise of the “model minorities” stereotype in the 1960s. It traces the evolution of their struggle for full acceptance and integration. The VSI provides a narrative interpretation of key themes within the broader history of Asian migrations to North America.
titles.summaryAI: History of Asian immigration and Asian American communities in the United States from the mid-nineteenth-century Gold Rush era to the present. Covers labor contributions including Transcontinental Railroad construction, exclusion laws, discrimination, and the World War II incarceration of Japanese Americans. Examines the emergence of the model minority stereotype in the 1960s and ongoing struggles for acceptance and integration. Traces migration patterns from multiple Asian countries to North America. Connects immigration policy, racial politics, labor history, and cultural identity to the broader narrative of American social history and civil rights.
highlights: Highlights how Asian immigration has shaped the evolution of ideological and legal interpretations of America as a "nation of immigrants."
First study to deal explicitly with the racial repositioning of Asians during the mid-20th century stemming from the retreat from segregation as the dominant racial ideology.
Illustrates the shifting of immigration threat to hemispheric migrations.

Considers critical themes of contemporary relevance such as immigration policy, socioeconomic inequality of the minorities, refugee policy and integration of immigrants, and international labor markets
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Home
titles.author: Michael Allen Fox
titles.subject: Philosophy/Sociology
titles.abstract: Thoughts and feelings about home traditionally provided people of all cultures with a firm sense of where they belonged, and why. But the world is rapidly changing: populations are constantly on the move, seeking better opportunities and living conditions, or an escape from violence and war. Despite this, the concept of home remains a central organizing influence in human life. Home: A Very Short Introduction considers the complex meaning of home and the essential importance of place to human psychology. It considers what a dwelling is, the variety of dwellings, and also looks at the politics of the concept of ‘home’, homelessness, refugeeism and migration, and the future of home.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical and sociological examination of home as a concept central to human psychology, identity, and cultural belonging. Covers the nature and variety of dwellings, the meaning of place and dwelling in human experience, and the psychological significance of domestic space. Examines the politics of home including homelessness, refugee displacement, migration, and the impact of globalization on traditional place-attachment. Draws on thinkers including Bachelard and Wittgenstein. Connects philosophy, psychology, sociology, and political theory to questions of belonging, displacement, and the future of habitation in a mobile world.
highlights: Discusses how the concept of home remains a central organizing influence in human life, even as people migrate for opportunities or to escape areas of conflict
Asks what we mean by 'home', and looks at those people who either literally have no home, or whose homes are mobile and transitory
Draws on an international array of examples to consider the full variety of human dwellings
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over seven million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Calvinism
titles.author: Jon Balserak
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Calvinism: A Very Short Introduction explores major ideas associated with the Calvinist system of thought. Beginning during the Protestant Reformation in cities like Zurich, Geneva, and Basel, Calvinism—also known as Reformed Theology—spread rapidly throughout Europe and the New World, eventually making its way to the African Continent and the East. This VSI examines how Calvinism spread and took root, helping shape church, society, and much of contemporary thought, especially Western thought, on everything from theology to civil government, economics, the arts, work and leisure, education, and the family.
