Addresses neuroscientific and developmental evidence explaining psychopathic behavior, including genetic and environmental contributors. Covers common misconceptions about psychopathy, its relationship to aggression and violence, and current scientific understanding of prevention and treatment possibilities.
highlights: Describes the personality disorder of psychopathy, dispelling common myths and misconceptions
Considers how psychopaths think and what is different about their brains, analysing what may drive their behaviour
Explores the genetic and environmental factors that can contribute to psychopathy
Discusses whether it is possible to prevent or even treat psychopathy
Part fo the Very Short Introductions series - over nine million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Philosophy of Biology
titles.author: Samir Okasha
titles.subject: Philosophy/Biology
titles.abstract: Philosophy of Biology: A Very Short Introduction outlines the core issues with which contemporary philosophers of biology grapple. Over the last forty years the philosophy of biology has emerged as an important sub-discipline of the philosophy of science. Addressing difficult conceptual issues that arise within the biological sciences, it also encompasses areas where biology has impinged on traditional philosophical questions, such as free-will, essentialism, and nature vs nurture. The book also explores topics such as the logic of Darwinian evolution; the concepts of function and design; the nature of species; and the debate over adaptationism.
titles.summaryAI: Philosophical analysis of conceptual issues in the biological sciences, covering the logic of Darwinian evolution, concepts of function and design, the nature of species, and the adaptationism debate. Addresses areas where biology intersects traditional philosophical questions including free will, essentialism, and the nature-nurture problem. Examines how philosophy of biology emerged as a major sub-discipline of philosophy of science over the past forty years, bridging evolutionary theory, genetics, and epistemological inquiry into biological knowledge and classification.
highlights: Offers an accessible introduction to the core issues in the expanding field of philosophy of biology, such as the theory of evolution by natural selection; the concepts of function and design; biological individuality; and the debate over adaptationism
Uses concrete biological examples to illustrate philosophical ideas
Concludes with important reflections on the relevance of biology for understanding human beings, human society, and our place in the natural world
Ideal for biology students interested in the philosophical foundations of their field
Part of the Very Short Introduction series - over nine million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Energy Systems
titles.author: Nick Jenkins
titles.subject: Engineering
titles.abstract: Energy Systems: A Very Short Introduction explores our historic exploitation of fossil energy resources and examines the role of renewable energy systems currently available. It also looks forward to the radical changes in fuel technology that will be necessary to provide greener, low carbon energy systems for the future. Modern societies require energy systems to provide energy for cooking, heating, transport, and materials processing, as well as for electricity generation. Energy systems include the primary fuel, its conversion, and transport to the point of use. Often, this primary fuel is still a fossil fuel, a one-use resource derived from a finite supply within our planet, causing considerable damage to the environment.
titles.summaryAI: Engineering analysis of energy systems encompassing primary fuels, conversion technologies, and distribution to end users for cooking, heating, transport, materials processing, and electricity generation. Covers historical dependence on fossil fuels and their environmental damage. Examines available renewable energy technologies and the radical technological changes needed for low-carbon energy transitions. Addresses bioenergy, hydrogen, and nuclear power as alternative fuel sources, and the relationship between energy system design, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development.
highlights: Offers an accessible overview of how we get the energy we use every day
Describes the main fuels used and how they are transported, converted into electricity, and distributed
Considers the environmental impact of our current reliance on fossil fuels
Examines the role of renewable energy systems today and looks to greener, low-carbon energy systems for the future
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million sold worldwide

titles.title: Tides
titles.author: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
titles.subject: Earth Sciences and Geography/Environment
titles.abstract: The tide is the greatest synchronized movement of matter on our planet. Every drop of seawater takes part in tidal motion, driven by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun. Tides: A Very Short Introduction blends clear explanations of well-known tidal phenomena with recent insights into the dynamics of the deep ocean and coastal seas, considering the tide’s nature and causes, its observation and prediction, and unusual tides and their relevance. It explains the importance of ocean tidal mixing as a key part of our planet’s climate-control system and for enhancing biological productivity. It also considers the effects of tidal processes beyond our own planet on the moons of Jupiter and Saturn.
titles.summaryAI: Earth science study of tidal phenomena driven by gravitational interactions of the Moon and Sun with ocean water. Covers tidal dynamics in deep oceans and coastal seas, observation and prediction methods, and unusual tidal events. Examines the role of tidal mixing in climate regulation and enhancement of marine biological productivity. Addresses tidal processes beyond Earth including effects on the moons of Jupiter and Saturn. Explores the interconnection between tidal forces, oceanography, planetary science, and climate systems.
highlights: Explains the nature and cause of the tide, its observation and prediction, unusual tides, and their relevance to us
Blends clear explanations of well known tidal phenomena with recent insights in the deep ocean and coastal seas
Considers the effects of tidal processes on the moons of Jupiter and Saturn and the possibility of tidally-heated liquid oceans with their own ecosystems
Discusses how we can study the role of the tide in the geological and biological evolution of our own planet with innovative computer models
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Topology
titles.author: Richard Earl
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Topology, the mathematical study of the properties that are preserved through the deformations, twistings, and stretchings of objects, is an important area of modern mathematics. Topology: A Very Short Introduction provides a sense of the more visual elements of topology (looking at surfaces) as well as covering the formal definition of continuity. Considering some of the eye-opening examples that led mathematicians to recognize a need for studying topology, it pays homage to the historical people, problems, and surprises that have propelled the growth of this field. As broad and fundamental as algebra and geometry, its study has important implications for science more generally, especially physics.
titles.summaryAI: Mathematical study of properties preserved under continuous deformation, including stretching and twisting of geometric objects. Covers both visual and formal aspects: surface topology, continuity, and topological invariants. Examines historical problems and figures that drove the field's development, including Euler, Cauchy, and Descartes. Addresses topology's relationship to algebra and geometry, its role as a foundational branch of modern mathematics, and its applications in physics and other sciences. Explores connections between topological concepts and Platonic solids.
highlights: Explores the mathematical field of topology, giving a sense of the visual elements of the field, as well as the formal definition of continuity
Discusses the important implications of topology, a major field of maths, for science more generally, especially physics
Considers some of the eye-opening examples that led mathematicians to recognize a need for studying topology
Pays homage to the historical people, problems, and surprises that propelled the growth of the field
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Superstition
titles.author: Stuart Vyse
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Do you touch wood for luck, or avoid hotel rooms on floor thirteen? Would you cross the path of a black cat, or step under a ladder? Despite the dominance of science in today’s world, superstitious beliefs—both traditional and new—remain surprisingly popular. Where did these superstitions come from, and why do they persist today? Superstition: A Very Short Introduction explores the nature and surprising history of superstition from antiquity to the present. It takes an exciting look at the varieties of popular superstitious beliefs today and the psychological reasons behind their continued existence, as well as the likely future course of superstition in our increasingly connected world.
titles.summaryAI: Psychological and historical study of superstitious beliefs from antiquity through the modern era, examining why irrational beliefs persist despite scientific dominance. Covers traditional superstitions such as luck rituals and omens alongside contemporary forms. Examines the historical roots of superstition in ancient Greek, Roman, and Chinese cultures. Addresses psychological mechanisms sustaining superstitious thinking, the relationship between superstition and religious belief, and the role of cultural transmission. Explores how increasing global connectivity shapes the evolution and spread of superstitious practices.
highlights: Explores the history of our deepest superstitions, and the psychological reasons behind why they persist today
Reveals the surprising connections between current everyday superstitions and the ancient world
Provides an up-to-date assessment of the psychological foundations of superstitious belief
Gives an overview of the fascinating array of contemporary superstitions throughout the world
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Country music
titles.author: Richard Carlin
titles.subject: Music
titles.abstract: Country Music: A Very Short Introduction presents an overview of country music and its impact on American culture. Country music has long been a marker of American identity; from popular culture to politics, it has provided a soundtrack to national life. While traditionally associated with the working class, country music’s appeal is far broader than any other popular music style. While this music rose from the people, it is also a product of the popular music industry, and the way the music was marketed to its audience is a key part of its story. This VSI considers country music’s roots and development, and describes key artists, songs, and musical styles.
titles.summaryAI: History and cultural analysis of country music as an American popular music genre from its folk and rural roots through its commercial development. Covers key artists, songs, and evolving musical styles. Examines the genre's associations with working-class identity and its broader appeal across American society. Addresses the music industry's role in marketing and shaping country music, its connection to national identity and politics, and its cultural significance as both grassroots expression and commercially produced popular entertainment.
highlights: Tells the story of country music from its folk origins to today's pop-country
Uses prominent songs and artists to illustrate the broader social and political context of the genre
Explores the different styles of country music, including bro-country, retro-country, and countrypolitan sound

titles.title: Korea
titles.author: Michael J. Seth
titles.subject: Geography/Politics
titles.abstract: Having spent centuries in the shadows of its neighbours China and Japan, Korea is now the object of considerable interest. Korea: A Very Short Introduction explores the history, culture, and society of a deeply divided region. It considers what it means to be Korean, and analyses how the various peoples of the Korean peninsula became one of the world’s most homogeneous nations, before exploring how this nation evolved, in a single lifetime, into today’s sharply contrasting societies of North Korea and South Korea. It also discusses how Korea fits into the larger narrative of both East Asian and world history, economically, politically, and socially.
titles.summaryAI: Historical and cultural survey of the Korean peninsula from ancient times through contemporary division into North and South Korea. Examines Korean identity formation and the development of one of the world's most homogeneous nations under the influence of neighboring China and Japan. Covers the dramatic divergence into contrasting political and economic systems within a single generation. Addresses Korea's role in East Asian and world history, including geopolitical dynamics, economic development in the South, and authoritarian isolation in the North.
highlights: Offers a short and accessible history of Korea from earliest times to the present
Places Korea within the context of East Asian and global history
Considers what it means to be Korean, and analyses how this nation evolved, in a single lifetime, into today's sharply contrasting societies
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Trigonometry
titles.author: Glen Van Brummelen
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Trigonometry: A Very Short Introduction draws together the full history of trigonometry, stretching across two millennia and several cultures such as ancient Greece, medieval India, and the Islamic world. It introduces the key concepts of trigonometry, drawing readers beyond the basic relationships first encountered in school to reveal the richness of the entire subject of trigonometry and ideas such as curved space. It also explores connections with genuine modern applications, including navigation, the analysis of music, computer graphics, and powerful modelling tools in science, and shows how trigonometry has participated in big questions about the world, including the shape of the universe and the nature of infinity.
titles.summaryAI: History and applications of trigonometry spanning two millennia across ancient Greek, medieval Indian, and Islamic mathematical traditions. Covers fundamental trigonometric concepts, relationships, and their extension into advanced topics including non-Euclidean geometry and curved space. Examines practical applications in navigation, music analysis, computer graphics, and scientific modeling. Addresses contributions of key mathematicians including Napier and Viete, connections to questions about the shape of the universe and the nature of infinity, and trigonometry's role in the broader development of mathematics.
highlights: Draws together the full history of trigonometry, stretching across two millennia and several cultures
Introduces the key concepts of trigonometry, drawing readers beyond the basic relationships first encountered in school to ideas such as curved space
Explores connections with genuine modern applications, including navigation, the analysis of music, and computer graphics
Shows how trigonometry has participated in big questions about the world, including the shape of the universe and the nature of infinity
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Niels Bohr
titles.author: J. L. Heilbron
titles.subject: History of science/Science/Biography
titles.abstract: Niels Bohr: A Very Short Introduction covers the life and work of the pioneer of the quantum theory of the atom who ranks with Einstein in importance for the development of modern physics. In addition to his role as a scientist, Bohr was a statesman and Danish cultural icon, who built scientific institutions and pushed for the extension of international cooperation to all nation states. He also had deep interests in philosophy, literature, and humanism. This VSI considers how all of these aspects of Bohr’s personality influenced his ground-breaking work.
titles.summaryAI: Scientific biography of Niels Bohr, pioneer of the quantum theory of the atom and figure of comparable importance to Einstein in twentieth-century physics. Covers his groundbreaking contributions to atomic structure, quantum mechanics, and the Copenhagen interpretation. Examines Bohr's roles as scientific institution builder, advocate for international cooperation among nation states, and Danish cultural figure. Addresses his philosophical interests, engagement with literature and humanism, and how his broader intellectual commitments shaped his approach to physics.
highlights: Covers the life and work of one of the most creative physicists of the 20th century, Niels Bohr, who pioneered the quantum theory of the atom
Considers how Bohr's work was influenced by his philosophy and humanist concerns
Alongside Bohr as a scientist, discusses his role as a statesman, institution builder, and Danish cultural icon
Offers the first overview in English that draws on all the available sources, which the author has had a hand in uncovering
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Albert Camus
titles.author: Oliver Gloag
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Albert Camus is one of the best known philosophers of the twentieth century, as well as a widely read novelist. Active in the first half of the twentieth century, his views contributed to the rise of absurdism philosophy, and his works have inspired numerous movies and are frequently referenced in contemporary politics. Albert Camus: A Very Short Introduction explores the life and work of a man full of contradictions, who occupied an ambiguous position in troubled and conflicted times. Following a broad chronological framework, it explores the major philosophical and literary works of Camus and analyses how the reception and popularity of these works are connected with contemporary political, social, and cultural issues.
titles.summaryAI: Literary and philosophical biography of Albert Camus, twentieth-century French-Algerian novelist and philosopher associated with absurdism. Covers major works including fiction and philosophical essays, examining themes of the absurd, revolt, and moral ambiguity. Addresses Camus's ambiguous position during the Algerian War and other political conflicts. Examines the reception and contemporary relevance of his works in politics and culture, his relationships with Sartre and other intellectuals, and the connections between his literary output and broader social and philosophical movements.
highlights: Explores the life and work of Albert Camus, major cultural icon of the twentieth century
Offers a comprehensive view of Camus' major works and interventions including his notion of the absurd and revolt
Analyses the enduring popularity of Camus in connection with contemporary political, social, and cultural issues
Part of the Very Short Introduction Series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Federalism
titles.author: Mark J. Rozell, Clyde Wilcox
titles.subject: Politics
titles.abstract: Federalism: A Very Short Introduction provides a concise overview of the principles and operations of federalism, the political system defined by power sharing between a national government and its subnational units, from its origins and evolution to the key events and constitutional decisions that have defined its framework. While the primary focus is on the United States, a comparative analysis of other federal systems, including those of Australia, Brazil, Canada, India, Nigeria, and Switzerland, is provided. The role of federal government is explained alongside the critical roles of state and local governments. This VSI also examines whether federal structures are viable in an era of increasingly centralized and authoritarian-style government.
titles.summaryAI: Comparative political analysis of federalism as a system of power-sharing between national and subnational governments. Primary focus on the United States with comparisons to Australia, Brazil, Canada, India, Nigeria, and Switzerland. Covers the origins, constitutional framework, and evolution of federal systems, including roles of state and local governments. Examines taxation, devolution, and policy variation across jurisdictions. Addresses whether federal structures remain viable amid trends toward governmental centralization and authoritarian governance styles globally.
highlights: A concise introduction to the principles and operations of federalism
Explores federalism on a global scale, from the United States to Brazil, South Africa, India, and the EU
Provides comparative analysis of federal systems around the world
Examines whether federal structures are viable in an era of increasingly centralized and authoritarian style government

titles.title: Aerial Warfare
titles.author: Frank Ledwidge
titles.subject: Military/History
titles.abstract: Aerial warfare has dominated war-making for over 100 years, and despite regular announcements of its demise, it shows no sign of becoming obsolete. Aerial Warfare: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of aerial warfare, introducing the major battles, crises, and controversies where air power has taken centre stage, and the changes in technology and air power capabilities over time. Highlighting the role played by air power in the First and Second World Wars, it also sheds light on the lesser-known theatres where the roles of air forces have been clearly decisive in conflicts in Africa, South America, and Asia. It also considers whether there is a future for manned air power.
titles.summaryAI: Military history of aerial warfare from the Wright brothers' era through modern conflicts, spanning over a century of air power development. Covers major air campaigns in both World Wars, the Cold War, and lesser-known conflicts in Africa, South America, and Asia. Examines technological evolution in aircraft, weapons systems, and air power capabilities. Addresses strategic debates about the decisiveness of air power, the relationship between air forces and ground operations, and the future of manned versus unmanned aerial combat.
highlights: Offers a concise history of air warfare, analysing its influence on wars, campaigns and conflicts from the pre-WW1 period to today and beyond
Considers how military operations from the air impact upon political decisions on the ground
Discusses the advances in air power technology and capabilities, and the impact this has on warfare
Part of the Very Short Introduction series - over 10 million copies sold worldwide
Previously published in hardback as Aerial Warfare: The Battle for the Skies

titles.title: Renewable Energy
titles.author: Nick Jelley
titles.subject: Engineering
titles.abstract: Energy is vital for a good standard of living, and affordable sources of power that do not cause climate change or pollution are crucial. Renewable energy provides the answer, and this Very Short Introduction describes the main sources—solar PV, wind, hydropower, and biomass—their innovative technologies, and what each could deliver. It also discusses the relatively small contributions expected from tidal, wave, geothermal, and nuclear power, and from carbon capture and storage. The book shows how the variability of solar PV and wind power can be handled in an electricity grid predominantly powered by renewables, and it discusses recent innovations in batteries and other energy storage technologies. It explains the importance of decarbonizing the huge global heat demand by using heat pumps, electricity directly, and hydrogen from electrolysers; and of changing to electric vehicles. Electrification will require approximately tripling the supply of electricity, and this can be met predominantly by solar PV and wind power. The learning effect has resulted in a dramatic drop in their price in the last decade, making them the cheapest generators of electricity in most parts of the world. This could make powering the world by renewables considerably cheaper than continuing to burn fossil fuels. The book explores the challenges in achieving this—notably, geopolitical tensions, lack of grid infrastructure, permitting (planning) issues, the availability of finance in parts of the Global South, and the vested interests in fossil fuels—and ends by discussing the actions needed.
titles.summaryAI: Engineering and policy analysis of renewable energy sources including solar photovoltaics, wind power, hydropower, and biomass, with discussion of tidal, wave, geothermal, and nuclear contributions. Covers electricity grid management with variable renewable inputs, battery storage technologies, and electrification of heat and transport through heat pumps and electric vehicles. Examines learning-curve-driven cost reductions making renewables the cheapest electricity source globally. Addresses decarbonization challenges including geopolitical tensions, grid infrastructure gaps, permitting barriers, and fossil fuel industry resistance.
highlights: Describes the main renewable sources of energy - solar, wind, hydropower, biomass, geothermal, tidal, and wave
Explains the reasons for and importance of the rapid deployment of solar PV and wind power and how their variability can be managed
Discusses the contributions and recent innovations in all the principal renewable and low-carbon sources of energy and associated technologies, including batteries, heat pumps, and electrolysers
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide
newToThisEdition: Up-to-date quantitative information on the topic of renewable energy which in the last five years has expanded significantly, notably with the agreement at COP28 to triple renewable energy capacity by 2030

titles.title: Prohibition
titles.author: W. J. Rorabaugh
titles.subject: Criminology/Politics/History - U.S.
titles.abstract: Prohibition: A Very Short Introduction traces the origins of prohibition back to the evangelical-based voluntary abstinence temperance movement in the early 1800s. It makes clear that public support for prohibition collapsed due to gangster violence and the need for local, state, and federal government alcohol revenue during the Great Depression. Americans have always been a hard-drinking people, but from 1920 to 1933 the country went dry. After decades of pressure from rural Protestants, the states ratified the Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution meaning alcohol could no longer be produced, imported, transported, or sold. How and why did prohibition come about? How did it work? And how did prohibition give way to strict governmental regulation of alcohol?
titles.summaryAI: History of alcohol prohibition in the United States from the early nineteenth-century evangelical temperance movement through the Eighteenth Amendment era (1920-1933) and its repeal. Covers the social, religious, and political forces driving the prohibition movement, particularly rural Protestant activism. Examines the consequences of prohibition including gangster violence and the collapse of public support. Addresses the role of the Great Depression in creating government need for alcohol tax revenue, and the transition from outright prohibition to strict governmental regulation of alcohol production and sales.
highlights: A short and engaging synthesis of Prohibition that illuminates its deep impact on American history, culture, and law from the 1920s to the present
Traces the origins of Prohibition back to the evangelical-based voluntary abstinence temperance movement in the early 1800s
Makes clear that public support for Prohibition collapsed due to gangster violence and the need for local, state, and federal government alcohol revenue during the Great Depression

titles.title: The Abrahamic Religions
titles.author: Charles L. Cohen
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Connected by their mutual—if differentiated—veneration of the One God proclaimed by Abraham, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam compose a family of related traditions. The Abrahamic Religions: A Very Short Introduction explores their intertwined histories and the ways in which encounters among their adherents have helped construct their own independent religious identities from antiquity to the present. Those identities have not been fixed and static, but have rather reflected particular historical contexts. The political arrangements in which the religions emerged and intermingled—notably, their changing relationships to state power—have figured importantly in their development. The common heritages of the Abrahamic religions have both brought them together and divided them.
titles.summaryAI: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam as interrelated monotheistic traditions tracing shared lineage to Abraham. Covers their intertwined histories from antiquity to the present, examining how mutual encounters shaped independent religious identities. Addresses the role of state power and political arrangements in each tradition's development. Explores how common heritage simultaneously unites and divides these faiths, with identities reflecting particular historical contexts rather than remaining fixed. Spans theological commonalities, differentiated veneration practices, and shifting relationships between religious authority and political governance across centuries.
highlights: An exploration of the intertwined histories of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam over three thousand years
Emphasizes the continuing interactions between Jews, Christians, and Muslims
Demonstrates the importance of political context to the development of the Abrahamic religions

titles.title: Systems Biology
titles.author: Eberhard O. Voit
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Systems biology came about as growing numbers of scientists and engineers from different fields created experimental methods and algorithms that supported the analysis of very large quantities of biological data. Systems Biology: A Very Short Introduction outlines the exciting processes and possibilities in this new field. It describes how modern biology enabled us to learn how intricately the expression of every gene is controlled, how signalling systems keep organisms running smoothly, how complicated even the simplest cells are, and how computational methods may help us understand these complex systems of life. It explores what this field is about, why it is needed, and how it will affect our understanding of life, particularly in the areas of personalized medicine, drug development, food and energy production, and sustainable stewardship of our environments.
titles.summaryAI: Interdisciplinary field integrating computational methods, experimental biology, and engineering to analyze large-scale biological data. Covers gene expression regulation, cellular signaling systems, and cellular complexity. Examines how systems-level approaches illuminate emergent properties of living organisms that reductionist methods cannot capture. Applications include personalized medicine, drug development, food and energy production, and environmental sustainability. Draws on techniques such as flow cytometry, mass spectrometry, and epigenetics. Bridges molecular biology, bioinformatics, and mathematical modeling to understand complex biological networks.
highlights: Outlines the exciting processes and possibilities in the new field of systems biology
Explores what this field is about, why it is needed, and how it will affect our understanding of life
Considers the new tools utilized by systems biology, and the insights and advances it has already delivered
Part of the Very Short introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Reconstruction
titles.author: Allen C. Guelzo
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: Reconstruction: A Very Short Introduction delves into the constitutional, political, and social issues behind Reconstruction to describe a historical moment that left an indelible mark on American social fabric. The Reconstruction era was one of the unhappiest times in American history. It succeeded in reuniting the nation politically after the Civil War, but it failed to chart a progressive course for race relations after the abolition of slavery and rise of Jim Crow, and struggled to successfully manage the Southern resistance towards a Northern, free-labor pattern. Despite this, there were some notable accomplishments: the 14th and 15th Amendments to the Constitution and the election of the first African American representatives to the US Congress.
titles.summaryAI: The Reconstruction era in the United States following the Civil War, approximately 1865 to 1877. Covers constitutional changes including the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments, the political reintegration of seceded Southern states, and the failure to establish progressive race relations after abolition. Examines the rise of Jim Crow laws, Southern resistance to Northern free-labor economic patterns, and the election of the first African American congressional representatives. Addresses tensions between federal authority and state sovereignty during national reunification.
highlights: Written by the leading authority on Civil War, Reconstruction, and Lincoln, and three-time winner of the Lincoln Prize
Provides a new perspective on Reconstruction, showing that it did not take place only in the South, but also in the West
Discusses other "reconstructions" that occurred in parallel with the political one: in philosophy, literature, law, and economy

titles.title: Number theory
titles.author: Robin Wilson
titles.subject: Mathematics
titles.abstract: Number Theory: A Very Short Introduction explains the branch of mathematics primarily concerned with the counting numbers, 1, 2, 3, …. Long considered one of the most ‘beautiful’ areas of mathematics, number theory dates back over two millennia to the Ancient Greeks, but has seen some startling new developments in recent years. Trailblazers in the field include mathematicians Euclid of Alexandria, Pierre de Fermat, Leonhard Euler, and Carl Friedrich Gauss. Number theory has intrigued and attracted amateurs and professionals alike for thousands of years, appearing in both recreational contexts (puzzles) and practical concerns (cryptography). Some problems in number theory are easy, whereas others are notorious with no known solutions to date.
titles.summaryAI: Branch of pure mathematics focused on properties and relationships of counting numbers. Traces historical development from Ancient Greek mathematics through contributions of Euclid, Fermat, Euler, and Gauss to modern developments. Covers prime numbers, divisibility, congruences, and unsolved problems. Addresses both recreational aspects including mathematical puzzles and practical applications in cryptography. Distinguishes between accessible elementary problems and notoriously difficult open conjectures. Spans over two millennia of mathematical inquiry bridging amateur and professional engagement with number-theoretic questions.
highlights: Introduces the main areas of classical number theory, both ancient and modern
Draws on much historical material to present the content firmly within a historical context
Highlights a range of applications, from the practical (cryptography) to the recreational (puzzles)
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Smell
titles.author: Matthew Cobb
titles.subject: Medicine
titles.abstract: Smell: A Very Short Introduction is an exploration of the science and physiology of smell and its historical, cultural, and environmental significance. What happens in our brains when we smell something? How do human olfactory processes differ from those of mammals, birds, and insects? Is there a link between smell and genetics? The connection between smell and memory is more than a literary conceit, with smells proving more effective than images at unlocking memories. The same odour can have different meanings to different people. Smells themselves are often blends, and our reactions to them are influenced by our memories and cultural conditioning.
titles.summaryAI: The science and physiology of olfaction across species, covering neural processing of odors in the brain, comparative olfactory systems in mammals, birds, and insects, and genetic underpinnings of smell perception. Examines the strong neurological link between smell and memory retrieval, demonstrating olfactory cues surpass visual ones for memory access. Addresses cultural conditioning and individual variation in odor interpretation, the blended chemical nature of smells, and historical and environmental significance of olfaction. Bridges neuroscience, genetics, sensory biology, and cultural anthropology.
highlights: Summarises the latest neurobiological research on smell, in humans and other mammals, as well as in insects and fish
Discusses how our genes determine what we can and cannot smell, and why some people like a given smell and others do not
Explores how animals use smell to navigate and communicate
Considers the future of smell in a world of robots and climate change
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Sun
titles.author: Philip Judge
titles.subject: Astronomy
titles.abstract: The Sun: A Very Short Introduction explores the science, structure, origins, workings, and future evolution of the Sun and solar physics, and its impact on life on earth. What are sunspots, and why do they form? What dynamic forces govern the Sun’s magnetic fields? Has the Sun grown brighter, smaller, or slower with age? Solar physics remains an observationally driven subject and determining cause and effect can be hampered by the Sun’s complexity. However, studying the Sun has led to remarkable discoveries in astronomy and basic physics, and recent technological developments and missions—some of which are already being deployed—will almost certainly improve our knowledge of the Sun, our nearest star.
titles.summaryAI: Solar physics covering the Sun's internal structure, origins, dynamic processes, and future evolution as a main-sequence star. Examines sunspot formation, solar magnetic field dynamics, and changes in brightness and size over the Sun's lifetime. Addresses the Sun's impact on Earth and life. Discusses observational methods and technological developments in solar missions. Highlights contributions of solar research to broader discoveries in astronomy and fundamental physics. Covers challenges of determining causation in a complex stellar system driven by magnetic and thermal processes.
highlights: Explains how the Sun works including its physics, and structure, origins, and future evolution
Considers the importance of the Sun for life on Earth, and its impact on our climate
Shows how studying the Sun has led to remarkable discoveries in astronomy and basic physics
Discusses what we still have yet to discover about our nearest star
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Émile Zola
titles.author: Brian Nelson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Émile Zola was a 19th-century novelist and social commentator, and the leader of the literary movement known as ‘naturalism’. Émile Zola: A Very Short Introduction explores key themes in his life and work, looking in detail at several major novels from his twenty-volume Rougon-Macquart cycle. His novels examine the changing cultural landscape of the late 19th century, creating an epic sense of social transformation. In so doing, they opened the novel up to a new realm of subjects, and they embodied a new freedom of expression in their depiction. Zola was often accused of sensationalism and vulgarity; his English publisher Henry Vizetelly was jailed on charges of obscenity.
titles.summaryAI: Nineteenth-century French novelist who led the literary naturalism movement. Focuses on major works from the twenty-volume Rougon-Macquart cycle examining social transformation in late nineteenth-century France. Covers themes of industrialization, changing cultural landscapes, and new freedoms of literary expression. Addresses accusations of sensationalism and obscenity, including the imprisonment of English publisher Henry Vizetelly. Situates Zola within the broader context of realist fiction and the expansion of novelistic subject matter. Explores connections between literary innovation and social commentary in Third Republic France.
highlights: Examines Émile Zola's distinctive place in the history of the European novel
Explores how Zola opened the novel up to a new realm of subjects and how his work embodies a new freedom of expression in their depiction
Situates Zola's work in its social and political contexts
Considers Zola's art criticism, and his role in the Dreyfus Affair, as well as his fiction
Part of the Very Short introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Fire
titles.author: Andrew C. Scott
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Fire: A Very Short Introduction considers fire’s four-hundred-million-year history, its chemical composition, its role in human development, and its different meanings, from heat and comfort to death and destruction. Fires in buildings regularly make the headlines, and news of wildfires now reaches our computer and smartphone screens. Urban and pastoral attitudes to fire can differ and formulating fire suppression policies can be complex. Two things have fundamentally altered our understanding: increased knowledge about fire in the deep time before human evolution, and the growth of satellite technology, which has transformed how we observe fire. In the context of our changing climate, an improved understanding of fire worldwide is urgently needed.
titles.summaryAI: Fire as a physical, ecological, and cultural phenomenon spanning four hundred million years of Earth history. Covers the chemistry of combustion, wildfire behavior, and fire's role in human evolution and development. Examines urban and rural perspectives on fire management, the complexity of suppression policies, and building fires. Addresses how deep-time geological evidence and satellite technology have transformed understanding of fire dynamics. Discusses fire ecology in the context of climate change, distinguishing between natural wildfire regimes and anthropogenic fire use across different landscapes and time periods.
highlights: Covers the fundamentals of fire, both wild and under human control, including the basics of ignition, combustion, and fuel
Describes the 400-million-year history of fire on our planet, and the environmental and human impact of wildfires
Discusses the role of fire in human development
Considers the problem of fires in urban settings, including new ways to prevent fire damage
Explores approaches to managing wildfires near human settlements
Part of the Very Short Introduction series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The U.S. Civil War
titles.author: Louis P. Masur
titles.subject: History – U.S.
titles.abstract: The US Civil War: A Very Short Introduction covers a period in American history characterized by decades of intensifying conflict over slavery and government authority, culminating in Abraham Lincoln’s election and eleven states seceding from the Union. The Civil War began as a limited conflict with the aim of restoring the Union. It became a diffuse, violent war that lasted four years, claimed hundreds of thousands of lives, and ultimately led to the abolition of slavery and a vigorous debate over the terms by which the seceded states would be restored to the nation. This VSI ends with a chapter on the aftermath of the war and the remaking of America.
titles.summaryAI: The American Civil War from antebellum tensions over slavery and federal authority through Abraham Lincoln's election, secession of eleven states, four years of combat, and the war's aftermath. Covers the transformation from a limited conflict aimed at restoring the Union into a total war claiming hundreds of thousands of lives. Examines the abolition of slavery as a wartime outcome, debates over terms of Confederate state reintegration, and the remaking of American national identity. Addresses military, political, constitutional, and social dimensions of the conflict.
highlights: The only short history of the Civil War on the market.
Written by a popular and prolific American historian.
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - millions of copies sold worldwide

titles.title: American Business History
titles.author: Walter A. Friedman
titles.subject: History – U.S./Economics
titles.abstract: American Business History: A Very Short Introduction looks at the rise of the American economy from its colonial and frontier beginnings. What made the United States an attractive testing ground for entrepreneurs? How did the United States come to have the largest business enterprises in the world by the early twentieth century? Why did business organizations gain a central place in American society? This VSI answers these questions through examining the formation of new organizational structures; the invention and commercialization of new technologies; the introduction of policymaking and regulatory frameworks; and the stories of entrepreneurs from around the world who embraced democratic capitalism in America.
titles.summaryAI: The development of American business enterprise from colonial and frontier origins to the early twentieth century when the United States possessed the world's largest corporations. Covers formation of new organizational structures, commercialization of technologies, and regulatory and policymaking frameworks. Examines why the United States attracted entrepreneurs from around the world and how business organizations gained central social prominence. Addresses the relationship between democratic capitalism, innovation, and economic growth. Spans topics including entrepreneurship, corporate evolution, and the intersection of commerce with American political culture.
highlights: Devotes significant attention to the connections between business strategy and culture
Provides a different interpretation of the growth of the U.S. economy by focusing on economic aggregates rather than on firms and entrepreneurs
Can be used in MBA programs and American economic history classes

titles.title: Dementia
titles.author: Kathleen Taylor
titles.subject: Medicine/Psychology
titles.abstract: Dementia: A Very Short Introduction explains how dementia is diagnosed, its different types and symptoms, and its effects on sufferers and their families. Why is dementia resistant to treatment? Why has the most successful scientific hypothesis not led to a cure? Are there variations between different countries, and given the rise in the ageing population, are there more or fewer cases than we think? This VSI looks at the history of dementia research and examines the genetic, physiological, and environmental risk factors and how individuals might reduce them. It also investigates developments in diagnosis and symptom management, and the economic and political context of dementia care.
titles.summaryAI: Neurodegenerative conditions characterized by cognitive decline, covering diagnosis, types, symptoms, and effects on patients and families. Examines genetic, physiological, and environmental risk factors and individual risk reduction strategies. Addresses the history of dementia research, why leading scientific hypotheses have not produced cures, and treatment resistance. Covers developments in diagnostic methods and symptom management. Discusses cross-country variations in prevalence, implications of aging populations, and the economic and political context of dementia care. Bridges neuroscience, geriatric medicine, public health policy, and caregiving.
highlights: Offers a clear guide to dementia, covering its history and its definition, different types and their symptoms, diagnosis and treatment, and the underlying science
Explains why we still have no cure for dementia, and looks at current research which could soon change that
Discusses both the human costs of brain disorders, and also their costs to society
Looks at risk factors, and how to lower our chances of succumbing to dementia
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Canada
titles.author: Donald Wright
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Canada: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of Canada’s history, and its many political and cultural achievements. Canada has never had a single national identity and is linguistically, ethnically, and religiously diverse. From the first European settlers onward, development and industrial growth were often at the expense of indigenous groups. The late 20th century saw a cultural explosion and the emergence of distinct nationalisms within Canada, particularly Quebec. Canada’s proximity to America has necessitated a diplomatic approach but Canada has used its peacekeeping strengths to further its position as a world power. Canada’s lack of a single identity is arguably a strength, not a weakness.
titles.summaryAI: Canadian history from early European settlement to the contemporary era, covering political development, cultural diversity, and national identity formation. Examines linguistic, ethnic, and religious pluralism as defining features. Addresses the impact of industrialization and development on indigenous peoples, the late twentieth-century cultural explosion, and distinct nationalisms including Quebec separatism. Covers Canada's diplomatic relationship with the United States, its peacekeeping role in international affairs, and its emergence as a world power. Argues that the absence of a single national identity constitutes a strength.
highlights: Draws on history, politics, and literature to introduce the identities and realities of modern Canada
Explores how Canada maintains national diversity through bilingualism, multiculturalism, and federalism
Discusses the ongoing reconciliation with Canada's Indigenous peoples
Considers Canada's historical relationship to Great Britain, and its ongoing relationship with the United States
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: French Philosophy
titles.author: Stephen Gaukroger, Knox Peden
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: French Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction outlines the history of French philosophy, summarising important thinkers and texts. Philosophy has played a significant role in French culture since the early modern period, and French philosophy has influenced the Western world for centuries. The Enlightenment was central to the relationship between philosophy and politics. Nineteenth-century French philosophy was focused on ideas of science and progress. In the twentieth century, existentialist ideas took hold, followed by an embrace of structuralism which led to post-structuralism and deconstruction. French philosophy is interwoven with political, literary, and scientific debates of the time, and ongoing conflicts between religious and secular beliefs.
titles.summaryAI: History of French philosophical thought from the early modern period through the twentieth century. Covers Montaigne, Enlightenment thinkers, and the relationship between philosophy and politics. Examines nineteenth-century focus on science and progress, twentieth-century existentialism, structuralism, post-structuralism, and deconstruction. Addresses how French philosophy interweaves with political, literary, and scientific debates and ongoing tensions between religious and secular worldviews. Highlights France's distinctive philosophical contributions to Western intellectual culture and the interplay between philosophical movements and broader cultural transformations.
highlights: Covers the whole period of French philosophy from its origins in the sixteenth century up to the present
Analyses the history of French philosophy in its social, political, and cultural context
Offers a corrective to narrow treatments of post-war French philosophy
Provides introductions to philosophers just now beginning to acquire a readership in English
Avoids a sectarian treatment of movements within contemporary French philosophy, showing how each plays a role in maintaining the distinct character of French philosophy, past and present
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Russian Economy
titles.author: Richard Connolly
titles.subject: Economics
titles.abstract: The Russian Economy: A Very Short Introduction looks at the historical roots of an economy dominated by the state and shaped by a need for security. The Soviet Union’s centrally planned economic system enabled industrialization, urbanization, and military success, but at what human cost? The transition to a market-based system in the late 20th century was difficult, and only partially successful. From the millennium onwards, Vladimir Putin’s economic policy emerged as a hybrid of state- and market-controlled approaches. Russia has been criticized for overdependence on natural resources and armaments. However, if some world powers can combine state control and a profile in the global markets, why not Russia?
titles.summaryAI: Russian economic development from the Soviet centrally planned system through post-Soviet transition to the contemporary hybrid economy under Vladimir Putin. Covers industrialization, urbanization, and military-industrial growth under Soviet planning, including human costs. Examines the difficult and partial transition to market economics in the 1990s. Addresses Russia's dependence on natural resources and armaments, state-controlled versus market-driven approaches, and Russia's position among world powers. Explores whether combining state economic control with global market participation represents a viable development model.
highlights: Provides a balanced analysis of the different features of the Russian economy, which is one of the largest in the world
Provides a historically-rooted account of the development of the Russian economy since the imperial era
Uses comparative data to show where Russia's economy is located in a global context
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide
newToThisEdition: Highlights the profound impact on the Russian economy and society more broadly from the Covid-19 pandemic
Discusses the huge changes in the structure of the economy caused by the decision to launch a full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022
Considers the imposition of Western (and allied) sanctions on Russia

titles.title: Biogeography
titles.author: Mark V. Lomolino
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Biogeography: A Very Short Introduction explains how our ability to place life in an explicit geographic context is key to understanding our natural world. The geological evolution of Earth has fundamentally influenced its life forms. Biogeography brings together insights from the fields of genetics, geology, paleontology, geography, anthropology, meteorology, oceanography, and ecology to demonstrate how the geological and evolutionary histories of our planet are interconnected. The tools of biogeography are essential for us to develop effective strategies for conserving life across our planet. Through examining the diversity and locations of life forms, biogeography provides an unparalleled opportunity to explore the “great lessons of the earth.”
titles.summaryAI: The study of geographic distribution of life forms across Earth, integrating genetics, geology, paleontology, geography, anthropology, meteorology, oceanography, and ecology. Examines how geological evolution has fundamentally shaped biodiversity patterns. Covers island biogeography, continental drift effects on species distribution, and interconnections between evolutionary and geological histories. Addresses conservation applications, demonstrating how biogeographic tools inform strategies for preserving biodiversity. Explores the diversity and spatial patterns of organisms to understand ecological and evolutionary processes at planetary scales.
highlights: Explains how our ability to place life in an explicit geographic context is key to understanding the natural world
Shows how the tools of biogeography are essential for us to develop effective strategies for conserving the great menagerie of life across our planet
Explains how the geological and evolutionary history of our planet are interconnected
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Ghetto
titles.author: Bryan Cheyette
titles.subject: History/Human Geography
titles.abstract: The Ghetto: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of the history of the ghetto, focusing on specific times and places throughout history. Is the ghetto real or imagined? The word ‘ghetto’ would not have existed without 16th-century Italian economic interests, which led to the policy of placing the Jewish population in enclosed enclaves in around twenty-four Italian towns and cities. During the Holocaust, there were many hundreds of Nazi ghettos in Eastern Europe varying in size, duration, and purpose. The history of the ghetto was adopted by African-Americans in the mid-20th century and is still used to describe both the concrete and abstract qualities of segregated urban life. There is no single idea or place which encompasses ‘the ghetto’.
titles.summaryAI: History of the ghetto as both a physical space and a conceptual category from sixteenth-century Italy to the present. Covers the origins in Italian economic policies confining Jewish populations to enclosed enclaves in approximately twenty-four towns. Examines the hundreds of Nazi ghettos in Eastern Europe during the Holocaust, varying in size, duration, and purpose. Addresses the adoption of ghetto terminology by African Americans in the mid-twentieth century to describe segregated urban conditions. Explores the ghetto as simultaneously real and imagined, encompassing concrete and abstract dimensions of urban segregation.
highlights: Covers the varying histories of different kinds of ghettos - urban, racial, and colonial
Considers the identity of the people in ghettos, and why and how ghetto communities are formed
Analyses the appearance of ghettos in popular culture
Shows how both the experience of ghettoization and the memory of ghettos impacts people living in them
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Ecology
titles.author: Jaboury Ghazoul
titles.subject: Biology
titles.abstract: Ecology is the science of how organisms interact with each other and their environment to form communities and ecosystems. Ecology: A Very Short Introduction explains the history of ecology, the principles of ecological thinking, how ecology affects our everyday lives, and how it guides environmental decisions, especially in the light of current and future environmental challenges. What are the factors behind ‘boom and bust’ cycles in species populations? How and why do two species cooperate? Do humans need so many species? The cultural significance of ecology is also explored, with examples of different schools of thought that envisage ecology as a science and a worldview.
titles.summaryAI: The science of organism interactions with each other and their environments, forming communities and ecosystems. Covers ecological principles including population dynamics, boom-and-bust cycles, interspecies cooperation, and biodiversity value. Examines the history of ecological thought, how ecology informs environmental policy and everyday decisions, and current and future environmental challenges. Addresses the cultural significance of ecology as both a scientific discipline and a worldview, including different schools of ecological thought. Bridges organismal biology, community ecology, ecosystem science, and conservation biology.
highlights: Presents key ecological principles in the context of current environmental challenges
Demonstrates ecological concepts with a lively range of examples
Discusses the evolution of ecological thinking over time
Considers the role of ecology in tackling future environmental issues
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: George Bernard Shaw
titles.author: Christopher Wixson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: George Bernard Shaw: A Very Short Introduction provides an accessible foundation for those discovering George Bernard Shaw’s writing for the first time. The verbosity and scope of Shaw’s writing can at times feel daunting. With a canon of plays astonishing in size, breadth, and ambition, Shaw is considered the second greatest playwright in English behind William Shakespeare. The range and popularity of his work as journalist, essayist, and polemicist as well as playwright made him one of the world’s most recognizable public intellectuals and literary figures. Charting the contours of what constitutes ‘Shavian’, this VSI traces the creative evolution of core themes and styles over Shaw’s long career.
titles.summaryAI: George Bernard Shaw as playwright, journalist, essayist, and public intellectual in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Covers the breadth and evolution of his dramatic canon, considered second only to Shakespeare in English-language theatre. Examines core Shavian themes and stylistic development across his long career. Addresses Shaw's roles as social critic and polemicist, his influence on modern drama, and his status as one of the world's most recognizable literary figures. Situates his work within British and Irish theatrical and intellectual traditions.
highlights: Takes a chronological approach through the life and works of George Bernard Shaw
Provides an overview of the evolution of Shaw's writing across his plays, essays, and public pieces
Highlights the thematic and stylistic choices that connect Shaw's many works
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Philosophical Method
titles.author: Timothy Williamson
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: What is philosophy and what are philosophers trying to achieve? Philosophical Method: A Very Short Introduction looks at the history of philosophy, including examples from history charting the successes and failures of philosophical thinking. Themes explored in detail include philosophy’s relationship to mathematics and science, common sense and its misinterpretations, the role of debate in the search for truth, and the importance of thought experiments to philosophical arguments. This VSI provides a contemporary look at philosophical methodology, asking if philosophy is always an ‘armchair-based’ discipline or if real-life thought experiments can help us solve philosophical problems.
titles.summaryAI: The methodology of philosophical inquiry, examining how philosophers approach problems and what constitutes philosophical progress. Covers the history of philosophical methods, including successes and failures. Examines philosophy's relationship to mathematics and science, the role of common sense and its misinterpretations, debate as a truth-seeking mechanism, and thought experiments in philosophical argumentation. Addresses whether philosophy is purely theoretical or whether empirical and real-life thought experiments contribute to solving philosophical problems. Bridges epistemology, philosophy of science, and metaPhilosophy.
highlights: Assuming no previous knowledge of philosophy, this is a highly accesible account of how modern philosophers think and work
Presents a distinctive view of philosophy, arguing that it is far more scientific than many philosophers think
Includes a wealth of examples from history charting the successes and failures of philosophical thinking
Offers a timely and much needed intervention in the current hot debate on philosophical methodology
Written by one of Britain's leading philosophers
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold wordlwide
Previously published in hardback as Doing Philosophy

titles.title: Soft Matter
titles.author: Tom McLeish
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Soft Matter: A Very Short Introduction explores the field of soft matter, looking beneath the appearances of matter into its inner structure. Drawing on physics, chemistry, mathematics, and engineering, soft matter science links fundamental scientific ideas to everyday phenomena such as ‘inkiness’ and ‘stickiness’, with a rich history and philosophy. It studies materials such as polymers, colloids, liquid crystals, and foams. This VSI shows how Brownian Motion—the random molecular motion underlying ‘heat’—is an underpinning principle of soft matter. From hair conditioners to honey, it discusses how common characteristics of these materials shape their behaviour and applications.
titles.summaryAI: The physics of soft matter, studying materials including polymers, colloids, liquid crystals, and foams. Covers how Brownian motion serves as an underpinning principle, linking molecular-scale thermal motion to macroscopic material behavior. Draws on physics, chemistry, mathematics, and engineering to connect fundamental concepts to everyday phenomena like viscosity and adhesion. Examines the inner structure beneath material appearances, the historical contributions of Boltzmann and Einstein, and applications ranging from consumer products to industrial materials. Bridges statistical mechanics, condensed matter physics, and materials science.
highlights: Introduces the still relatively young field of soft matter - the study of materials such as polymers, colloids, liquid crystals, and foams
Examines the inner structure of these forms of matter and the shared physical properties and behaviours that emerge from that structure
Shows how the field of soft matter brings together physics, chemistry, engineering, and mathematics
Highlights the important industrial applications of soft matter
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Ovid
titles.author: Llewelyn Morgan
titles.subject: Classical Studies/Biography
titles.abstract: Ovid: A Very Short Introduction discusses Ovid’s poetry, and the social and cultural context in which it was written. No poet of the Graeco-Roman world has had a deeper impact on subsequent literature and art than Ovid. But he was also a man of his time, and while the poetry he wrote still speaks to us today, it channels the cultural and political upheavals that Rome in his day was experiencing: its public life under Rome’s first emperor Augustus, changing sexual mores, religion, literary debt to Greece, and urban landscape. This VSI introduces Ovid’s poetry on love, heroic women, metamorphosis, Roman festivals, and his own exile by Augustus. It also explores his immense influence on later literature and art, an uninterrupted popularity through the Middle Ages and into modern times. Artists as diverse as Chaucer, Goethe, and Dali are all his heirs. But it focuses on his own poetry. Ovid was the wittiest, most inventive, and least deferential of Roman poets, his poetry a scintillating combination of high intellect and mischief.
titles.summaryAI: The Greco-Roman poet Ovid and his literary works within the cultural context of Augustan Rome. Covers poetry on love, heroic women, metamorphosis, Roman festivals, and exile. Examines connections to Roman public life under Emperor Augustus, changing sexual mores, religion, literary debt to Greece, and Roman urban landscape. Addresses Ovid's immense influence on subsequent Western literature and art from the Middle Ages through modernity, including Chaucer, Goethe, and Dali. Highlights his wit, inventiveness, and irreverence as distinctive qualities within Roman literary tradition.
highlights: Explores the core themes running throughout Ovid's poetic works
Sets the poet and his writing in the wider literary and social context
Discusses Ovid's own turbulent personal life, including his fall from grace and subsequent exile
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: Modern Brazil
titles.author: Anthony W. Pereira
titles.subject: Human Geography
titles.abstract: Modern Brazil: A Very Short Introduction provides an account of modern Brazil, covering some of the major features of the country’s transformation. Brazil is alternately praised as a rising power or written off as a country forever failing to reach its potential. This VSI discusses the rise of the modern state in the mid-20th century, and the domestic economic, political, and social challenges faced by the country today. Highlighting some of the peculiar features of Brazil’s development, it also examines how its political leaders engage in informal political deals. Moreover, it looks at the state’s welfare institutions and Brazil’s long history of peaceful relations with neighbours.
titles.summaryAI: Brazil's political, economic, and social transformation from the mid-twentieth century to the present. Covers the rise of the modern Brazilian state, democratic development, and challenges including poverty, racism, and citizenship. Examines informal political deal-making, welfare institutions, and economic development patterns. Addresses Brazil's long history of peaceful relations with neighboring countries and its international positioning. Explores the tension between assessments of Brazil as a rising power versus a country failing to meet its potential. Connects domestic social challenges with foreign policy and regional diplomacy.
highlights: Introduces Brazil as a country, as well as an idea
Considers the economic, political, and foreign policy challenges facing Brazil today
Covers some of the major features of the country's transformation from a poor agricultural outpost to an integral part of the 21st century global order
Includes material on the 2018 elections, in which a polarized and disgruntled electorate voted for a wide-open field of candidates
Part of the Very Short Introductions series - over ten million copies sold worldwide

titles.title: City Planning
titles.author: Carl Abbott
titles.subject: Architecture
titles.abstract: City Planning: A Very Short Introduction gives an international overview of progress in city planning over the last century. City planning explores the tension between the idea of cities as individually held land-parcels and as representations of community and identity. It has inevitable political and ethical dimensions. Over time, cities have grown and merged, leading to larger-scale thinking about planning, but it remains a regional discipline. Part of city planning involves making cities more resilient to natural disasters and civil conflict. Data, technological developments, commerce, and efficient functioning are important, but human connection is necessary for cities to survive.
titles.summaryAI: Urban planning as an international discipline over the past century, examining tensions between private land ownership and community identity in cities. Covers the political and ethical dimensions of planning, metropolitan growth and regional consolidation, and resilience to natural disasters and civil conflict. Addresses how data, technology, and commerce intersect with human connection in urban environments. Examines the evolution from city-scale to regional-scale planning approaches. Bridges architecture, public policy, transportation, infrastructure design, and social considerations in shaping livable urban communities.
highlights: Provides a broad international overview of progress in city planning over the last century
Examines the intersectional nature of urban design and development
Part of the Very Short Introduction series—millions of copies sold worldwide

titles.title: The Maya
titles.author: Matthew Restall, Amara Solari
titles.subject: History/Archaeology
titles.abstract: The Maya: A Very Short Introduction examines the history and evolution of Maya civilization, explaining Maya polities or city-states, artistic expression, and ways of understanding the universe. Study of the Maya has tended to focus on the 2,000 years of history prior to contact with Europeans, and romantic ideas of discovery and disappearance have shaped popular myths about the Maya. However, they neither disappeared at the close of the Classic era nor were completely conquered by Europeans. Independent Maya kingdoms continued until the seventeenth century, and while none exists today, it is still possible to talk about a Maya world and Maya civilization in the twenty-first century.
titles.summaryAI: Maya civilization from its ancient origins through the Classic era, European contact, and into the twenty-first century. Covers Maya city-states, political organization, artistic expression, and cosmological understanding. Challenges popular myths about Maya disappearance, demonstrating continuity through independent kingdoms surviving until the seventeenth century. Examines the persistence of Maya cultural identity in the modern world. Addresses over two thousand years of pre-contact history and the impact of European colonization. Covers archaeological evidence, hieroglyphic writing, architecture, and ritual practices across Mesoamerican geography.
highlights: A rare packaging of all Maya history from origins to the present
Takes a multidisciplinary approach to study of the Maya people, including literature, religion, and archaeology
Expands traditional periodization of Mayan history to explore more historical nuance

titles.title: American Military History
titles.author: Joseph T. Glatthaar
titles.subject: History – U.S./Military
titles.abstract: American Military History: A Very Short Introduction outlines the forces shaping the American military for the past 400 years. Since the colonial period, the United States has struggled to balance standing armed forces with citizen soldiers and sailors. Technological developments and two world wars forced the military to embrace professionalism and its increased obligations. The United States emerged from World War II in a strong position but failed to recognize the limits of its power, a legacy that some might say continues. Recent wars highlight some of the problems of a volunteer-dominated force. To succeed, the American military needs improved communication, understanding, and support.
titles.summaryAI: Four hundred years of American military development from the colonial period through modern conflicts. Covers the tension between standing professional forces and citizen soldiers, the impact of technological developments on military organization, and transformations driven by two world wars. Examines American emergence as a global military power after World War II and failures to recognize the limits of that power. Addresses challenges of volunteer-dominated forces, the need for improved civil-military communication, and the evolving relationship between military professionalism and democratic governance.
highlights: Introduces readers to the role of the American military in the country's past
Explores how the military is shaping the country and current and future national security issues
Examines the difference between civilian soldiers and a standing army, professionalism in the military, the growth of mechanization and technology, and the limits of power

titles.title: Montaigne
titles.author: William M. Hamlin
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Montaigne: A Very Short Introduction provides an overview of Michel de Montaigne’s life, thought, and writing. This VSI situates Montaigne’s influential Essays within his lived experience. Montaigne was a member of the minor aristocracy, a judicial investigator in his youth, and a traveler in later life. He also held the office of mayor and acted as a diplomatic link in war-torn France. Montaigne explored debates and dualities that would inspire future French philosophers, comparing marriage with friendship, solitude with sociability, and the Old World with the New. As a philosopher, he is seen as an early modern skeptic, but his broad-minded approach continues to inspire writers today.
titles.summaryAI: Michel de Montaigne's life, thought, and literary output in sixteenth-century France. Covers the Essays as a genre-defining work situated within his experience as minor aristocrat, judicial investigator, mayor, diplomat, and traveler during France's religious wars. Examines philosophical dualities including marriage versus friendship, solitude versus sociability, and Old World versus New World encounters. Addresses Montaigne's role as an early modern skeptic and his influence on subsequent French philosophical tradition. Bridges Renaissance humanism, moral philosophy, political engagement, and autobiographical literary innovation.
highlights: Serves as a comprehensive overview of Montaigne's life and writings
Illustrates features of Montaigne's prose that are often lost in translation, such as his reliance on image and metaphor, his biting humor and irony, and his colloquial bluntness
Concentrates on topics in the Essays that will be of greatest interest to present-day readers

titles.title: Global Islam
titles.author: Nile Green
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: Global Islam—A Very Short Introduction looks at the methods used by individuals, organizations, and states to spread multiple versions of Islam around the world. Since the late nineteenth century, publications, missions, congresses, and pilgrimages have contributed to the communication and evolution of Islam. At the start of the twentieth century, the infrastructure of European empire allowed for the widespread communication of Islamic beliefs. During a period of secularism in the mid-twentieth century, global Islam became more accessible and, in some cases, more political. How have today’s broadcasting and smartphone technologies changed the face of global Islam? Will communication technologies reconcile the contradictions between variations of the faith, or will they create new ones?
titles.summaryAI: The global spread and diversification of Islam from the late nineteenth century to the present. Covers methods used by individuals, organizations, and states to disseminate multiple versions of Islam worldwide through publications, missions, congresses, and pilgrimages. Examines how European imperial infrastructure facilitated Islamic communication, mid-twentieth-century interactions between Islam and secularism, and the politicization of global Islam.
